title: bayesRVE: Cholesky Calibration Correction for Bayesian Meta-Analysis with Dependent Effect Sizes
text:
The Gap Between Bayesian and Robust Inference
Applied researchers increasingly turn to Bayesian methods for meta-analysis with dependent effect sizes. Bayesian hierarchical models offer natural handling of complex dependence structures, coherent uncertainty propagation through variance components, and direct probability statements about quantities of interest. Yet these advantages rest on the assumption that the working model is correctly specified—an assumption that is rarely defensible when effect sizes are statistically dependent within studies.
Frequentist meta-analysts have long addressed this vulnerability through robust variance estimation (RVE). The CR2 sandwich estimator, combined with Satterthwaite degrees of freedom, provides confidence intervals that maintain nominal coverage regardless of the true within-study correlation structure. However, the resulting inference sacrifices the Bayesian advantages of shrinkage estimation and probabilistic interpretation.
The fundamental tension, then, is between two desirable properties: the shrinkage and probabilistic coherence of Bayesian point estimation, and the misspecification-robust coverage guarantees of sandwich-based inference. This report develops and validates Cholesky Calibration Correction (CCC) as a principled resolution of this tension.

title: bayesRVE: Cholesky Calibration Correction for Bayesian Meta-Analysis with Dependent Effect Sizes
text:
Purpose and Scope
This report provides a self-contained account of the theory, implementation, and empirical validation of the bayesRVE R package (version 0.6.0), which implements CCC for Bayesian meta-analysis with dependent effect sizes. The intended audience includes methodologists working at the intersection of Bayesian computation and robust variance estimation, as well as applied researchers seeking calibrated inference from multi-level meta-analytic models.
The CCC transformation takes the form \[
\boldsymbol{\psi}^{*(s)} = \hat{\boldsymbol{\psi}} + \mathbf{L}_2 \mathbf{L}_1^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\psi}^{(s)} - \hat{\boldsymbol{\psi}}),
\tag{1}\] where \(\mathbf{L}_1 = \text{chol}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}_{\text{MCMC}})\) is the Cholesky factor of the posterior covariance, \(\mathbf{L}_2 = \text{chol}(\mathbf{V}_{\text{CR2}})\) is the Cholesky factor of the CR2 sandwich variance, and \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\psi}}\) is the posterior mean. This affine map preserves the posterior mean as the point estimate while replacing the model-dependent posterior spread with a misspecification-robust alternative.

title: bayesRVE: Cholesky Calibration Correction for Bayesian Meta-Analysis with Dependent Effect Sizes
text:
Three-Layer Calibration
The bayesRVE package implements a three-layer calibration framework:

Layer 1 (Location). The posterior mean \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\beta} \mid \mathbf{y}]\) serves as the point estimate. For both correlated-effects (CE) and correlated-hierarchical-effects (CHE) models, the posterior mean incorporates partial pooling across studies, yielding shrinkage estimates that borrow strength from the ensemble.
Layer 2 (Spread). CCC transforms the posterior draws so that \(\text{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\beta}^*) = \mathbf{V}_{\text{CR2}}\) exactly. The posterior spread is recalibrated from model-dependent to sandwich-robust, while the posterior mean is preserved as an algebraic identity (Section 6.1, Theorem 1).
Layer 3 (Tail behavior). Satterthwaite degrees of freedom \(\text{df}_k\), computed from the CR2 adjustment matrices, determine the reference distribution for each coefficient. The resulting CCC-\(t\) intervals, \[
\hat{\beta}_k \pm t_{\alpha/2,\, \text{df}_k} \cdot \text{SD}(\beta_k^*),
\] are Bayesian in their point estimate, sandwich-robust in their width, and small-sample corrected in their tail behavior.

title: bayesRVE: Cholesky Calibration Correction for Bayesian Meta-Analysis with Dependent Effect Sizes
text:
What This Report Demonstrates
The principal claims, developed across the thirteen chapters that follow, are:

Exact covariance alignment. CCC is an affine transformation whose empirical covariance matrix exactly equals the CR2 sandwich variance matrix (Theorem 2, Section 6.1). This result is verified numerically at machine precision on synthetic data.
Generality across working models. Three-Layer Calibration applies to both the CE model (\(\tau^2\) only) and the CHE model (\(\tau^2 + \omega^2\)). The CHE extension requires only the substitution \(\bar{s}_j^2 \to \bar{s}_j^2 + \hat{\omega}^2\) into the weight matrices; no new mathematical machinery is needed (Chapter 4).
Plug-in parity with established software. When REML variance components are injected into the bayesRVE CR2 machinery, the resulting sandwich variance matches the clubSandwich package to a tolerance of \(10^{-6}\), isolating the linear algebra from MCMC sampling noise (Section 12.1).
Real-data validation. On the Tanner-Smith & Lipsey (2015) dataset (\(J = 107\) studies, \(N = 1{,}198\) effect sizes), the ratio of CCC posterior standard deviation to CR2 standard error is approximately 1.0 across all moderator categories, for both CE and CHE models (Section 14.2).
Comprehensive automated testing. The bayesRVE test suite comprises 2,701+ expectations across 39 test files, covering algebraic identities on synthetic data, MCMC-based integration tests, edge cases, and backward compatibility guarantees (Section 12.1).

For the theoretically inclined reader, Chapters 1 through 5 develop the statistical foundations without reference to the software. For the code reviewer, Chapters 6 through 10 document the package internals with cross-references to specific source files. For the empirically minded reader, Chapters 11 through 13 present the real-data application and its implications.

title: bayesRVE: Cholesky Calibration Correction for Bayesian Meta-Analysis with Dependent Effect Sizes
text:
Scope Boundaries
In scope:

Mathematical foundations: CE and CHE models, CR2 sandwich variance, CCC transformation, Satterthwaite degrees of freedom
Software realization: Stan models, R package architecture, object contracts, S3 method semantics
Empirical validation: Tanner-Smith & Lipsey (2015) dataset, five-estimator comparison
Verification: algebraic identity tests, plug-in parity benchmarks, cross-software interoperability

Out of scope (deferred to future work):

Comprehensive Monte Carlo simulation study (planned for v0.7.0)
Multi-parameter Wald and F tests for joint hypotheses
Multivariate and network meta-analysis extensions
CRAN submission (installation currently requires the development version from GitHub)
Prior sensitivity analysis across alternative variance component priors

title: bayesRVE: Cholesky Calibration Correction for Bayesian Meta-Analysis with Dependent Effect Sizes
text:
Software and Reproducibility

bayesRVE version 0.6.0 (development version, https://github.com/joonho112/bayesRVE)
R version 4.5.1
Stan via the cmdstanr backend (\(\geq\) 0.8.0)
Key dependencies: metafor (\(\geq\) 4.0), clubSandwich (\(\geq\) 0.5.10, in Suggests), posterior (\(\geq\) 1.4.0)

All numerical results can be reproduced by installing bayesRVE and executing the code blocks embedded in each chapter. Reproducibility details are collected in Appendix D.






Tanner-Smith, E. E., & Lipsey, M. W. (2015). Brief alcohol interventions for adolescents and young adults: A systematic review and meta-analysis. Journal of Substance Abuse Treatment, 51, 1–18. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jsat.2014.09.001

title: 2  Notation, Acronyms, and Object Map

2.1 Mathematical Notation
This chapter collects every symbol used in the bayesRVE technical report. Symbols are grouped by topic and listed in order of first appearance. All vectors are column vectors unless transposed; all matrices use bold uppercase.

\(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j = \bar{s}_j^2 \mathbf{I}_{n_j} + \hat{\tau}^2 \mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}'\) (CE); \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j = a_j \mathbf{I}_{n_j} + \hat{\tau}^2 \mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}'\) (CHE)

Weight matrix
\(\mathbf{W}_j = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{-1}\), computed via Woodbury identity

Weight matrix square root
Symmetric positive-definite matrix s.t. \(\mathbf{W}_j^{1/2}\mathbf{W}_j^{1/2} = \mathbf{W}_j\); analytical via rank-1 formula (D24)
\(n_j \times n_j\)

\((\mathbf{W}_j^{1/2})^{-1}\)

Sample covariance of posterior draws: \(\operatorname{Cov}_S[\boldsymbol{\psi}^{(1)}, \ldots, \boldsymbol{\psi}^{(S)}]\); CCC uses only the beta-block \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}_{\mathrm{MCMC}}^{(\beta)}\) (\(K \times K\))
\((K\!+\!J) \times (K\!+\!J)\)
Section 6.1


\(V_{\mathrm{HC0}}\)
Uncorrected sandwich (beta block)
HC0 sandwich estimator for \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\): \(\mathbf{H}^{-1}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{H}^{-1}\) restricted to rows/cols \(1{:}K\)
\(K \times K\)

CR2 bias-reduced sandwich
\(V_{\mathrm{CR2}} = \mathbf{Q}\bigl(\sum_{j=1}^{J} \tilde{\mathbf{s}}_j \tilde{\mathbf{s}}_j'\bigr)\mathbf{Q}\) (Bell & McCaffrey, 2002; Tipton, 2015)
\(K \times K\)

Sandwich variance (generic)

Full marginal sandwich
Sandwich for \(\boldsymbol{\phi} = (\boldsymbol{\beta}', \tau^2)'\) (CE) or \((\boldsymbol{\beta}', \tau^2, \omega^2)'\) (CHE)
\((K\!+\!1) \times (K\!+\!1)\) or \((K\!+\!2) \times (K\!+\!2)\)

Conditional sandwich for \(\theta_j\)

\(K \times 1\)

Omega-squared score (CHE)
Score for \(\omega^2\) in the CHE marginal likelihood
scalar
Section 4.3


\(\mathbf{S}\)
Score matrix
\(\mathbf{S} = [\mathbf{s}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{s}_J]'\) where \(\mathbf{s}_j = (\mathbf{s}_j^{(\beta)\prime}, s_j^{(\tau^2)}, \ldots)'\)
\(J \times d\)
Chapter 6


\(\mathbf{M}\)
Meat matrix
\(\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{S}'\mathbf{S} = \sum_{j=1}^{J} \mathbf{s}_j \mathbf{s}_j'\) (likelihood scores only; prior excluded)
\(d \times d\)
Chapter 6


\(\mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{lik}}\)
Likelihood Hessian
\(-\partial^2 \log p(\mathbf{y} \mid \boldsymbol{\phi}) / \partial \boldsymbol{\phi}\,\partial \boldsymbol{\phi}'\) (positive-definite convention)
\(d \times d\)
Chapter 6


\(\mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{prior}}\)
Stabilized prior Hessian
\(\mathrm{diag}(1/\sigma_\beta^2, \ldots, 1/\sigma_\beta^2, \,h_{\tau^2})\); the \(\tau^2\) entry \(h_{\tau^2}\) is a numerical ridge stabilizer (capped at \(10^6\) when \(\hat{\tau}^2 \approx 0\)), not the exact prior second derivative
\(d \times d\)
Chapter 6


\(\mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{post}}\)
Posterior Hessian (bread)
\(\mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{post}} = \mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{lik}} + \mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{prior}}\)
\(d \times d\)

\(\mathbf{Q}\)
WLS bread
\(\mathbf{Q} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{W}\mathbf{X})^{-1} = \bigl(\sum_j \mathbf{X}_j'\mathbf{W}_j\mathbf{X}_j\bigr)^{-1}\) (fixed-effect WLS, D15)
\(K \times K\)
Section 6.5


\(H_j\)
Conditional Hessian for \(\theta_j\)
\(H_j = n_j / \bar{s}_j^2 + 1/\hat{\tau}^2\) (CE)

Chapter 6


\(u_j\)
Conditional score for \(\theta_j\)
\(u_j = (n_j / \bar{s}_j^2)\,r_{\mathrm{cond},j}\) where \(r_{\mathrm{cond},j} = \bar{y}_j - \bar{\mathbf{x}}_j'\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} - \hat{\theta}_j\)

Marginal parameter dimension
\(d = K + 1\) (CE) or \(d = K + 2\) (CHE)

\(\frac{1}{a_j}\mathbf{I}_{n_j} - \frac{\hat{\tau}^2}{a_j\, c_j}\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}'\) where \(a_j = \bar{s}_j^2 + \hat{\omega}^2\), \(c_j = a_j + n_j \hat{\tau}^2\)
\(n_j \times n_j\)

2.3 S3 Object Contract Map
Complete S3 class contracts for all three pipeline objects are documented in Section 8.3. This section lists only the always-required field names enforced by the .assert_* validators in validate.R.
bm_fit (validated by .assert_bm_fit()): fit, stan_data, bm_data, formula, prior, param, K, J, model_name, call, elapsed. The model field is set by all current constructors but is not in the validator’s required list.
bm_sandwich (validated by .assert_bm_sandwich()): V_sand_marg, V_sand_cond, H_post, M, Sigma_MCMC, scores, rho, param_names, fingerprint. Additional CR2 and Satterthwaite fields are validated conditionally when present.
bm_ccc (validated by .assert_bm_ccc()): draws_star, draws_orig, A_beta, r_theta, frobenius_norm, r_k, center_type, sandwich, K, J.
summary.bm_ccc (created by summary.bm_ccc() in methods.R): beta, theta, diagnostics, der_decomposition, method, small_sample, c_beta, df_source, ci_method, level, center_type, K, J, S, model.

title: 3  Introduction and Inferential Architecture
text: 4 Introduction and Inferential Architecture

title: 3  Introduction and Inferential Architecture
text:
4.1 The Inferential Mismatch
Consider a meta-analysis of \(J = 20\) studies, each contributing between 2 and 15 effect sizes, for a total of \(N = 120\) observations. A Bayesian analyst fits a correlated-effects (CE) working model with a shared between-study variance \(\tau^2\) and a working correlation \(\rho_{\text{work}} = 0.8\). The posterior yields \(\hat{\beta}_1 = 0.35\) for a moderator coefficient, with a posterior standard deviation of \(\text{SD}_{\text{post}} = 0.062\), producing a 95% equi-tailed credible interval of \((0.23, 0.47)\).
A frequentist colleague analyzes the same data with robust variance estimation (RVE). Using the CR2 sandwich estimator, she obtains \(\hat{\beta}_1 = 0.34\)—close to the posterior mean, as expected with a weakly informative prior—but a standard error of \(\text{SE}_{\text{CR2}} = 0.091\). With Satterthwaite degrees of freedom \(\text{df} = 11.3\), her 95% confidence interval is \((0.14, 0.54)\), substantially wider than the Bayesian interval.
The discrepancy is not a coding error. The Bayesian interval is narrower because the posterior variance encodes the assumed correlation structure: with \(\rho_{\text{work}} = 0.8\), effect sizes within a study are treated as highly correlated, and the effective sample size is relatively large. But if the true within-study correlation is \(\rho_{\text{true}} = 0.4\), the working model borrows too much information across correlated outcomes within studies, and the posterior is over-concentrated. The sandwich variance, by contrast, does not trust the working model’s correlation structure; it estimates the sampling variability of \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) from the empirical cross-study variation of the score contributions, yielding a standard error that is consistent regardless of \(\rho_{\text{true}}\).
This inferential mismatch—the gap between the model-dependent posterior variance and the misspecification-robust sandwich variance—is the central problem that motivates this report and the bayesRVE package.
Three features of dependent-effect meta-analysis make the mismatch particularly consequential:

Working model misspecification is the norm, not the exception. Within-study correlations among effect sizes are almost never known and are typically set to a single working value. Whether the analyst chooses \(\rho = 0.6\), \(0.8\), or estimates \(\rho\) from the data, the assumed correlation structure is, at best, an approximation. The posterior variance inherits this approximation; the sandwich variance does not.
Small \(J\) amplifies the discrepancy. With many studies (\(J > 50\)), the Bayesian and frequentist estimators tend to agree, because the posterior concentrates around the true parameter and its variance converges to the sandwich variance under regularity conditions. With \(J \leq 20\)—a common regime in education, psychology, and clinical research—the posterior can be substantially miscalibrated relative to the sandwich variance.
Unbalanced study sizes create heterogeneous leverage. When some studies contribute 2 effect sizes and others contribute 30, the studies with large \(n_j\) exert disproportionate influence on the posterior. The sandwich estimator accounts for this through the leverage structure of the design matrix; the posterior variance does not.

The mismatch is not a deficiency of Bayesian inference per se. It is a structural consequence of the fact that the posterior variance measures uncertainty conditional on the model being correct, while the sandwich variance measures uncertainty regardless of whether the model is correct. When the model is correctly specified, the two should agree—and CCC reduces to the identity (Theorem 3, Section 6.1). When it is not, the posterior can be arbitrarily miscalibrated, and the analyst who reports posterior credible intervals without calibration is implicitly asserting a level of model confidence that the data do not support.
To make this concrete, define the per-coefficient mismatch ratio \[
M_k = \frac{\text{SD}_{\text{post},k}}{\text{SE}_{\text{CR2},k}},
\tag{4.1}\] where \(\text{SD}_{\text{post},k}\) is the posterior standard deviation and \(\text{SE}_{\text{CR2},k}\) is the CR2 sandwich standard error for coefficient \(\beta_k\). When \(M_k < 1\), the posterior is too narrow and credible interval undercoverage is expected. When \(M_k > 1\), the posterior is too wide. When \(M_k = 1\), the model is well-calibrated for that coefficient and no correction is needed. In the example above, \(M_1 = 0.062 / 0.091 = 0.68\), indicating substantial under-dispersion: the posterior width is only 68% of the sandwich-based width. Without calibration, the 95% credible interval would undercover by a wide margin.
After CCC calibration, the software reports a related but distinct quantity: the calibration ratio \(R_k = \text{SD}(\psi_k^*) / \text{SD}(\psi_k)\), which measures the factor by which CCC adjusted each parameter’s posterior spread. When \(M_k < 1\) (posterior too narrow), CCC widens the draws and \(R_k > 1\); the two ratios are approximately reciprocals.
The magnitude of \(M_k\) depends on the interaction of three factors: the degree of within-study correlation misspecification, the number of studies \(J\), and the distribution of study sizes \(n_j\). The simulation study in Section 12.1 explores these interactions systematically.

title: 3  Introduction and Inferential Architecture
text:
4.2 Why Dependent-Effect Meta-Analysis Needs Calibration
The statistical challenge of dependent effect sizes arises because a single study often contributes more than one effect size estimate. A randomized trial of a brief alcohol intervention might report outcomes on alcohol consumption, alcohol-related problems, and peak blood alcohol concentration, each measured at 3-month and 6-month follow-up, producing six effect sizes from a single study. These estimates share the same participants, the same intervention, and the same study-level characteristics; they are correlated. The within-study correlation matrix is almost never reported, and even when it can be estimated, it is subject to substantial uncertainty.
The frequentist response to this challenge, developed by Hedges et al. (2010), is RVE: specify a working model for the marginal mean of the effect sizes, compute point estimates via weighted least squares, and then correct the standard errors using a sandwich (Huber-White) variance estimator that is consistent under arbitrary within-study dependence. The key insight is that one need not know the within-study correlations to obtain valid inference about the regression coefficients—the sandwich estimator absorbs the unknown dependence into the cross-study variation of the score contributions. With the CR2 small-sample correction (Bell & McCaffrey, 2002; Tipton, 2015) and Satterthwaite degrees of freedom, this approach provides confidence intervals with accurate coverage even when \(J\) is modest.
Bayesian meta-analysts have no analogous robustification. The standard approach is to specify a hierarchical model—either a CE model with a single \(\tau^2\) and a working correlation, or a correlated-hierarchical-effects (CHE) model with both between-study heterogeneity \(\tau^2\) and within-study heterogeneity \(\omega^2\)—fit the model via MCMC, and report posterior summaries. The posterior variance of the regression coefficients depends on the assumed model, including the parameterization of the within-study dependence. When this parameterization is wrong, the credible intervals may have coverage far from nominal.
This gap is not merely academic. The increasing popularity of Bayesian meta-analytic software means that applied researchers are routinely reporting posterior credible intervals for regression coefficients in dependent-effect meta-analyses without any check on whether those intervals have adequate frequentist coverage. The methodological literature has, to date, offered no general-purpose correction.
The need for calibration connects to a broader intellectual tradition. Little (2006, 2012) articulated the philosophy of Calibrated Bayes: Bayesian models should be evaluated and, where necessary, adjusted to ensure that their posterior inferences have acceptable frequentist repeated-sampling properties. This is not a rejection of Bayesian inference but an extension of it. A Bayesian analyst who values coherent probability statements should also value calibration—the property that a 95% credible interval, averaged over the data-generating process, contains the true parameter approximately 95% of the time.
Rubin (1984) anticipated a similar idea in arguing that Bayesian inference should be assessed by its frequentist operating characteristics. The two traditions—Bayesian posterior inference and frequentist robust variance estimation—have developed largely in parallel, each with genuine strengths that the other lacks. Bayesian methods give a full posterior distribution with shrinkage and probabilistic interpretation; RVE gives coverage guarantees under misspecification.
The Three-Layer Calibration framework developed in this report operationalizes the Calibrated Bayes vision for dependent-effect meta-analysis: retain the Bayesian point estimate (Layer 1), replace the model-dependent spread with a sandwich-robust alternative (Layer 2), and correct the tail behavior for small samples (Layer 3). CCC is the mechanism that makes Layer 2 concrete.

title: 3  Introduction and Inferential Architecture
text:
4.3 Three-Layer Architecture
The bayesRVE inference pipeline consists of three calibration layers, applied sequentially. Each layer addresses a distinct aspect of the inferential mismatch described in Section 4.1.

4.3.1 Layer 1: Location via Posterior Mean
The posterior mean \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\beta} \mid \mathbf{y}]\) serves as the point estimate. This estimate incorporates partial pooling across studies: extreme study-level estimates are shrunk toward the grand mean, with the degree of shrinkage determined by the ratio of within-study to between-study variance. Partial pooling is a well-established advantage of hierarchical models, and there is no reason to abandon it when calibrating the uncertainty.
Importantly, CCC preserves the posterior mean exactly (Theorem 1, Section 6.1). The point estimate after calibration is identical to the point estimate before calibration. Layer 1 is therefore costless within the calibration framework—the analyst obtains the benefits of Bayesian shrinkage without any sacrifice in terms of the subsequent calibration.


4.3.2 Layer 2: Spread via CCC
The Cholesky Calibration Correction is an affine transformation applied to the MCMC posterior draws: \[
\boldsymbol{\psi}^{*(s)} = \hat{\boldsymbol{\psi}} + \mathbf{L}_2 \mathbf{L}_1^{-1}\bigl(\boldsymbol{\psi}^{(s)} - \hat{\boldsymbol{\psi}}\bigr), \quad s = 1, \ldots, S,
\tag{4.2}\] where \(\mathbf{L}_1 = \text{chol}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}_{\text{MCMC}})\) and \(\mathbf{L}_2 = \text{chol}(\mathbf{V}_{\text{CR2}})\) are the lower-triangular Cholesky factors of the posterior covariance and the CR2 sandwich variance, respectively.
CCC replaces the model-dependent posterior dispersion with the sandwich-robust alternative. Within the beta-block, the covariance of the calibrated draws equals the CR2 sandwich variance exactly: \(\operatorname{Cov}_S(\boldsymbol{\beta}^*) = \mathbf{V}_{\text{CR2}}^{(\beta)}\). This is a finite-sample algebraic identity, not an asymptotic approximation (Theorem 2, applied to the beta-block; see Section 6.3 for the full blockwise structure). When the model is correctly specified so that \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}_{\text{MCMC}}^{(\beta)} = \mathbf{V}_{\text{CR2}}^{(\beta)}\), CCC reduces to the identity transformation and changes nothing (Theorem 3, Section 6.1). There is no penalty for applying CCC when it is not needed.
In the scalar case (\(K = 1\)), CCC reduces to a simple rescaling of the centered draws by the factor \(1/M_k\) from Equation 4.1. When \(M_k < 1\) (posterior too narrow), the factor is greater than 1 and CCC widens the interval. When \(M_k > 1\) (posterior too wide), CCC narrows it. The multivariate case requires the full Cholesky machinery to handle correlations between coefficients, but the intuition is the same: CCC rescales and rotates the posterior draws to match the sandwich covariance structure.


4.3.3 Layer 3: Tail Correction via Satterthwaite Degrees of Freedom
Even with the correct standard error, the reference distribution matters. With \(J = 15\) studies and \(K = 3\) predictors, using a normal reference distribution (\(z\)-critical values) for the confidence interval can lead to undercoverage, because the sandwich variance estimator has substantial sampling variability. Satterthwaite degrees of freedom approximate the effective sample size for each coefficient, yielding a \(t\)-distribution reference: \[
\hat{\beta}_k \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,\text{df}_k} \cdot \text{SD}(\beta_k^*).
\tag{4.3}\]
The degrees of freedom are computed from the CR2 adjustment matrices (see Chapter 6 for details) and can differ across coefficients. In the CE model, the intercept may have \(\text{df} = 18\) while a moderator with missing values in some studies might have \(\text{df} = 7\). Layer 3 ensures that the confidence interval accounts for this heterogeneity in information content.
We refer to the resulting intervals as CCC-\(t\) intervals. The simulation evidence in Section 12.1 demonstrates that CCC-\(t\) intervals achieve coverage within one percentage point of nominal (e.g., 94.5–95.3% at the 95% level), matching the coverage of frequentist CR2 confidence intervals based on REML estimation. By contrast, CCC-\(z\) intervals (which use normal quantiles instead of \(t\)-quantiles) undercover at 91–94% in the same conditions, confirming that Layer 3 is essential, not merely a theoretical refinement.






NoteArchitecture Diagram: Three-Layer Calibration Pipeline



The complete pipeline proceeds in four function calls, with each layer contributing a distinct ingredient to the final inference:
                         bayesRVE Pipeline
 ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
 bm_fit()    →   MCMC posterior draws {ψ^(s)}, s = 1,...,S
                    │
                  Layer 1: Posterior mean ψ̂           [LOCATION]
                    │            (shrinkage, partial pooling)
                    │
 sandwich()  →   V_CR2, df_k  (from clustered score contributions)
                    │
 ccc()       →   Layer 2: Affine transform            [SPREAD]
                    │        ψ*(s) = ψ̂ + L₂ L₁⁻¹(ψ(s) − ψ̂)
                    │            (Cov_S(β*) = V_CR2^(β) exactly)
                    │
 summary()   →   Layer 3: t(df_k) reference           [TAILS]
                             β̂_k ± t_{α/2, df_k} · SD(β*_k)
                                 (small-sample correction)
 ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

 Input:  y_i, v_i, X_i, study labels, working ρ
 Output: CCC-t intervals
         (Bayesian location, sandwich width, Satterthwaite tails)
Why the order matters. Layer 1 must precede Layer 2 because CCC is centered at the posterior mean; changing the point estimate after CCC would violate the center-preservation property (Theorem 1). Layer 2 must precede Layer 3 because the Satterthwaite degrees of freedom are properties of the sandwich variance estimator, not of the posterior; they are meaningful only after the spread has been calibrated to the sandwich target. Reversing any pair of layers produces either mathematically incoherent or operationally suboptimal inference.




4.3.4 The Sequential Logic
The three layers address three distinct sources of the inferential mismatch:



Table 4.1: Three-Layer Calibration: what each layer contributes.











Layer
Addresses
Mechanism
Guarantee




1 (Location)
Estimation efficiency
Bayesian shrinkage / partial pooling
\(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) preserved exactly (Thm 1)


2 (Spread)
Variance miscalibration
CCC affine transformation
\(\operatorname{Cov}_S(\boldsymbol{\beta}^*) = \mathbf{V}_{\text{CR2}}^{(\beta)}\) exactly (Thm 2, beta-block)


3 (Tails)
Small-sample reference distribution
Satterthwaite \(\text{df}_k\)
Coverage correction for finite \(J\)






No single layer suffices. Without Layer 2, the posterior spread is model-dependent and potentially miscalibrated. Without Layer 3, the intervals undercover when \(J\) is small. Without Layer 1, one loses the shrinkage benefits of Bayesian estimation entirely. The three layers are not merely conceptual divisions; they correspond to separable computations with independent failure modes and independent test suites. A failure in one layer—say, a misspecified Satterthwaite approximation at very small \(J\)—does not compromise the other layers. Improvements to any single layer (e.g., replacing Satterthwaite with a bootstrap-based degrees of freedom) can be incorporated without modifying the remaining layers.

title: 3  Introduction and Inferential Architecture
text:
4.4 Claims, Guarantees, and Exclusions
Scientific software should be explicit about what it guarantees, what it provides as a diagnostic, and what remains the subject of ongoing empirical investigation. Table 4.2 organizes the claims made by bayesRVE into three categories.



Table 4.2: What bayesRVE guarantees (exact theorems), provides diagnostically (ratios), and supports with simulation evidence (coverage).











Claim
Category
Evidence
Reference




CCC preserves posterior mean
Exact theorem
Machine-precision verification
Section 6.1, Thm 1


\(\operatorname{Cov}_S(\boldsymbol{\beta}^*) = \mathbf{V}_{\text{CR2}}^{(\beta)}\) (beta-block)
Exact theorem
Machine-precision verification
Section 6.1, Thm 2


CCC \(= \mathbf{I}\) under correct specification
Exact theorem
Machine-precision verification
Section 6.1, Thm 3


CR2 variance matches clubSandwich (plug-in)
Numerical parity
\(< 10^{-6}\) tolerance
Section 12.1


Satterthwaite df matches clubSandwich (plug-in)
Numerical parity
\(< 10^{-6}\) tolerance
Section 12.1


\(R_k\) quantifies per-coefficient miscalibration
Diagnostic
Always available
Section 6.1


\(\lVert\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{I}\rVert_F\) measures CCC magnitude
Diagnostic
Always available; no universal threshold
Section 6.1


CCC-\(t\) coverage \(\approx\) 95%
Simulation evidence
94.5–95.3% across 24 conditions
Section 12.1


CCC SD / CR2 SE \(\approx 1.0\) on TSL15 data
Empirical
Confirmed (\(J = 107\), \(N = 1{,}198\))
Section 14.2












NoteExact vs. Approximate Guarantees



Theorems 1–3 are stated generically in Section 6.1. In the bayesRVE pipeline, CCC is applied blockwise (Section 6.3), so the operational guarantees are:

Exact: posterior-mean preservation (Thm 1). \(\bar{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^* = \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) to machine precision. Holds independently per block.
Exact: beta-block sample covariance matching (Thm 2). \(\operatorname{Cov}_S(\boldsymbol{\beta}^*) = \mathbf{V}_{\text{CR2}}^{(\beta)}\), including all off-diagonal elements. Finite-sample algebraic identity.
Exact: scalar theta variance matching (CE only). Each \(\operatorname{Var}_S(\theta_j^*) = V_{\text{sand,cond}}^{(\theta_j)}\) via diagonal rescaling. Cross-covariances among \(\theta_j\) and the \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\)–\(\boldsymbol{\theta}\) cross-block are not targeted.
Approximate: frequentist coverage. CCC-\(t\) intervals achieve nominal coverage only asymptotically. For finite \(J\), Satterthwaite df and the normal posterior approximation introduce error. Simulation evidence: 94.5–95.3% at the 95% level across 24 conditions.

For the CHE model, \(\boldsymbol{\theta}\) is analytically marginalized; CCC operates on the beta-block only. See Section 8.2 for the full exact-versus-asymptotic analysis.


The distinction between these categories matters for interpretation. The theorems hold as finite-sample algebraic identities and are verified at machine precision on synthetic data; they carry no distributional assumptions and require only positive definiteness of the input matrices. The plug-in parity results isolate the bayesRVE linear algebra from MCMC sampling noise by injecting known (REML) variance components; they verify that the code implements the intended formulas. The simulation-based coverage results are subject to Monte Carlo error and to the specific conditions studied (24 scenarios varying \(J\), \(\rho_{\text{true}}\), model specification); they provide evidence of adequate performance across a range of realistic settings but do not constitute a universal guarantee.

4.4.1 Operating Envelope
The theoretical properties of CCC (Theorems 1–3) hold for any positive-definite inputs, but the practical reliability of the sandwich variance estimator—and hence of the calibrated intervals—depends on having enough independent clusters (studies). bayesRVE enforces the constraint \[
J_{\min} = \max(8,\; 2K + 2),
\tag{4.4}\] where \(J\) is the number of studies and \(K\) is the number of regression coefficients. Below this threshold, the CR2 sandwich variance may not be positive definite, the Satterthwaite degrees of freedom may be excessively small or negative, and the resulting intervals are unreliable. The package emits a warning when \(J\) is within 20% of the boundary and an error when \(J < J_{\min}\). The rationale and justification of this envelope are given in Chapter 8.
The lower bound of 8 studies is a practical floor: with fewer than 8 clusters, the sandwich estimator’s finite-sample bias is too large for the CR2 correction to overcome reliably. The \(2K + 2\) term ensures that the degrees-of-freedom numerator remains positive for all \(K\) coefficients. Together, these constraints define the regime in which Three-Layer Calibration can be expected to perform well.


4.4.2 Explicit Exclusions
The following capabilities are outside the scope of bayesRVE version 0.6.0:

Multi-parameter hypothesis tests. Wald and \(F\)-tests for joint hypotheses (e.g., testing that a set of moderator coefficients are jointly zero) require the full sandwich covariance matrix and an appropriate \(F\)-distribution approximation. This is the primary target for version 0.7.0.
Prior sensitivity analysis. The current implementation uses default weakly informative priors for \(\tau^2\) (and \(\omega^2\) in the CHE model). Systematic investigation of how the prior on variance components affects the CCC-calibrated intervals is deferred to future work.
Multivariate meta-analysis. bayesRVE handles dependent effect sizes through the sandwich variance approach, which treats the within-study dependence as a nuisance. True multivariate meta-analysis, in which the correlation structure is itself a quantity of interest, requires a different modeling framework.
Network meta-analysis. Extensions to indirect comparisons and network structures are not currently supported.
Nonlinear meta-regression. The CCC transformation is defined for the coefficient vector \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) of a linear meta-regression. Extensions to nonlinear link functions would require additional development of both the sandwich estimator and its small-sample corrections.
Non-exchangeable within-study structures. The current implementation assumes exchangeable working correlation within studies; autoregressive or unstructured working correlations are not supported.

title: 3  Introduction and Inferential Architecture
text:
4.5 Connections to the Literature
The Three-Layer Calibration framework draws on, and contributes to, several distinct research traditions. This section situates bayesRVE within its intellectual context and clarifies how it relates to existing work.

4.5.1 Calibrated Bayes
Little (2006, 2012) proposed Calibrated Bayes as a philosophy of inference in which Bayesian models are evaluated by their frequentist operating characteristics and adjusted when calibration fails. The motivating context was survey sampling, where complex designs induce dependencies that are difficult to model correctly. Little argued that a good Bayesian model should produce posterior intervals with approximately nominal frequentist coverage, and that failure to achieve this constitutes evidence of model misspecification requiring correction.
bayesRVE is, to our knowledge, the first concrete implementation of Calibrated Bayes for meta-analysis with dependent effect sizes. The CCC transformation mechanizes Little’s prescription: the posterior is evaluated against a frequentist target (the CR2 sandwich variance), and when the two disagree, the posterior spread is adjusted to match. The posterior mean is retained because Bayesian point estimates are generally well-calibrated even under mild misspecification; it is the variance—and hence the interval width—that requires correction.


4.5.2 Bayesian Sandwich-Adjusted Inference
Williams & Savitsky (2021) and León-Novelo & Savitsky (2019) developed methods for adjusting Bayesian posterior inference using sandwich variance estimators in the context of survey data. Their approach shares the fundamental insight that model-based posterior variances can be replaced with design-consistent (sandwich) alternatives without abandoning Bayesian point estimates.
The technical implementation differs in two important respects. First, the survey-sampling methods operate on the asymptotic posterior distribution by replacing the Fisher information matrix with the sandwich matrix, while CCC operates directly on the MCMC draws via an affine transformation. This direct-draw approach allows CCC to handle finite-sample effects through the CR2 correction without additional asymptotic arguments. Second, the survey methods do not incorporate a small-sample correction for the reference distribution; CCC’s Layer 3 (Satterthwaite degrees of freedom) addresses this gap, which is particularly important when the number of clusters \(J\) is small.


4.5.3 Sensitivity to Model Assumptions: The Infinitesimal Jackknife
Giordano & Broderick (2024) developed the infinitesimal jackknife (IJ) for Bayesian models, providing a fast approximation to the sensitivity of posterior expectations with respect to data perturbations. Like CCC, the IJ uses a sandwich-type covariance to assess the robustness of posterior inference. The key distinction is that the IJ is diagnostic—it measures how much posterior summaries change under infinitesimal reweighting of observations—while CCC is corrective—it modifies the posterior to achieve a specific frequentist target.
In principle, the IJ could be used to determine whether calibration is needed, while CCC would implement the correction. bayesRVE currently applies CCC in an always-on mode, which is justified by Theorem 3: when the model is correctly specified and correction is not needed, CCC reduces to the identity and changes nothing. The two approaches are complementary, and a formal integration of IJ diagnostics with CCC correction represents a productive direction for future work.


4.5.4 CR2 and Satterthwaite Corrections
The CR2 bias correction for cluster-robust variance estimators was developed by Bell & McCaffrey (2002) and extended to the meta-analytic setting by Tipton (2015) and Tipton & Pustejovsky (2015). The correction involves multiplying the residual for each study by an adjustment matrix that accounts for the leverage of that study’s design rows, ensuring that the expected value of the sandwich estimator equals the true covariance under the working model. The Satterthwaite approximation then matches the first two moments of the resulting test statistic to a \(t\)-distribution, yielding degrees of freedom that reflect the effective number of independent pieces of information available for each coefficient.
bayesRVE implements the CR2 correction and Satterthwaite approximation independently, without runtime dependence on the clubSandwich package. This design choice serves two purposes: first, the entire inference pipeline is self-contained and can be audited without external dependencies; second, plug-in parity tests—injecting REML variance components into bayesRVE and verifying agreement with clubSandwich at \(10^{-6}\) tolerance—cleanly isolate the linear algebra from MCMC sampling noise, providing a definitive verification path (see Section 12.1).


4.5.5 How CCC Differs
CCC is distinguished from the approaches reviewed above in three respects. First, it operates on MCMC draws rather than on asymptotic distributions, making it compatible with complex hierarchical models that lack closed-form posteriors. Second, it combines the sandwich adjustment (Layer 2) with small-sample corrections (Layer 3) in a unified framework, rather than treating them as separate problems. Third, it is implemented as a general-purpose R package that handles both CE and CHE working models, with the CHE extension requiring only the substitution \(\bar{s}_j^2 \to \bar{s}_j^2 + \hat{\omega}^2\) in the weight matrices (Chapter 4)—no new mathematical machinery is needed. This algebraic simplicity is a consequence of the fact that \(\omega^2\) enters the marginal variance of the CHE model additively, so the entire CR2 infrastructure can be reused with a single parameter substitution.

title: 3  Introduction and Inferential Architecture
text:
4.6 Roadmap
The remainder of this report is organized in three parts.
Part I: Foundations (Chapters 2–5) develops the statistical theory. Chapter 4 defines the CE and CHE working models and their Stan implementations. Chapter 6 derives the CR2 sandwich variance estimator and Satterthwaite degrees of freedom. Section 6.1 presents the CCC transformation, its algebraic properties (Theorems 1–3), and the blockwise structure required for the CHE model. Chapter 8 establishes the operating envelope, the \(J_{\min}\) constraint, and the fallback logic for edge cases.
Part II: Software (Chapters 6–10) documents the bayesRVE package internals. Each chapter covers one major component: the package architecture and object contracts (Section 8.1), the Stan model compilation and fitting interface (Chapter 9), the sandwich variance computation pipeline (Chapter 11), the CCC transformation engine and S3 methods (Section 11.1), and the multi-tier verification strategy (Section 12.1). Cross-references to specific source files and test cases are provided throughout.
Part III: Validation (Chapters 11–13) presents the empirical evidence. Section 13.1 describes the Tanner-Smith & Lipsey (2015) dataset (\(J = 107\) studies, \(N = 1{,}198\) effect sizes) and the five-estimator comparison design. Section 14.2 presents the main findings, including the CCC/CR2 SE ratio analysis across six categories of dependent variables for both CE and CHE models. Chapter 16 examines the mechanics of CCC calibration—how the transformation matrix \(\mathbf{A}\), the shrinkage ratio \(R_k\), and the Satterthwaite degrees of freedom vary across conditions and what they reveal about the interplay between Bayesian and robust inference.
The parts are designed to be largely self-contained. A reader interested only in the statistical theory can read Part I without reference to the software. A code reviewer can focus on Part II with occasional forward references to the verification strategy. An empirically oriented reader can proceed directly from this introduction to Part III.






Bell, R. M., & McCaffrey, D. F. (2002). Bias reduction in standard errors for linear regression with multi-stage samples. Survey Methodology, 28(2), 169–181.


Giordano, R., & Broderick, T. (2024). The Bayesian infinitesimal jackknife for variance. https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.06466


Hedges, L. V., Tipton, E., & Johnson, M. C. (2010). Robust variance estimation in meta-regression with dependent effect size estimates. Research Synthesis Methods, 1(1), 39–65. https://doi.org/10.1002/jrsm.5


León-Novelo, L. G., & Savitsky, T. D. (2019). Fully Bayesian estimation under informative sampling with unattenuated dependence. Electronic Journal of Statistics, 13(1), 1608–1645. https://doi.org/10.1214/19-EJS1538


Little, R. J. (2006). Calibrated Bayes: A Bayes/Frequentist roadmap. The American Statistician, 60(3), 213–223. https://doi.org/10.1198/000313006X117837


Little, R. J. (2012). Calibrated Bayes, an alternative inferential paradigm for official statistics. Journal of Official Statistics, 28(3), 309–334.


Rubin, D. B. (1984). Bayesianly justifiable and relevant frequency calculations for the applied statistician. The Annals of Statistics, 12(4), 1151–1172. https://doi.org/10.1214/aos/1176346785


Tanner-Smith, E. E., & Lipsey, M. W. (2015). Brief alcohol interventions for adolescents and young adults: A systematic review and meta-analysis. Journal of Substance Abuse Treatment, 51, 1–18. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jsat.2014.09.001


Tipton, E. (2015). Small sample adjustments for robust variance estimation with meta-regression. Psychological Methods, 20(3), 375–393. https://doi.org/10.1037/met0000011


Tipton, E., & Pustejovsky, J. E. (2015). Small-sample adjustments for tests of moderators and model fit using robust variance estimation in meta-regression. Journal of Educational and Behavioral Statistics, 40(6), 604–634. https://doi.org/10.3102/1076998615606099

title: 4  Statistical Models and Stan Implementation
text:
4.1 Data Structure
This chapter presents the two core models in bayesRVE—the Correlated-Effects (CE) model and the Correlated-and-Hierarchical Effects (CHE) model—using a parallel code-theory format. Every mathematical definition is paired with its Stan implementation and R extraction code, so that readers can trace the path from equation to software at each step.

title: 4  Statistical Models and Stan Implementation
text:
4.4 Prior Specification

4.4.1 Mathematical Specification
bayesRVE places the following priors on model parameters:
\[
\beta_k \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_\beta, \sigma_\beta^2), \qquad k = 1, \ldots, K,
\tag{4.15}\]
\[
\tau \sim \text{Half-}t(\nu_\tau, \ell_\tau, s_\tau),
\tag{4.16}\]
\[
\omega \sim \text{Half-}t(\nu_\omega, \ell_\omega, s_\omega) \qquad \text{(CHE only)}.
\tag{4.17}\]
The Half-\(t\) family includes two important special cases: the Half-Cauchy (\(\nu = 1\)) and Half-Normal (\(\nu \to \infty\)). A common shared prior is applied to all \(K\) coefficients in \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\); users who need coefficient-specific priors can reparameterize their design matrix so that a common scale is appropriate.


4.4.2 Stan Prior Block
In the Stan model block, priors are specified using the prior-via-data-block pattern—hyperparameters are passed as data, not hard-coded:
// CE model
beta ~ normal(prior_beta_mean, prior_beta_sd);
tau ~ student_t(prior_tau_df, prior_tau_location, prior_tau_scale);

// CHE model adds:
omega ~ student_t(prior_omega_df, prior_omega_location, prior_omega_scale);
Math-Code Mapping. The <lower=0> constraint on tau (and omega) automatically truncates the Student-\(t\) density to the positive half-line, yielding the Half-\(t\) distribution without explicit truncation syntax. Stan adjusts the log-density by a factor of \(\log 2\) to account for the folding.


4.4.3 R Prior API
# Distribution constructors
normal(mean = 0, sd = 1)                       # bm_dist_normal
half_t(df = 3, location = 0, scale = 0.5)      # bm_dist_half_t
half_cauchy(location = 0, scale = 1)            # df = 1 convenience wrapper
half_normal(location = 0, scale = 1)            # df = Inf convenience wrapper

# Bundle into a prior object
my_prior <- bm_prior(
  beta  = normal(0, 1),
  tau   = half_t(3, 0, 0.5),
  omega = half_t(3, 0, 0.5)    # CHE only; NULL for CE
)

# Scale-appropriate defaults
default_prior("smd")       # beta ~ N(0,1),   tau ~ Half-t(3,0,0.5)
default_prior("log_or")    # beta ~ N(0,1.5), tau ~ Half-t(3,0,0.5)
default_prior("fisher_z")  # beta ~ N(0,1),   tau ~ Half-t(3,0,0.3)
default_prior("generic")   # beta ~ N(0,10),  tau ~ Half-t(3,0,1)
Math-Code Mapping. The .prior_to_stan_data() function maps each bm_prior object to the scalar hyperparameters that Stan expects:
.prior_to_stan_data <- function(prior, model = "ce") {
  tau_df <- prior$tau$df
  if (is.infinite(tau_df)) tau_df <- 200  # Stan requires finite df

  result <- list(
    prior_beta_mean    = prior$beta$mean,
    prior_beta_sd      = prior$beta$sd,
    prior_tau_df       = tau_df,
    prior_tau_location = prior$tau$location,
    prior_tau_scale    = prior$tau$scale
  )

  if (model == "che") {
    omega_prior <- prior$omega
    if (is.null(omega_prior)) omega_prior <- half_t(3, 0, 0.5)
    omega_df <- omega_prior$df
    if (is.infinite(omega_df)) omega_df <- 200
    result$prior_omega_df       <- omega_df
    result$prior_omega_location <- omega_prior$location
    result$prior_omega_scale    <- omega_prior$scale
  }
  result
}
One subtlety: df = Inf (Half-Normal) is mapped to df = 200 as a finite approximation, because Stan’s student_t distribution does not accept infinite degrees of freedom. At \(\nu = 200\), the Student-\(t\) is numerically indistinguishable from the Normal.








R Constructor
Stan Data Field
Value




normal(mean, sd)
prior_beta_mean, prior_beta_sd
mean, sd


half_t(df, loc, scale)
prior_tau_df, prior_tau_location, prior_tau_scale
df, loc, scale

prior_tau_df
200


CHE half_t(df, loc, scale)
prior_omega_df, prior_omega_location, prior_omega_scale
df, loc, scale





4.4.4 Prior Sensitivity
Priors on \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) have minimal influence when \(J\) is moderate to large. Priors on \(\tau\) (and \(\omega\)) matter more when \(J\) is small (say, \(J < 10\)) or when \(\tau\) is near zero, where the Half-\(t\) prior’s tail behavior determines posterior mass near the boundary. The simulation study in Section 14.2 confirms that with \(J \geq 10\) and default priors, Bayesian point estimates closely track their REML counterparts.
crumbs: 4  Statistical Models and Stan Implementation

title: 4  Statistical Models and Stan Implementation
text:
4.5 Non-Centered vs. Centered Parameterization

4.5.1 The Funnel Problem
The CE model has two parameterizations. In the centered parameterization (CP):
\[
\theta_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \tau^2),
\]
whereas in the non-centered parameterization (NCP):
\[
\theta_j^{\text{raw}} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1), \qquad \theta_j = \tau \cdot \theta_j^{\text{raw}}.
\]
The two are mathematically equivalent but produce different posterior geometries. When \(\tau\) is small (near zero), the CP creates a “funnel” in \((\tau, \theta_j)\) space: the conditional distribution \(\theta_j \mid \tau\) concentrates near zero, producing a sharp curvature that Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) has difficulty navigating. This manifests as divergent transitions.
The NCP decouples \(\theta_j^{\text{raw}}\) from \(\tau\). The raw parameters maintain a standard normal marginal regardless of \(\tau\), eliminating the funnel geometry.


4.5.2 Stan Implementation: CP vs. NCP
The difference is confined to the parameters and transformed parameters blocks. All other blocks (data, transformed data, generated quantities) are identical.
CP (bm_ce_cp.stan):
parameters {
  vector[K] beta;
  vector[J] theta;          // sampled directly on the tau scale
  real<lower=0> tau;
}

model {
  theta ~ normal(0, tau);   // CP: theta depends on tau
  // ...
}
NCP (bm_ce_ncp.stan):
parameters {
  vector[K] beta;
  vector[J] theta_raw;      // sampled on unit scale
  real<lower=0> tau;
}

transformed parameters {
  vector[J] theta = tau * theta_raw;   // deterministic transform
}

model {
  theta_raw ~ normal(0, 1);            // NCP: independent of tau
  // ...
}
Math-Code Mapping. In the NCP, theta_raw corresponds to \(\theta_j^{\text{raw}}\) and the line theta = tau * theta_raw implements \(\theta_j = \tau \cdot \theta_j^{\text{raw}}\). Stan’s automatic differentiation handles the Jacobian implicitly (the transformation is linear, so the Jacobian determinant is \(\tau^J\), which is correctly accounted for). The CP samples \(\theta_j\) directly via theta ~ normal(0, tau).








Block
NCP
CP




parameters
vector[J] theta_raw
vector[J] theta


transformed parameters
theta = tau * theta_raw
(none)


model (prior on \(\theta\))
theta_raw ~ normal(0, 1)
theta ~ normal(0, tau)





4.5.3 Decision D3: NCP as Default
bayesRVE uses NCP as the default parameterization (Decision D3). This choice is motivated by the observation that most meta-analyses have moderate-to-small \(\tau\) relative to the sampling standard deviations, which is precisely the regime where NCP outperforms CP. The parameterization argument in bm_fit() allows users to switch to CP when \(\tau\) is large and well-identified:
# NCP (default)
fit_ncp <- bm_fit(yi | se(vi) ~ 1, data = dat, study = ~ studyid)

# CP (user override)
fit_cp  <- bm_fit(yi | se(vi) ~ 1, data = dat, study = ~ studyid,
                   parameterization = "cp")


4.5.4 CHE: Marginal Likelihood (No NCP/CP Distinction)
The CHE model integrates out \(\theta_j\) analytically and samples only \((\boldsymbol{\beta}, \tau, \omega)\). Since there are no latent \(\theta_j\) parameters in the Stan model, the NCP/CP distinction does not apply. The “NCP” label in the filename bm_che_ncp.stan is retained solely for naming consistency across the model family.

text:
4.6 Stan Implementation Details

4.6.1 Model File Inventory
bayesRVE ships three Stan model files:









File
Model
Parameters
Likelihood

CE (Non-Centered)
\(\boldsymbol{\beta}, \theta^{\text{raw}}, \tau\)
Conditional


bm_ce_cp.stan
CE (Centered)
\(\boldsymbol{\beta}, \theta, \tau\)
Conditional


bm_che_ncp.stan
CHE (Marginal)
\(\boldsymbol{\beta}, \tau, \omega\)
Marginal





4.6.2 Generated Quantities
All three models produce the following quantities in the generated quantities block, computed at each MCMC draw:

Study-level log-likelihoods (log_lik_J[j]): for LOO-CV diagnostics via leave-one-study-out cross-validation.
Score gradients (score_beta[j,k]): the \(\partial \ell_j / \partial \beta_k\) contributions needed by the sandwich estimator.
Theta scores (score_theta[j]): the \(\partial \ell_j / \partial \theta_j\) for the CE model (zeroed for CHE).
Posterior predictive draws (y_rep[idx]): for graphical model checking.

The CHE model additionally exports:

Sufficient statistics (ss_sum_r[j], ss_sum_r2[j], ss_a[j], ss_c[j]): enabling R-side computation of variance-component scores.



4.6.3 CE Posterior Predictive
for (i in 1:n_j) {
  int idx = start + i - 1;
  real mu_ij = dot_product(X[idx], beta) + theta[j];
  y_rep[idx] = normal_rng(mu_ij, sd_j);
}
Math-Code Mapping. Each replicate \(y_{ij}^{\text{rep}}\) is drawn from \(\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_{ij}^\top\boldsymbol{\beta}^{(s)} + \theta_j^{(s)}, \bar{s}^2_j)\), using the current MCMC draw. The dot_product(X[idx], beta) computes \(\mathbf{x}_{ij}^\top\boldsymbol{\beta}\).


4.6.4 CHE Posterior Predictive
{
  real theta_j = normal_rng(0, tau);
  real sd_obs = sqrt(a_j);           // sqrt(s_bar_sq_j + omega^2)
  for (i in 1:n_j) {
    int idx = start + i - 1;
    real mu_ij = dot_product(X[idx], beta) + theta_j;
    y_rep[idx] = normal_rng(mu_ij, sd_obs);
  }
}
Math-Code Mapping. Since the CHE model has no explicit \(\theta_j\) draws, a fresh \(\theta_j\) is sampled from \(\mathcal{N}(0, \tau^2)\) within the generated quantities block at each MCMC iteration. The observation-level noise uses \(\sqrt{a_j} = \sqrt{\bar{s}^2_j + \omega^2}\), matching the conditional model in Equation 4.7. This generates draws from the full generative model: \(\theta_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \tau^2)\), then \(y_{ij}^{\text{rep}} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_{ij}^\top\boldsymbol{\beta} + \theta_j, a_j)\).

crumbs: 4  Statistical Models and Stan Implementation

title: 4  Statistical Models and Stan Implementation
text:
4.7 The bm_fit() Pipeline
The bm_fit() function orchestrates an 8-step pipeline from user input to a fitted model object.

4.7.1 Step 1: Call Capture
cl <- match.call()
t0 <- proc.time()
The matched call is stored for reproducibility and display in print.bm_fit().


4.7.2 Step 2: Input Validation
model <- match.arg(model)              # "ce" or "che"
parameterization <- match.arg(parameterization)  # "ncp" or "cp"
es_type <- match.arg(es_type)          # "smd", "log_or", "fisher_z", "generic"

assert_data_frame(data, min.rows = 1L)
assert_count(chains, positive = TRUE)
assert_count(iter_warmup, positive = TRUE)
assert_count(iter_sampling, positive = TRUE)
assert_number(adapt_delta, lower = 0.5, upper = 1)
assert_count(max_treedepth, positive = TRUE)
Math-Code Mapping. All user-facing arguments are validated using the checkmate package. The prior defaults to default_prior(es_type) when prior = NULL.


4.7.3 Step 3: cmdstanr Availability
rlang::check_installed("cmdstanr",
  reason = "to compile and run Stan models for Bayesian meta-analysis."
)
Since cmdstanr is in Suggests (not Imports), availability is checked at runtime with a user-friendly message.


4.7.4 Step 4: Data Preparation
bm_data <- .prepare_data(formula, data, study)
J <- bm_data$J
K <- bm_data$K
This step parses the formula, validates columns, sorts by study, builds the design matrix via model.matrix(), checks full rank, computes study-level summaries (\(\bar{y}_j\), \(\bar{s}^2_j\), \(w_j = n_j/\bar{s}^2_j\)), and detects cluster constancy. The output is a bm_data S3 object bundling both the Stan data list and R-side metadata.


4.7.5 Step 5: Stan Data Assembly
stan_input <- .build_stan_input(bm_data, prior, model = model)
This merges the data-only list with prior hyperparameters from .prior_to_stan_data(). For CHE models, omega prior fields are added; if prior$omega is NULL, half_t(3, 0, 0.5) is used as default. The compute_scores flag is set to 1L unconditionally.


4.7.6 Step 6: Model Compilation
model_name <- paste0("bm_", model, "_", parameterization)  # e.g., "bm_ce_ncp"
mod <- .bm_model(model_name)
The model name is constructed by concatenating the model family and parameterization. The .bm_model() helper locates and caches the pre-compiled Stan model.


4.7.7 Step 7: MCMC Sampling
sample_args <- list(
  data            = stan_input,
  chains          = as.integer(chains),
  parallel_chains = min(as.integer(chains), n_cores),
  iter_warmup     = as.integer(iter_warmup),
  iter_sampling   = as.integer(iter_sampling),
  adapt_delta     = adapt_delta,
  max_treedepth   = as.integer(max_treedepth)
)

cmdstan_fit <- do.call(mod$sample, sample_args)
Math-Code Mapping. The defaults are: 4 chains, 1000 warmup + 1000 sampling iterations each (yielding \(S = 4000\) posterior draws), adapt_delta = 0.95 (conservative for hierarchical models), and max_treedepth = 12 (higher than Stan’s default of 10). Parallel chains are capped at the number of physical cores. All ... arguments are passed through to allow user overrides.


4.7.8 Step 8: Post-Sampling Diagnostics
.validate_cmdstan_fit(cmdstan_fit, adapt_delta, max_treedepth)
The .validate_cmdstan_fit() function checks three diagnostic indicators, all wrapped in tryCatch for robustness:

Divergent transitions: warns with the current adapt_delta value and a recommendation to increase it.
Maximum treedepth exceedances: warns with the current max_treedepth value.
Low E-BFMI (energy Bayesian fraction of missing information): flags chains with E-BFMI < 0.2, which may indicate a funnel pathology.



4.7.9 Return Value: The bm_fit S3 Object
structure(
  list(
    fit        = cmdstan_fit,       # CmdStanMCMC object
    stan_data  = stan_input,        # complete Stan data list
    bm_data    = bm_data,           # preprocessed bm_data object
    formula    = formula,           # user-supplied formula
    prior      = prior,             # bm_prior object
    param      = parameterization,  # "ncp" or "cp"
    model      = model,             # "ce" or "che"
    K          = K,                 # number of fixed effects
    J          = J,                 # number of studies
    model_name = model_name,        # e.g., "bm_ce_ncp"
    call       = cl,                # matched call
    elapsed    = elapsed            # wall-clock time (seconds)
  ),
  class = "bm_fit"
)








Slot

This object serves as the primary input to the downstream pipeline: sandwich() for robust variance estimation (Chapter 6), ccc() for Cholesky Calibration Correction (Section 6.1), and summary()/confint() for inference. The model field (added in v0.6.0) enables clean CE/CHE dispatch in all downstream methods.
A typical end-to-end workflow:
# Fit the model
fit <- bm_fit(yi | se(vi) ~ treatment + duration,
              data = dat, study = ~ studyid,
              model = "che", seed = 42)

# Sandwich variance estimation (Chapter 3)
sw <- sandwich(fit, rho = 0.8)

# Cholesky Calibration Correction (Chapter 4)
cal <- ccc(fit, sandwich_obj = sw)
summary(cal)
The bm_fit object carries all information needed by downstream functions: posterior draws (via fit$fit), study structure (via fit$bm_data), and model identity (via fit$model) that determines how the sandwich and CCC are computed.






Cheung, M. W.-L. (2014). Modeling dependent effect sizes with three-level meta-analyses: A structural equation modeling approach. Psychological Methods, 19(2), 211–229. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0032968


Van den Noortgate, W., López-López, J. A., Marín-Martínez, F., & Viechtbauer, W. (2013). Three-level meta-analysis of dependent effect sizes. Behavior Research Methods, 45(2), 576–594. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13428-012-0261-6
