title: 5  Sandwich Variance Estimation
text:
6 Sandwich Variance Estimation
This chapter develops the complete theory of cluster-robust sandwich variance estimation as implemented in sandwich.R, the mathematical heart of bayesRVE. The sandwich estimator provides the frequentist-valid variance target that the Cholesky Calibration Correction (CCC, Section 6.1) uses to recalibrate Bayesian posterior draws. We cover the HC0 estimator, the CR2 bias correction of Bell & McCaffrey (2002), Satterthwaite degrees of freedom following Tipton (2015), and the CHE overlay pattern that unifies the correlated-effects (CE) and correlated-hierarchical-effects (CHE) models under a single computational framework.
The presentation is self-contained: a reader familiar with weighted least squares and basic matrix algebra can follow this chapter without reference to other parts of the report.

title: 5  Sandwich Variance Estimation
section: 6.1 Why the Full-Joint Sandwich Is the Wrong Target
text:
6.1 Why the Full-Joint Sandwich Is the Wrong Target

6.1.1 The Full Parameter Vector
In the CE conditional model (Section 4.2), the full parameter vector is
\[
\boldsymbol{\psi} = (\boldsymbol{\beta}', \theta_1, \ldots, \theta_J)' \in \mathbb{R}^{K+J},
\tag{6.1}\]
where \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) collects \(K\) fixed-effect coefficients and \(\theta_j\) are study-specific random intercepts. A naive approach would compute the full \((K+J) \times (K+J)\) sandwich:
\[
\mathbf{V}_{\text{sand}}^{\text{full}} = \mathbf{H}_{\text{full}}^{-1} \, \mathbf{M}_{\text{full}} \, \mathbf{H}_{\text{full}}^{-1},
\tag{6.2}\]
where \(\mathbf{H}_{\text{full}}\) is the posterior Hessian and \(\mathbf{M}_{\text{full}} = \sum_{j=1}^{J} \mathbf{s}_j \mathbf{s}_j'\) is the outer-product meat. This approach fails for three distinct reasons.
The \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\)-block collapses. In the conditional model, \(\theta_j\) acts as a study-specific intercept that absorbs nearly all between-study variation. The \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\)-scores \(\partial \ell_j / \partial \boldsymbol{\beta}\) become nearly constant across studies, so the meat’s \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\)-block—which measures between-study heterogeneity of scores—is near zero. The sandwich would dramatically understate the true sampling variance of \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\).
Structural rank deficiency. The meat \(\mathbf{M}_{\text{full}} = \sum_{j=1}^{J} \mathbf{s}_j \mathbf{s}_j'\) is a sum of \(J\) rank-one matrices, so \(\operatorname{rank}(\mathbf{M}_{\text{full}}) \leq J\). Since the parameter dimension is \(d = K + J > J\) (for \(K \geq 1\)), \(\mathbf{M}_{\text{full}}\) is structurally singular. A Cholesky factorization of the full sandwich, as CCC requires, is therefore impossible. This reflects the classical incidental-parameters problem (Neyman & Scott, 1948): with \(J\) nuisance parameters estimated from \(J\) clusters, the meat cannot span the full parameter space.
Cubic computational cost. Inverting a \((K+J) \times (K+J)\) Hessian costs \(O((K+J)^3)\), which becomes prohibitive as \(J\) grows into the hundreds.


6.1.2 The Blockwise Strategy
The correct approach, codified in Decision D2, is a blockwise hybrid target. The key insight is that \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) and \(\boldsymbol{\theta}\) play fundamentally different roles: \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) is the inferential target; \(\boldsymbol{\theta}\) is a vector of nuisance parameters.
For the \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\)-block, we integrate out \(\boldsymbol{\theta}\) to obtain a marginal model in which the between-study heterogeneity captured by \(\theta_j\) is absorbed into the marginal variance \(v_j = \bar{s}_j^2 / n_j + \tau^2\). The marginal sandwich then measures the correct quantity: the between-study variability of \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\)-scores under the marginal likelihood.
For the \(\boldsymbol{\theta}\)-block, we condition on \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) and exploit the fact that, given \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\), the \(\theta_j\) are conditionally independent. The conditional sandwich reduces to \(J\) scalar computations.
The CCC target is therefore block-diagonal:
\[
\mathbf{V}_{\text{CCC}} = \begin{pmatrix}
\mathbf{V}_{\text{CCC}}^{(\beta)} & \mathbf{0} \\
\mathbf{0} & \mathbf{V}_{\text{CCC}}^{(\theta)}
\end{pmatrix},
\tag{6.3}\]
where \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{CCC}}^{(\beta)}\) is a \(K \times K\) matrix from the marginal sandwich and \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{CCC}}^{(\theta)}\) is a diagonal \(J \times J\) matrix from the conditional sandwich. The cross-block is set to zero by design (not estimation), reflecting the blockwise application of CCC (Section 6.1). This reduces the Cholesky cost from \(O((K+J)^3)\) to \(O(K^3 + J)\).
In sandwich.R, the marginal sandwich is computed by .marginal_sandwich() and the conditional sandwich by .conditional_sandwich(). The CHE model, where \(\boldsymbol{\theta}\) is analytically integrated out, requires only the marginal component (via .che_sandwich()). The final object is assembled by .assemble_bm_sandwich().

title: 5  Sandwich Variance Estimation
section: 6.2 The Marginal Score and HC0 Sandwich
text:
6.2 The Marginal Score and HC0 Sandwich

6.2.1 The CE Marginal Model
Integrating out \(\theta_j\) from the CE conditional model (Section 4.2) yields the marginal distribution of study means:
\[
\bar{y}_j \mid \boldsymbol{\beta}, \tau^2 \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\bar{\mathbf{x}}_j' \boldsymbol{\beta},\; v_j\right),
\quad v_j = \frac{\bar{s}_j^2}{n_j} + \tau^2,
\tag{6.4}\]
where \(\bar{y}_j = n_j^{-1} \sum_i y_{ij}\) is the study mean, \(\bar{\mathbf{x}}_j = n_j^{-1} \sum_i \mathbf{x}_{ij}\) is the study-mean design vector, and \(\bar{s}_j^2 = n_j^{-1} \sum_i V_{ij}\) is the mean sampling variance. The marginal variance \(v_j\) combines within-study sampling uncertainty (\(\bar{s}_j^2 / n_j\)) and between-study heterogeneity (\(\tau^2\)).
The marginal parameter vector is \(\boldsymbol{\phi} = (\boldsymbol{\beta}', \tau^2)' \in \mathbb{R}^{K+1}\).


6.2.2 Score Function
The study-level marginal log-likelihood is
\[
\ell_j(\boldsymbol{\phi}) = -\tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[\log(2\pi) + \log v_j + r_j^2 / v_j\right],
\tag{6.5}\]
where \(r_j = \bar{y}_j - \bar{\mathbf{x}}_j' \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) is the marginal residual. Differentiating with respect to each parameter at the posterior mean \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}}\) yields the likelihood score vector:
\[
\mathbf{s}_j = \begin{pmatrix}
\frac{\partial \ell_j}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial \ell_j}{\partial \tau^2}
\end{pmatrix}
= \begin{pmatrix}
\frac{r_j}{v_j} \bar{\mathbf{x}}_j \\[4pt]
-\frac{1}{2v_j} + \frac{r_j^2}{2v_j^2}
\end{pmatrix}
\in \mathbb{R}^{K+1}.
\tag{6.6}\]
The \(J \times (K+1)\) score matrix \(\mathbf{S} = (\mathbf{s}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{s}_J)'\) is computed in .marginal_sandwich(). In the study-level path (cluster-constant predictors), the computation is fully vectorized:
score_beta <- (r_j / v_j) * x_bar       # J x K
score_tau2 <- -1 / (2 * v_j) + r_j^2 / (2 * v_j^2)
S <- cbind(score_beta, score_tau2)       # J x (K+1)


6.2.3 Bread, Meat, and HC0 Sandwich
The posterior Hessian (bread) uses the positive-definite convention \(\mathbf{H} = -\partial^2 \log p(\boldsymbol{\phi} \mid \mathbf{y}) / \partial \boldsymbol{\phi} \partial \boldsymbol{\phi}'\) and decomposes as
\[
\mathbf{H}_{\text{post}} = \mathbf{H}_{\text{lik}} + \mathbf{H}_{\text{prior}},
\tag{6.7}\]
where \(\mathbf{H}_{\text{lik}}\) is the negative second derivative of the likelihood and \(\mathbf{H}_{\text{prior}}\) captures prior curvature. The likelihood Hessian has blocks (computed in .build_marginal_hessian()):
\[
\begin{aligned}
H_{\beta\beta} &= \sum_{j=1}^{J} \frac{\bar{\mathbf{x}}_j \bar{\mathbf{x}}_j'}{v_j}, &
H_{\beta,\tau^2} &= \sum_{j=1}^{J} \frac{r_j \bar{\mathbf{x}}_j}{v_j^2}, &
H_{\tau^2,\tau^2} &= \sum_{j=1}^{J} \left[-\frac{1}{2v_j^2} + \frac{r_j^2}{v_j^3}\right].
\end{aligned}
\tag{6.8}\]
The stabilized prior Hessian (.build_prior_hessian()) is diagonal: \(\operatorname{diag}(1/\sigma_\beta^2, \ldots, 1/\sigma_\beta^2, \,h_{\tau^2})\), where \(h_{\tau^2}\) is a numerical ridge stabilizer capped at \(10^6\) to prevent overflow when \(\hat{\tau}^2 \approx 0\).
The meat is the outer product of likelihood scores only:
\[
\mathbf{M} = \sum_{j=1}^{J} \mathbf{s}_j \mathbf{s}_j' = \mathbf{S}'\mathbf{S}.
\tag{6.9}\]
Prior scores are excluded from the meat. This is the foundational principle of sandwich estimation: the meat measures data-driven between-cluster variability, which captures working-model misspecification. Including prior scores would contaminate this signal with data-external information, destroying the estimator’s robustness. The prior enters only through the bread, where it determines the location of the Bayesian center; its influence is \(O(1/J)\) relative to the likelihood contribution and vanishes asymptotically.
The HC0 sandwich estimator is
\[
\mathbf{V}_{\text{HC0}} = \mathbf{H}_{\text{post}}^{-1} \, \mathbf{M} \, \mathbf{H}_{\text{post}}^{-1}.
\tag{6.10}\]
The \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\)-block target is the upper-left \(K \times K\) submatrix of \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{HC0}}\). In .marginal_sandwich(), this extraction is
V_sand <- H_inv %*% M %*% H_inv
V_beta <- V_sand[seq_len(K), seq_len(K), drop = FALSE]


6.2.4 Finite-Sample Bias of HC0
While \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{HC0}}\) is consistent as \(J \to \infty\), it is downward-biased in finite samples:
\[
\mathrm{E}[\mathbf{V}_{\text{HC0}}] < \mathrm{Var}_{\text{freq}}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}),
\tag{6.11}\]
because the residuals \(\hat{e}_j\) used in the meat understate the true errors—the fitted model absorbs some of the variation that should appear in the residuals. This bias is analogous to the well-known \(n/(n-p)\) correction in ordinary least squares but takes a matrix-valued form in the clustered setting. The CR2 correction of Bell & McCaffrey (2002), described in Section 6.5, addresses this bias.

title: 5  Sandwich Variance Estimation
section: 6.3 Weight Matrices and the Woodbury Identity
text:
6.3 Weight Matrices and the Woodbury Identity

6.3.1 The Working Covariance and Its Inverse
Under the CE working model (\(\rho_{\text{work}} = 0\)), the marginal covariance of \(\mathbf{y}_j\) is
\[
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j = \bar{s}_j^2 \mathbf{I}_{n_j} + \tau^2 \mathbf{1}_{n_j} \mathbf{1}_{n_j}',
\tag{6.12}\]
a scaled identity plus a rank-one matrix. The weight matrix \(\mathbf{W}_j = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{-1}\) is computed by the Woodbury matrix identity.
Applying the Woodbury matrix identity (the full derivation is given in Section B.1) yields:
\[
\mathbf{W}_j = \frac{1}{\bar{s}_j^2}\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j} - \frac{\tau^2}{\bar{s}_j^2 \, c_j}\,\mathbf{J}_{n_j},
\tag{6.13}\]
where \(\mathbf{J}_{n_j} = \mathbf{1}_{n_j}\mathbf{1}_{n_j}'\) is the \(n_j \times n_j\) all-ones matrix and
\[
c_j = \bar{s}_j^2 + n_j \tau^2.
\tag{6.14}\]
The intuition is that the rank-one structure of \(\tau^2\mathbf{J}_{n_j}\) allows the Woodbury identity to reduce the \(n_j \times n_j\) inversion to a scalar computation. The correction term \(-(\tau^2/(\bar{s}_j^2 c_j))\mathbf{J}_{n_j}\) downweights the common component shared across effect sizes within a study.
This is implemented in .compute_W_j(). For singletons (\(n_j = 1\)), the weight reduces to the scalar \(1/(\bar{s}_j^2 + \tau^2)\).
The Woodbury form reduces the computational cost of \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{-1}\) from \(O(n_j^3)\) to \(O(n_j)\), and all downstream operations—score computation (\(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{-1} \mathbf{r}_j\)), Hessian blocks (\(\mathbf{X}_j' \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{-1} \mathbf{X}_j\))—benefit proportionally. For example, applying \(\mathbf{W}_j\) to a residual vector:
\[
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{-1} \mathbf{r}_j = \frac{\mathbf{r}_j}{\bar{s}_j^2} - \frac{\tau^2 \cdot \bar{r}_j}{\bar{s}_j^2 \cdot v_j}\,\mathbf{1}_{n_j},
\tag{6.15}\]
where \(\bar{r}_j = n_j^{-1} \sum_i r_{ij}\) and \(v_j = \bar{s}_j^2 / n_j + \tau^2\).


6.3.2 The Study-Level Path
When all predictors are cluster-constant (the common case), the observation-level computation collapses to a scalar path. The study-mean marginal model has variance \(v_j = \bar{s}_j^2 / n_j + \tau^2\), and the weight is the scalar \(w_j = 1/v_j\). In .compute_wls_bread(), this is the “study” level path:
v_j <- s_bar2 / n_j + tau2_hat
W_list[[j]] <- matrix(1 / v_j[j], 1L, 1L)
X_list[[j]] <- matrix(x_bar[j, ], nrow = 1L)
The study-level and observation-level paths produce identical results for cluster-constant designs but differ when within-study varying moderators are present (Section 6.7).


6.3.3 CHE Weight Matrices
The CHE model adds within-study heterogeneity \(\omega^2\), giving marginal covariance
\[
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\text{CHE}} = (\bar{s}_j^2 + \omega^2)\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j} + \tau^2\,\mathbf{J}_{n_j} = a_j\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j} + \tau^2\,\mathbf{J}_{n_j},
\tag{6.16}\]
where the unified absorption parameter is
\[
a_j = \bar{s}_j^2 + \hat{\omega}^2.
\tag{6.17}\]
Setting \(\omega^2 = 0\) recovers the CE case. The Woodbury inverse is identical in form to Equation 6.13 with \(\bar{s}_j^2\) replaced by \(a_j\):
\[
\mathbf{W}_j^{\text{CHE}} = \frac{1}{a_j}\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j} - \frac{\tau^2}{a_j\,c_j}\,\mathbf{J}_{n_j},
\quad c_j = a_j + n_j\tau^2.
\tag{6.18}\]
In .compute_W_j(), the omega2_hat parameter (default 0) controls this substitution, implementing Decision D20.


6.3.4 Analytical \(\mathbf{W}_j^{1/2}\): The Rank-1 Woodbury Square Root
The CR2 symmetrization procedure (Section 6.5) requires both \(\mathbf{W}_j^{1/2}\) and \(\mathbf{W}_j^{-1/2}\). Because \(\mathbf{W}_j\) has compound-symmetric structure, its eigenvalues are \(\lambda_1 = 1/c_j\) (eigenvector \(\mathbf{1}_{n_j}/\sqrt{n_j}\)) and \(\lambda_2 = 1/a_j\) (multiplicity \(n_j - 1\)). Taking the positive square root of each eigenvalue and reassembling via the spectral theorem yields closed-form expressions (Decision D24). The full derivation is given in Section B.2.
\[
\boxed{
\mathbf{W}_j^{1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a_j}}\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j} + \frac{1}{n_j}\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{c_j}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{a_j}}\right)\mathbf{J}_{n_j}.
}
\tag{6.19}\]
\[
\boxed{
\mathbf{W}_j^{-1/2} = \sqrt{a_j}\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j} + \frac{1}{n_j}\bigl(\sqrt{c_j} - \sqrt{a_j}\bigr)\,\mathbf{J}_{n_j}.
}
\tag{6.20}\]
The key insight is that the rank-1 structure of \(\mathbf{J}_{n_j}\) means \(\mathbf{W}_j\) has only two distinct eigenvalues, so both the square root and inverse square root remain in the same \(\alpha\mathbf{I} + \beta\mathbf{J}\) family—no eigendecomposition is needed at runtime. This analytical formula, implemented in .compute_W_j_sqrt(), reduces cost from \(O(n_j^3)\) to \(O(1)\) scalar operations plus \(O(n_j)\) array fills. The function falls back to eigendecomposition only when eigenvalues are non-positive (a numerical edge case).

title: 5  Sandwich Variance Estimation
section: 6.4 The WLS Bread and Hat Matrix
text:
6.4 The WLS Bread and Hat Matrix

6.4.1 Why the WLS Bread, Not the Posterior Hessian
Decision D15 mandates that the CR2 computation use the weighted-least-squares (WLS) bread
\[
\mathbf{Q} = \left(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{W}\mathbf{X}\right)^{-1} = \left(\sum_{j=1}^{J} \mathbf{X}_j'\,\mathbf{W}_j\,\mathbf{X}_j\right)^{-1}
\tag{6.21}\]
rather than the posterior Hessian \(\mathbf{H}_{\text{post}}^{-1}\). This distinction is critical. CR2 is a frequentist bias correction derived from the properties of the WLS hat matrix; it assumes the bread is the inverse of the weighted normal equations. The posterior Hessian includes prior precision, which would distort the hat matrix and invalidate the CR2 adjustment.
In the study-level path, Equation 6.21 simplifies to
\[
\mathbf{Q} = \left(\sum_{j=1}^{J} \frac{\bar{\mathbf{x}}_j\,\bar{\mathbf{x}}_j'}{v_j}\right)^{-1},
\tag{6.22}\]
where \(v_j = a_j / n_j + \tau^2\). In the observation-level path (within-study varying predictors), the full \(n_j \times n_j\) weight matrices \(\mathbf{W}_j\) are used.
The WLS bread is computed by .compute_wls_bread(), which returns \(\mathbf{Q}\), the per-study weight matrices \(\mathbf{W}_j\), design matrices \(\mathbf{X}_j\), and a level indicator (“study” or “obs”). For the CHE model, the omega2_hat argument flows through to .compute_W_j(), implementing the \(a_j = \bar{s}_j^2 + \hat{\omega}^2\) substitution.


6.4.2 The Hat Matrix
The cluster-level hat matrix block for study \(j\) is
\[
\mathbf{H}_{jj} = \mathbf{X}_j\,\mathbf{Q}\,\mathbf{X}_j'\,\mathbf{W}_j,
\tag{6.23}\]
an \(n_j \times n_j\) matrix (generally not symmetric). In the study-level path, this reduces to the scalar leverage \(h_j = \bar{\mathbf{x}}_j'\,\mathbf{Q}\,\bar{\mathbf{x}}_j / v_j\). The hat matrix blocks are computed by .compute_hat_blocks(), which also tracks diagnostic quantities including the maximum leverage across studies.
The diagonal entries of \(\mathbf{H}_{jj}\) measure how much influence study \(j\) exerts on the fitted values. When \(\max_j h_j > 0.5\), the function emits a warning because the CR2 adjustment may be numerically unstable for high-leverage studies.


6.4.3 The Symmetrized Hat Matrix
For the CR2 eigendecomposition, we need a symmetric version of \(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}_{jj}\). The symmetrization uses the weight square root from Section 6.3.4:
\[
\tilde{\mathbf{H}}_{jj} = \mathbf{W}_j^{1/2}\,\mathbf{X}_j\,\mathbf{Q}\,\mathbf{X}_j'\,\mathbf{W}_j^{1/2},
\tag{6.24}\]
which is symmetric by construction. The matrix \(\mathbf{I} - \tilde{\mathbf{H}}_{jj}\) then has a well-defined matrix square root via eigendecomposition, which is the foundation of the CR2 adjustment.

title: 5  Sandwich Variance Estimation
text:
6.5 CR2 Bias Correction

6.5.1 The Bias Equation
The HC0 sandwich Equation 6.10 uses residuals \(\hat{\mathbf{e}}_j = \mathbf{y}_j - \mathbf{X}_j\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) in the meat. Because \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) is fitted to the data, the residuals systematically underestimate the true errors. To see the exact form of this bias, decompose the WLS residual.
The WLS estimator is \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{Q}\,\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{W}\,\mathbf{y}\), so the residual for study \(j\) is
\[
\hat{\mathbf{e}}_j = \mathbf{y}_j - \mathbf{X}_j\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{y}_j - \mathbf{X}_j\mathbf{Q}\sum_{l=1}^{J}\mathbf{X}_l'\mathbf{W}_l\mathbf{y}_l.
\tag{6.25}\]
Writing \(\mathbf{y}_l = \mathbf{X}_l\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_l\) and using \(\mathbf{X}_j\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{W}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{X}_j\boldsymbol{\beta}\):
\[
\hat{\mathbf{e}}_j = \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_j - \sum_{l=1}^{J} \mathbf{X}_j\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{X}_l'\mathbf{W}_l\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_l = (\mathbf{I}_{n_j} - \mathbf{H}_{jj})\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_j - \sum_{l \neq j}\mathbf{H}_{jl}\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_l,
\tag{6.26}\]
where \(\mathbf{H}_{jl} = \mathbf{X}_j\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{X}_l'\mathbf{W}_l\) and \(\mathbf{H}_{jj} = \mathbf{X}_j\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{X}_j'\mathbf{W}_j\) is the \(j\)-th diagonal block of the hat matrix.
Under between-study independence (\(\mathrm{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_j, \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_l) = \mathbf{0}\) for \(j \neq l\)), the expected outer product is
\[\begin{align}
\mathrm{E}[\hat{\mathbf{e}}_j\hat{\mathbf{e}}_j']
&= (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}_{jj})\,\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j\,(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}_{jj})' + \sum_{l \neq j}\mathbf{H}_{jl}\,\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_l\,\mathbf{H}_{jl}'. \tag{3.B1}
\end{align}\]
The first term dominates and is strictly smaller than \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j\) (since \(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}_{jj}\) is a contraction). The second (cross-study) term is \(O(1/J)\) and vanishes asymptotically. The net effect is that the HC0 meat is downward-biased:
\[
\mathrm{E}[\hat{\mathbf{e}}_j\hat{\mathbf{e}}_j'] \neq \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j,
\tag{6.27}\]
and the deficit grows with leverage.
The scalar corrections HC1 (\(J/(J-K)\)), HC2 (\((1-h_j)^{-1}\)), and HC3 (\((1-h_j)^{-2}\)) address this for univariate observations. For clustered data, the bias correction must be matrix-valued: each study \(j\) requires an \(n_j \times n_j\) adjustment that accounts for the full hat matrix block \(\mathbf{H}_{jj}\), not merely its trace or diagonal.


6.5.2 The CR2 Goal
The CR2 correction of Bell & McCaffrey (2002), adapted for meta-analysis by Tipton (2015), seeks adjustment matrices \(\mathbf{A}_j\) such that the sandwich variance estimator is unbiased:
\[
\mathrm{E}[\mathbf{V}_{\text{CR2}}] = \text{Var}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = \mathbf{Q}.
\tag{6.28}\]
This aggregate target is achieved by choosing each \(\mathbf{A}_j\) to solve a per-study sufficient condition. Focusing on the dominant within-study contribution (the cross-study terms are \(O(1/J)\) and vanish under study independence), the design equation for \(\mathbf{A}_j\) is
\[
\mathbf{A}_j\,(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}_{jj})\,\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j\,(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}_{jj})'\,\mathbf{A}_j' = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j.
\tag{6.29}\]


6.5.3 Symmetrization via \(\mathbf{W}^{1/2}\)
A complication arises because \(\mathbf{H}_{jj} = \mathbf{X}_j\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{X}_j'\mathbf{W}_j\) is generally not symmetric (the right multiplication by \(\mathbf{W}_j\) breaks symmetry whenever \(\mathbf{W}_j\) has off-diagonal elements). The matrix \(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}_{jj}\) is therefore not symmetric, and a matrix square root is not uniquely defined without additional structure.
Bell & McCaffrey (2002) resolved this by conjugating with \(\mathbf{W}_j^{1/2}\). Define the symmetrized hat block
\[
\tilde{\mathbf{H}}_{jj} = \mathbf{W}_j^{1/2}\,\mathbf{X}_j\,\mathbf{Q}\,\mathbf{X}_j'\,\mathbf{W}_j^{1/2}.
\tag{6.30}\]
This is symmetric because it has the form \(\mathbf{B}\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{B}'\) with \(\mathbf{B} = \mathbf{W}_j^{1/2}\mathbf{X}_j\). The relationship to the original hat block is a similarity transform:
\[
\tilde{\mathbf{H}}_{jj} = \mathbf{W}_j^{1/2}\,\mathbf{H}_{jj}\,\mathbf{W}_j^{-1/2},
\tag{6.31}\]
so \(\tilde{\mathbf{H}}_{jj}\) and \(\mathbf{H}_{jj}\) have the same eigenvalues.


6.5.4 Derivation of \(\mathbf{A}_j\)
The derivation proceeds by conjugating the bias equation Equation 6.29 with \(\mathbf{W}_j^{1/2}\) to work in a symmetrized space, solving for the adjustment in that space, and back-transforming. The full step-by-step proof is given in Section B.3. The result is:
\[
\boxed{
\mathbf{A}_j = \mathbf{W}_j^{-1/2}\,(\mathbf{I}_{n_j} - \tilde{\mathbf{H}}_{jj})^{-1/2}\,\mathbf{W}_j^{1/2}.
}
\tag{6.32}\]
The three computational stages are: (1) form the symmetrized hat matrix \(\tilde{\mathbf{H}}_{jj}\) using the analytical \(\mathbf{W}_j^{1/2}\) from Equation 6.19; (2) eigendecompose \(\mathbf{I}_{n_j} - \tilde{\mathbf{H}}_{jj}\) and take the inverse square root of each eigenvalue; (3) sandwich between \(\mathbf{W}_j^{-1/2}\) and \(\mathbf{W}_j^{1/2}\). In the implementation (.compute_cr2_adjustments()), eigenvalues of \(\mathbf{I} - \tilde{\mathbf{H}}_{jj}\) below \(10^{-10}\) are truncated for numerical stability, with a warning.


6.5.5 The Scalar Case
When \(n_j = 1\) or at the study level, all matrices reduce to scalars. The hat block becomes \(H_{jj} = h_j = \bar{\mathbf{x}}_j'\mathbf{Q}\bar{\mathbf{x}}_j \cdot w_j\), and the CR2 adjustment simplifies to
\[
A_j = (1 - h_j)^{-1/2},
\tag{6.33}\]
the familiar HC2-type correction (MacKinnon and White, 1985) applied at the cluster level.
This three-step procedure (symmetrize, eigendecompose, back-transform) is implemented in .compute_cr2_adjustments(). When a study’s leverage \(h_j\) exceeds 0.999, it is capped to prevent numerical overflow, with a warning.


6.5.6 The CR2 Sandwich
With the adjustment matrices in hand, the CR2-adjusted scores are
\[
\tilde{\mathbf{s}}_j = \mathbf{X}_j'\,\mathbf{W}_j\,\mathbf{A}_j\,\hat{\mathbf{e}}_j,
\tag{6.34}\]
and the CR2 sandwich variance estimator is
\[
\boxed{
\mathbf{V}_{\text{CR2}} = \mathbf{Q}\left(\sum_{j=1}^{J} \tilde{\mathbf{s}}_j\,\tilde{\mathbf{s}}_j'\right)\mathbf{Q}.
}
\tag{6.35}\]
The bread is the WLS bread \(\mathbf{Q}\) (Equation 6.21), not the posterior Hessian. This is computed in .compute_vcr2(). In the study-level path, each adjusted score is a \(K\)-vector:
s_tilde_j <- x_bar[j, ] * w_j * a_j * r_j
M_CR2 <- M_CR2 + tcrossprod(s_tilde_j)
Decision D12, finalized in v0.5.0, mandates that \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{CR2}}\) (not \(c_\beta \cdot \mathbf{V}_{\text{HC0}}\)) is the CCC target. This is because CR2 provides study-specific, matrix-valued corrections that a scalar multiplier cannot replicate, particularly in designs with heterogeneous study sizes or unbalanced moderators.
The key properties of CR2 that distinguish it from simpler corrections are:

Bias removal. \(\mathrm{E}[\mathbf{V}_{\text{CR2}}] \approx \mathrm{Var}_{\text{freq}}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}})\) under the working model (Bell & McCaffrey, 2002), with the approximation becoming exact when cross-study hat matrix blocks are negligible.
Matrix-valued adjustment. Each \(\mathbf{A}_j\) is an \(n_j \times n_j\) matrix, capturing the full influence structure within each cluster.
Exact recovery. When all \(n_j = 1\) and leverage is uniform, CR2 reduces to the classical \(J/(J-K)\) correction.

title: 5  Sandwich Variance Estimation
text:
6.6 Satterthwaite Degrees of Freedom

6.6.1 The Reference Distribution Problem
Even with the bias-reduced \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{CR2}}\), inference requires a reference distribution for the test statistic
\[
T_k = \frac{\hat{\beta}_k}{\operatorname{SE}_{\text{CR2}}(\hat{\beta}_k)} = \frac{\hat{\beta}_k}{\sqrt{[\mathbf{V}_{\text{CR2}}]_{kk}}}.
\tag{6.36}\]
Under \(H_0: \beta_k = 0\), the numerator \(\hat{\beta}_k\) is approximately normal, but the denominator \(\operatorname{SE}_{\text{CR2}}\) is itself a random variable (it depends on the data through the residuals and variance component estimates). Using \(T_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)\) treats \(\operatorname{SE}_{\text{CR2}}\) as if it were known, ignoring its sampling variability and leading to undercoverage when \(J\) is small. The simulation study sim3 confirmed this: CCC intervals using posterior quantiles (CCC-q, implicitly \(z\)-based) achieved only 91–94% coverage at the nominal 95% level when \(J \leq 20\), while CCC-\(t\) with Satterthwaite degrees of freedom achieved 94.5–95.3%.
The classical solution is to model \(T_k^2 \approx F(1, \nu_k)\) under \(H_0\), or equivalently \(T_k \sim t_{\nu_k}\), where the degrees of freedom \(\nu_k\) quantify how precisely \(\operatorname{SE}_{\text{CR2}}\) estimates the true standard error. Larger \(\nu_k\) means the variance estimate is more precise, producing a reference distribution closer to the normal; smaller \(\nu_k\) yields heavier tails and wider intervals.


6.6.2 The Satterthwaite Moment-Matching Principle
Satterthwaite (1946) proposed approximating the distribution of a positive quadratic form in normal variables by a scaled chi-squared distribution, with the scale and degrees of freedom determined by matching the first two moments.
Suppose \(\hat{V}\) is a variance estimator with \(\mathrm{E}[\hat{V}] = V\) and \(\mathrm{Var}(\hat{V}) = \sigma^2_V\). We seek \(c\) and \(\nu\) such that \(\hat{V} \approx c\,\chi^2_\nu\), which has \(\mathrm{E}[c\chi^2_\nu] = c\nu\) and \(\mathrm{Var}(c\chi^2_\nu) = 2c^2\nu\). Setting \(c\nu = V\) and \(2c^2\nu = \sigma^2_V\) and eliminating \(c\):
\[
\nu = \frac{2V^2}{\sigma^2_V} = \frac{2\,[\mathrm{E}(\hat{V})]^2}{\mathrm{Var}(\hat{V})}.
\tag{6.37}\]
If \(\hat{V}\) is an equally-weighted average of \(m\) independent \(\chi^2_1\) variables (all with the same scale), the formula gives \(\nu = m\)—the exact result. When the weights are unequal, \(\nu < m\), reflecting the reduced effective sample size.


6.6.3 Derivation of \(\nu_k\) for the CR2 Estimator
We now derive the Satterthwaite degrees of freedom for the \(k\)-th diagonal element of \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{CR2}}\), following Bell & McCaffrey (2002) and the generalization by Imbens & Kolesár (2016).
Step 1: Express \(\hat{V}_k\) as a sum of squared linear forms.
Let \(\mathbf{c}_k \in \mathbb{R}^K\) be the \(k\)-th standard basis vector. The variance estimate for coefficient \(k\) is
\[
\hat{V}_k = \mathbf{c}_k'\,\mathbf{V}_{\text{CR2}}\,\mathbf{c}_k
= \mathbf{c}_k'\mathbf{Q}\left(\sum_{j=1}^J \tilde{\mathbf{s}}_j\tilde{\mathbf{s}}_j'\right)\mathbf{Q}\,\mathbf{c}_k
= \sum_{j=1}^J \bigl(\mathbf{c}_k'\mathbf{Q}\,\tilde{\mathbf{s}}_j\bigr)^2.
\tag{6.38}\]
Each term \(\mathbf{c}_k'\mathbf{Q}\,\tilde{\mathbf{s}}_j\) is a scalar that depends on the \(j\)-th cluster’s adjusted score.
Step 2: Linearize in the errors.
Expanding the adjusted score \(\tilde{\mathbf{s}}_j = \mathbf{X}_j'\mathbf{W}_j\mathbf{A}_j\hat{\mathbf{e}}_j\) and the residual \(\hat{\mathbf{e}}_j = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}_{jj})\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_j - \sum_{l \neq j}\mathbf{H}_{jl}\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_l\):
\[
\mathbf{c}_k'\mathbf{Q}\,\tilde{\mathbf{s}}_j = \mathbf{c}_k'\mathbf{Q}\,\mathbf{X}_j'\mathbf{W}_j\mathbf{A}_j\left[(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}_{jj})\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_j - \sum_{l \neq j}\mathbf{H}_{jl}\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_l\right].
\]
This is a linear function of the full error vector \(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = (\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_1', \ldots, \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_J')'\). Therefore \(\hat{V}_k\) is a quadratic form in Gaussian errors, and the Satterthwaite approximation applies.
Step 3: Define the deterministic \(\mathbf{g}_{jk}\) vectors.
For each study \(j\) and coefficient \(k\), define the \(n_j\)-vector
\[
\mathbf{g}_{jk} = \mathbf{A}_j'\,\mathbf{W}_j\,\mathbf{X}_j\,\mathbf{Q}\,\mathbf{c}_k.
\tag{6.39}\]
This vector is deterministic conditional on the plug-in variance components (\(\hat{\tau}^2\), \(\hat{\omega}^2\))—it depends on the design matrices \(\mathbf{X}_j\), the weights \(\mathbf{W}_j\), the bread \(\mathbf{Q}\), and the CR2 adjustments \(\mathbf{A}_j\), but not directly on the outcome vector \(\mathbf{y}\). The inner product \(\mathbf{g}_{jk}'\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_l\) captures how the errors from study \(l\) contribute to the \(k\)-th variance estimate through study \(j\)’s adjusted score.
Step 4: Compute \(\mathrm{Var}(\hat{V}_k)\) via the \(J \times J\) matrix \(\mathbf{P}_k\).
Because \(\hat{V}_k\) is a quadratic form in the Gaussian vector \(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\), its variance is determined by the trace of the square of an associated matrix. Define the symmetric \(J \times J\) matrix \(\mathbf{P}_k\) as follows.
The contribution of cluster \(j\)’s errors to \(\hat{V}_k\) (through both its own residual and its influence on other clusters’ residuals via the hat matrix) gives rise to the \((i,j)\) element of \(\mathbf{P}_k\).
Diagonal elements: The term \([\mathbf{P}_k]_{jj}\) measures the variance contribution of study \(j\) alone:
\[
[\mathbf{P}_k]_{jj} = \mathbf{g}_{jk}'\,\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j\,\mathbf{g}_{jk} - \mathbf{g}_{jk}'\,\mathbf{X}_j\,\mathbf{Q}\,\mathbf{X}_j'\,\mathbf{g}_{jk}.
\tag{6.40}\]
The first term is \(\mathbf{g}_{jk}'\mathbf{W}_j^{-1}\mathbf{g}_{jk}\) (since \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j = \mathbf{W}_j^{-1}\)), which is the variance of \(\mathbf{g}_{jk}'\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_j\). The second term is a leverage correction that accounts for the fact that \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) absorbs some of the signal from study \(j\).
Off-diagonal elements: The cross-cluster terms arise from the hat matrix coupling:
\[
[\mathbf{P}_k]_{ij} = -\mathbf{g}_{ik}'\,\mathbf{X}_i\,\mathbf{Q}\,\mathbf{X}_j'\,\mathbf{g}_{jk}, \qquad i \neq j.
\tag{6.41}\]
This can be rewritten efficiently using the \(K\)-vector \(\mathbf{X}_j'\mathbf{g}_{jk}\):
\[
[\mathbf{P}_k]_{ij} = -(\mathbf{X}_i'\mathbf{g}_{ik})'\,\mathbf{Q}\,(\mathbf{X}_j'\mathbf{g}_{jk}),
\tag{6.42}\]
requiring only a \(K\)-dimensional inner product per pair \((i, j)\).
The matrix \(\mathbf{P}_k\) is symmetric by construction (the off-diagonal formula is symmetric in \(i\) and \(j\)).
Step 5: Apply the moment-matching formula.
Under normality, it can be shown that \(\mathrm{E}[\hat{V}_k] = \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{P}_k)\) and \(\mathrm{Var}(\hat{V}_k) = 2\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{P}_k^2)\) (Bell & McCaffrey, 2002, Lemma 1). Substituting into Equation 6.37:
\[
\boxed{
\nu_k = \frac{[\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{P}_k)]^2}{\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{P}_k^2)}.
}
\tag{6.43}\]
The traces are computed as:
\[
\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{P}_k) = \sum_{j=1}^J [\mathbf{P}_k]_{jj}, \qquad
\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{P}_k^2) = \sum_{i=1}^J \sum_{j=1}^J [\mathbf{P}_k]_{ij}^2,
\tag{6.44}\]
where the second equality uses \(\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{P}^2) = \|\mathbf{P}\|_F^2\) for symmetric matrices.


6.6.4 Connection to Imbens & Kolesár (2016)
Imbens & Kolesár (2016) generalized the Bell–McCaffrey framework to arbitrary linear contrasts \(\mathbf{c}'\boldsymbol{\beta}\). Their Theorem 1 shows that under normality of \(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\), the random variable \(\hat{V}_{\mathbf{c}} / \mathrm{E}[\hat{V}_{\mathbf{c}}]\) is exactly distributed as a weighted sum of independent \(\chi^2_1\) variables:
\[
\frac{\hat{V}_{\mathbf{c}}}{\mathrm{E}[\hat{V}_{\mathbf{c}}]} \sim \sum_{i=1}^J \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_l \lambda_l}\,\chi^2_{1,i},
\]
where \(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_J\) are the eigenvalues of \(\mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{c}}\). The Satterthwaite approximation \(\nu = (\sum \lambda_i)^2 / (\sum \lambda_i^2)\) matches the first two moments of this distribution exactly. This provides a rigorous theoretical justification for Equation 6.43 beyond the heuristic moment-matching argument.


6.6.5 Computational Implementation
The computation is implemented in .satterthwaite_df(). For efficiency, the function precomputes \(\mathbf{G}_j = \mathbf{A}_j'\,\mathbf{W}_j\,\mathbf{X}_j\,\mathbf{Q}\) (an \(n_j \times K\) matrix) for each study, so that \(\mathbf{g}_{jk} = \mathbf{G}_j[\,,\,k]\). The computation proceeds as:

Precompute \(\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{X}_j'\) for each \(j\) (used in the off-diagonal terms).
Precompute \(\mathbf{G}_j = \mathbf{A}_j'\mathbf{W}_j\mathbf{X}_j\mathbf{Q}\) for each \(j\) (one matrix product).
For each coefficient \(k\):

Extract \(\mathbf{g}_{jk} = \mathbf{G}_j[\,,\,k]\) for all \(j\).
Precompute \(\mathbf{X}_j'\mathbf{g}_{jk}\) for all \(j\) (\(K\)-vectors).
Build the \(J \times J\) matrix \(\mathbf{P}_k\) using Equation 6.40 and Equation 6.42.
Compute \(\nu_k = (\operatorname{tr}\mathbf{P}_k)^2 / \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{P}_k^2)\).


The total cost per coefficient is \(O(J^2 K)\) for the off-diagonal elements.


6.6.6 Interpretation and Edge Cases
Per-coefficient degrees of freedom. Each \(\beta_k\) has its own \(\nu_k\), reflecting the fact that different coefficients may be estimated with different effective sample sizes. A coefficient that correlates strongly with high-leverage studies will have smaller \(\nu_k\).
Upper bound. When all studies contribute equally (balanced design, equal \(n_j\)), \(\mathbf{P}_k\) approaches a scalar multiple of \(\mathbf{I}_J\), and \(\nu_k \to J\).
Deterministic property. The \(\mathbf{g}_{jk}\) vectors (Equation 6.39) depend only on \(\mathbf{X}\), \(\mathbf{W}\), \(\mathbf{Q}\), and \(\mathbf{A}\)—not on the data \(\mathbf{y}\). Therefore \(\nu_k\) is a fixed property of the design and variance components, not a random variable.
Small \(\mathrm{df}_k\). When a few studies have high leverage (e.g., one study with many effect sizes and a unique moderator value), those studies dominate the variance estimate. The \(t\) distribution has heavier tails, producing wider intervals. Tipton (2015) reported that with \(J = 28\), a single coefficient could have \(\mathrm{df}_k = 2.8\), resulting in a critical value of \(t_{2.8, 0.025} = 3.28\) versus \(z_{0.025} = 1.96\)—a 67% wider interval.
Fallback logic. When the Satterthwaite computation fails for a coefficient (e.g., \(\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{P}_k^2) \approx 0\) or numerical overflow), the function returns NA and the caller substitutes the naive degrees of freedom \(\mathrm{df}_{\text{naive}} = \max(J - K, 1)\). When \(\mathrm{df}_k < 1\), the value is also replaced with \(\mathrm{df}_{\text{naive}}\). When \(\mathrm{df}_k < 4\), a warning is emitted following Tipton (2015)’s recommendation that inference is unreliable in this regime. The df_source vector in the bm_sandwich object records whether each coefficient used the Satterthwaite or naive value.

title: 5  Sandwich Variance Estimation
text:
6.7 Within-Study Varying Moderators
When the design matrix \(\mathbf{X}\) contains predictors that vary within studies (e.g., outcome type, measurement instrument), the study-level aggregation \(\bar{\mathbf{x}}_j = n_j^{-1}\sum_i \mathbf{x}_{ij}\) is no longer sufficient. The scores must be computed at the observation level.
Auto-detection. The function .is_cluster_constant() in data-prep.R checks whether all predictors are constant within each study during data preparation. The result is stored in bm_data$cluster_constant and queried by sandwich.bm_fit() to select the computation path.
Observation-level scores. When within-study varying predictors are detected, the \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\)-score for study \(j\) is computed as
\[
\mathbf{s}_j^{(\beta)} = \mathbf{X}_j'\,\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{-1}\,\mathbf{r}_j
= \sum_{i=1}^{n_j} \left[\frac{r_{ij}}{\bar{s}_j^2} - \frac{\tau^2\,\bar{r}_j}{\bar{s}_j^2\,v_j}\right] \mathbf{x}_{ij},
\tag{6.45}\]
using the Woodbury form of \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{-1}\mathbf{r}_j\) (Equation 6.15). The \(\tau^2\)-score remains a study-level quantity because the marginal likelihood for \(\tau^2\) depends only on \(\bar{r}_j\).
Impact on dimensions. In the observation-level path, \(\mathbf{W}_j\) is \(n_j \times n_j\) (not \(1 \times 1\)), \(\mathbf{X}_j\) is \(n_j \times K\) (not \(1 \times K\)), and the hat matrix block \(\mathbf{H}_{jj}\) is \(n_j \times n_j\). All downstream CR2 computations—the symmetrized hat matrix, the \(\mathbf{A}_j\) adjustment, and the Satterthwaite \(\mathbf{g}_{jk}\) vectors—operate at observation level without modification to their mathematical form, only to their dimensions.
In .compute_wls_bread(), the observation-level path is
X_list[[j]] <- X_obs[idx, , drop = FALSE]    # n_j x K
W_list[[j]] <- .compute_W_j(s_bar2[j], tau2_hat, nj)
XtWX <- XtWX + crossprod(X_list[[j]], W_list[[j]] %*% X_list[[j]])

title: 5  Sandwich Variance Estimation
text:
6.8 The CHE Overlay Pattern

6.8.1 The Substitution Principle
The central insight of the v0.6.0 implementation is that the CHE model requires no new mathematical machinery for sandwich estimation. The CHE marginal covariance (Equation 6.16) has the same algebraic structure as the CE covariance (Equation 6.12) with the substitution \(\bar{s}_j^2 \to a_j = \bar{s}_j^2 + \hat{\omega}^2\). Every formula in Section 6.3 through Section 6.6 carries over with this single parameter change.
The plug-in estimator for \(\omega^2\) is
\[
\hat{\omega}^2 = \frac{1}{S}\sum_{s=1}^{S} \omega^{(s)2},
\tag{6.46}\]
the posterior mean of \(\omega^2\) (Decision D20). This is the mean of the squared draws, not the square of the mean draw. The distinction matters because \(\mathrm{E}[\omega^2] \neq (\mathrm{E}[\omega])^2\) by Jensen’s inequality; using \((\bar{\omega})^2\) would underestimate the true posterior mean of the variance component. This convention is consistent with the CE treatment: \(\hat{\tau}^2 = S^{-1}\sum_s \tau^{(s)2}\).


6.8.2 Implementation: A Single Parameter
In .compute_W_j(), the CHE overlay is a single line:
a_j <- s_bar2_j + omega2_hat
In .compute_wls_bread(), it flows through as the omega2_hat argument:
bread_info <- .compute_wls_bread(bm_data, tau2_hat, K, J,
                                  omega2_hat = omega2_hat)
All downstream functions—.compute_hat_blocks(), .compute_cr2_adjustments(), .compute_vcr2(), .satterthwaite_df()—are reused without modification. The CHE dispatch in sandwich.bm_fit() delegates to .che_sandwich(), which calls the same shared infrastructure.


6.8.3 Gate 1: CE–CHE Equivalence
When \(\omega^2 = 0\), the CHE weights reduce to CE weights at machine precision. This is verified by the Gate 1 test: fitting a CHE model to data generated with \(\omega^2 = 0\) and confirming that all sandwich outputs (V_CR2, Satterthwaite df) match the CE result to within floating-point tolerance. This gate ensures that the CHE overlay introduces no regressions for the CE special case.


6.8.4 Singleton Non-Identifiability
When a study has \(n_j = 1\), the within-study variance \(\omega^2\) and between-study variance \(\tau^2\) are not separately identified from the data; separation relies entirely on the prior. Decision D22 mandates warnings in .che_sandwich():

If all studies are singletons (\(n_j = 1\) for all \(j\)): a warning that \(\tau^2\) and \(\omega^2\) are “not separately identified.”
If more than 50% of studies are singletons: a warning that the parameters may be “weakly identified.”

These warnings do not block computation but alert the user that CR2 benchmarks from a frequentist multilevel analysis may diverge from the Bayesian results in this regime.


6.8.5 The Plug-In Parity Test (Decision D25)
To validate the linear-algebra pathway independently of MCMC noise, Decision D25 specifies a plug-in parity test: inject the REML estimates of \(\tau^2\) and \(\omega^2\) (from metafor::rma.mv) into the bayesRVE sandwich machinery and verify that \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{CR2}}\) matches the clubSandwich::vcovCR(type = "CR2") result to within \(10^{-6}\). This test isolates the CR2 computation from posterior estimation, confirming that the shared CE/CHE infrastructure produces numerically identical results to the reference implementation. The plug-in parity test passes for both CE and CHE models across all test scenarios in the v0.6.0 validation suite.






Bell, R. M., & McCaffrey, D. F. (2002). Bias reduction in standard errors for linear regression with multi-stage samples. Survey Methodology, 28(2), 169–181.


Imbens, G. W., & Kolesár, M. (2016). Robust standard errors in small samples: Some practical advice. Review of Economics and Statistics, 98(4), 701–712. https://doi.org/10.1162/REST_a_00552


Neyman, J., & Scott, E. L. (1948). Consistent estimates based on partially consistent observations. Econometrica, 16(1), 1–32. https://doi.org/10.2307/1914288


Satterthwaite, F. E. (1946). An approximate distribution of estimates of variance components. Biometrics Bulletin, 2(6), 110–114. https://doi.org/10.2307/3002019


Tipton, E. (2015). Small sample adjustments for robust variance estimation with meta-regression. Psychological Methods, 20(3), 375–393. https://doi.org/10.1037/met0000011

title: 6  Cholesky Calibration Correction
text: 6.1 Definition and Geometric Intuition

title: 6  Cholesky Calibration Correction
text:
6.1.1 The CCC Affine Transformation
The Cholesky Calibration Correction (CCC) occupies the third stage of the bayesRVE inference pipeline:
\[
\texttt{bm\_fit()} \;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{sandwich()} \;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{ccc()} \;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{summary()}
\]
Given \(S\) posterior draws \(\psi^{(1)}, \ldots, \psi^{(S)}\) of a \(d\)-dimensional parameter vector \(\psi\), with posterior mean \(\hat{\psi} = S^{-1}\sum_{s=1}^{S}\psi^{(s)}\) and sample covariance \(\hat{\Sigma}_{\text{MCMC}}\), and a symmetric positive definite target \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{target}}\), CCC defines calibrated draws:
\[
\psi^{*(s)} = \hat{\psi} + \mathbf{A}\!\left(\psi^{(s)} - \hat{\psi}\right), \quad s = 1, \ldots, S
\tag{6.1}\]
where the transformation matrix is
\[
\mathbf{A} = L_2 \, L_1^{-1},
\tag{6.2}\]
with \(L_1 = \operatorname{chol}(\hat{\Sigma}_{\text{MCMC}})\) and \(L_2 = \operatorname{chol}(\mathbf{V}_{\text{target}})\) denoting lower-triangular Cholesky factors of the MCMC covariance and target covariance, respectively.
In bayesRVE, \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{target}}\) is the CR2 sandwich variance estimator from Chapter 6, making CCC the mechanism that bridges Bayesian posterior inference and frequentist robust variance estimation.


6.1.2 Geometric Interpretation
CCC admits a vivid geometric reading. Consider the posterior draws as a cloud of points in \(\mathbb{R}^d\). Under a well-specified model, this cloud forms an ellipsoid whose shape and orientation are governed by the posterior covariance \(\hat{\Sigma}_{\text{MCMC}}\). The sandwich variance \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{target}}\) defines a second, “target” ellipsoid encoding the robust frequentist uncertainty.
The Cholesky factorization decomposes each covariance into a sequence of elementary operations: \(L_1\) maps the unit sphere to the MCMC ellipsoid, and \(L_2\) maps it to the target ellipsoid. The transformation \(\mathbf{A} = L_2 L_1^{-1}\) therefore:

Unstandardizes the draws: \(L_1^{-1}\) maps the MCMC ellipsoid back to a sphere, removing the posterior’s shape and orientation.
Restandardizes to the target: \(L_2\) then maps the sphere to the target ellipsoid, imposing the sandwich covariance structure.

The net effect is a rotation and scaling of the posterior draw cloud so that its covariance exactly matches the sandwich ellipsoid, while preserving its center at \(\hat{\psi}\). When the two ellipsoids already coincide — that is, when the working model is correctly specified — \(L_1 = L_2\) and \(\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{I}_d\): the cloud passes through unchanged.
This ellipsoid-matching perspective clarifies several features of CCC:

Bidirectional calibration. If the posterior ellipsoid is narrower than the sandwich ellipsoid along some axis, CCC stretches the draws along that axis. If it is wider, CCC compresses. Both directions are valid calibration.
Correlation structure. CCC does not merely rescale marginal variances; the full \(\mathbf{A}\) matrix rotates the cloud to match off-diagonal covariances as well.
Uniqueness. The positive-diagonal Cholesky factorization of a positive definite matrix is unique, so \(\mathbf{A}\) is uniquely determined by the pair \((\hat{\Sigma}_{\text{MCMC}}, \mathbf{V}_{\text{target}})\).



6.1.3 Scalar Special Case
When \(d = 1\), CCC reduces to variance rescaling:
\[
\psi^{*(s)} = \hat{\psi} + r\,(\psi^{(s)} - \hat{\psi}), \quad r = \sqrt{\frac{V_{\text{target}}}{\hat{\Sigma}_{\text{MCMC}}}}
\tag{6.3}\]
Here \(r > 1\) means the posterior is too narrow (CCC widens), \(r < 1\) means too wide (CCC narrows), and \(r = 1\) is identity. This scalar form is precisely the operation applied to each \(\theta_j\) in the theta-block diagonal rescaling (Section 6.5).
Note the relationship to the diagnostic ratio \(R_k = \text{SD}(\psi_k^*) / \text{SD}(\psi_k)\) reported by bayesRVE: in the scalar case, \(R_k = r\) exactly. In the multivariate case (\(K > 1\)), \(R_k\) is the marginal SD ratio for coefficient \(k\), which reflects the combined effect of the \(K \times K\) Cholesky transformation including cross-coefficient correlations.

title: 6  Cholesky Calibration Correction
text:
6.2 Core Theorems
Three theorems establish the algebraic properties of CCC. All three are finite-sample identities requiring no asymptotic arguments.

6.2.1 Theorem 1: Center Preservation
Theorem 1. CCC preserves the posterior mean:
\[
\bar{\psi}^* \;=\; \frac{1}{S}\sum_{s=1}^{S} \psi^{*(s)} \;=\; \hat{\psi}.
\tag{6.4}\]
Proof. By linearity of the sample mean and Equation 6.1:
\[
\bar{\psi}^* = \frac{1}{S}\sum_{s=1}^{S}\left[\hat{\psi} + \mathbf{A}(\psi^{(s)} - \hat{\psi})\right] = \hat{\psi} + \mathbf{A}\!\left(\frac{1}{S}\sum_{s=1}^{S}\psi^{(s)} - \hat{\psi}\right) = \hat{\psi} + \mathbf{A}\cdot\mathbf{0} = \hat{\psi}.
\]
The cancellation \(\bar{\psi} - \hat{\psi} = \mathbf{0}\) holds because \(\hat{\psi}\) is defined as the posterior sample mean. \(\square\)
Theorem 1 holds exactly (to machine precision) when the center is the posterior mean. For alternative centers such as the posterior median, the calibrated sample mean is \(\hat{\psi}_{\text{med}} + \mathbf{A}(\bar{\psi} - \hat{\psi}_{\text{med}})\), which generally differs from both \(\hat{\psi}_{\text{med}}\) and \(\bar{\psi}\). This is the primary reason posterior mean is the default center in bayesRVE.

6.2.1.1 Verification
The .verify_ccc() function checks Theorem 1 as verification V1: it computes
mean_dev <- max(abs(colMeans(draws_star) - center))
and asserts mean_dev < 1e-6. Because center preservation is an exact algebraic identity under posterior-mean centering, deviations exceeding this threshold indicate an implementation bug, and .verify_ccc() raises an error (not a warning). In all 2,701 passing tests at v0.6.0, this check passes with deviations at the level of \(10^{-14}\) to \(10^{-12}\), consistent with IEEE 754 double-precision arithmetic. See Section 12.1 for full benchmark results.



6.2.2 Theorem 2: Exact Covariance Matching
Theorem 2. The sample covariance of the calibrated draws equals the target covariance:
\[
\operatorname{Cov}(\psi^{*(1)}, \ldots, \psi^{*(S)}) = \mathbf{V}_{\text{target}}.
\tag{6.5}\]
Proof. Define centered draws \(\tilde{\psi}^{(s)} = \psi^{(s)} - \hat{\psi}\). By Theorem 1, the calibrated draws satisfy \(\psi^{*(s)} - \bar{\psi}^* = \mathbf{A}\tilde{\psi}^{(s)}\). The sample covariance is therefore:
\[
\operatorname{Cov}(\psi^*) = \frac{1}{S-1}\sum_{s=1}^{S} \mathbf{A}\tilde{\psi}^{(s)}\tilde{\psi}^{(s)\top}\mathbf{A}^\top = \mathbf{A}\,\hat{\Sigma}_{\text{MCMC}}\,\mathbf{A}^\top.
\]
Substituting \(\mathbf{A} = L_2 L_1^{-1}\) and \(\hat{\Sigma}_{\text{MCMC}} = L_1 L_1^\top\):
\[
\mathbf{A}\,\hat{\Sigma}_{\text{MCMC}}\,\mathbf{A}^\top = L_2 L_1^{-1} L_1 L_1^\top (L_1^{-1})^\top L_2^\top = L_2 L_2^\top = \mathbf{V}_{\text{target}}.
\]
\(\square\)
This is an exact, finite-sample algebraic identity, not an asymptotic approximation. It holds regardless of the number of draws \(S\), the distribution from which the original draws were generated, or the specific values of \(\hat{\Sigma}_{\text{MCMC}}\) and \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{target}}\).

6.2.2.1 Verification
The .verify_ccc() function checks Theorem 2 in two stages:

V2 (diagonal): For each \(k\), the ratio \(\operatorname{Var}(\beta_k^*) / [\mathbf{V}_{\text{sand,marg}}]_{kk}\) is computed and checked to be within 5% of unity.
V4 (full covariance, \(K \geq 2\)): The relative Frobenius norm \(\|\operatorname{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\beta}^*) - \mathbf{V}_{\text{sand,marg}}\|_F / \|\mathbf{V}_{\text{sand,marg}}\|_F\) is checked against a 5% tolerance.

Because CCC uses the sample covariance of the draws to construct \(L_1\), the algebraic identity guarantees that these checks pass to machine precision for the beta-block (typically deviations of order \(10^{-13}\)). The 5% tolerance is set conservatively to accommodate the theta-block, where near-zero MCMC variances can introduce numerical noise. See Section 12.1 for full benchmark results.



6.2.3 Theorem 3: Identity Under Correct Specification
Theorem 3. If the MCMC posterior covariance equals the target, CCC is the identity transformation:
\[
\hat{\Sigma}_{\text{MCMC}} = \mathbf{V}_{\text{target}} \implies \mathbf{A} = \mathbf{I}_d \implies \psi^{*(s)} = \psi^{(s)} \;\;\forall\, s.
\tag{6.6}\]
Proof. If \(\hat{\Sigma}_{\text{MCMC}} = \mathbf{V}_{\text{target}}\), then \(L_1 = L_2\) because the Cholesky factorization of a positive definite matrix with positive diagonal is unique. Therefore \(\mathbf{A} = L_2 L_1^{-1} = L_1 L_1^{-1} = \mathbf{I}_d\), and \(\psi^{*(s)} = \hat{\psi} + \mathbf{I}_d(\psi^{(s)} - \hat{\psi}) = \psi^{(s)}\). \(\square\)
Theorem 3 is the theoretical foundation for the “always-on” CCC design in bayesRVE. When the working model is correctly specified, the Bayesian posterior already has the correct frequentist covariance, and CCC passes the draws through unchanged. This means applying CCC incurs no cost under correct specification — the Frobenius norm \(\|\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{I}\|_F\) is zero, and no draws are altered. There is therefore no need for a diagnostic gate (such as a design-effect ratio threshold) to decide whether to apply CCC; it is safe to apply unconditionally.

6.2.3.1 Verification
Theorem 3 is verified in the test suite by constructing synthetic scenarios where the MCMC covariance is set exactly equal to the sandwich target. In these tests:
# Synthetic: V_target == cov(draws) by construction
# => A_beta should be identity, frob_norm should be 0
expect_equal(obj$frobenius_norm, 0, tolerance = 1e-10)
expect_equal(obj$draws_star, obj$draws_orig, tolerance = 1e-10)
The identity is confirmed to machine precision (\(\|\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{I}\|_F < 10^{-12}\)). This is the Tier 0 algebraic gate that must pass before any MCMC-based benchmarks proceed. See Section 12.1 for full benchmark results.

title: 6  Cholesky Calibration Correction
text:
6.3 Blockwise Structure

6.3.1 Why Full-Joint CCC Is Impossible
The full parameter vector \(\boldsymbol{\phi} = (\boldsymbol{\beta}^\top, \theta_1, \ldots, \theta_J)^\top\) has dimension \(d = K + J\). The full-joint sandwich meat matrix is a sum of \(J\) rank-1 outer products:
\[
\mathbf{M}_{\text{full}} = \sum_{j=1}^{J} \mathbf{s}_j \mathbf{s}_j^\top, \quad \mathbf{s}_j \in \mathbb{R}^{K+J},
\]
so \(\operatorname{rank}(\mathbf{M}_{\text{full}}) \leq J < K + J = d\) for any \(K \geq 1\). The full-joint sandwich is therefore structurally singular, and its Cholesky factorization does not exist. Full-joint CCC is mathematically impossible.
This rank deficiency is not a finite-sample artifact but an algebraic consequence of the incidental parameters structure: each study \(j\) contributes exactly one independent observation unit (one score vector \(\mathbf{s}_j\)) to the meat, but the parameter vector has \(K + J\) components. This is the sandwich-variance manifestation of the Neyman & Scott (1948) incidental parameters problem.
Applying nearPD() to the singular sandwich to force positive definiteness is explicitly rejected as an anti-pattern. Such an approach inflates eigenvalues artificially, breaking the exact covariance matching property of Theorem 2 and conferring false precision.


6.3.2 Block-Diagonal Target
Decision D2 mandates blockwise hybrid CCC with a block-diagonal target:
\[
\mathbf{V}_{\text{CCC}} = \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{V}_{\text{CCC}}^{(\beta)} & \mathbf{0}_{K \times J} \\ \mathbf{0}_{J \times K} & \mathbf{V}_{\text{CCC}}^{(\theta)} \end{pmatrix}
\tag{6.7}\]
The beta-block \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{CCC}}^{(\beta)} = \mathbf{V}_{\text{sand,marg}}^{(\beta)}\) is the \(K \times K\) marginal sandwich from Chapter 6, a full (generally non-diagonal) matrix that captures the correlation structure among fixed effects. The theta-block \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{CCC}}^{(\theta)} = \operatorname{diag}(V_{\text{sand,cond}}^{(\theta_1)}, \ldots, V_{\text{sand,cond}}^{(\theta_J)})\) is a diagonal matrix of per-study conditional sandwich variances. The cross-block is zero — not as a statistical claim that \(\beta\)-\(\theta\) covariance is zero, but as a design decision that cross-block covariance is not targeted by CCC.
This blockwise structure is justified on three grounds:

Different inferential targets. \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) are population-level parameters and \(\boldsymbol{\theta}\) are study-specific random effects, answering different research questions.
Different sandwich sources. The beta-block target comes from the marginal sandwich and the theta-block target from the conditional sandwich — two different statistical models.
Computational efficiency. Full \((K+J) \times (K+J)\) Cholesky costs \(O((K+J)^3)\); blockwise costs \(O(K^3 + J)\). With \(K \leq 10\) and \(J\) potentially in the hundreds, this difference is substantial.

A key consequence is block independence: the beta-block and theta-block CCC operations have no data dependency and can be computed independently. Each block achieves Theorem 2 within its own subspace:
\[
\operatorname{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\beta}^*) = \mathbf{V}_{\text{CCC}}^{(\beta)}, \quad \operatorname{diag}\!\left\{\operatorname{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\theta}^*)\right\} = \operatorname{diag}\!\left(\mathbf{V}_{\text{CCC}}^{(\theta)}\right).
\]
The assembled calibrated draws have cross-block covariance \(\mathbf{A}_\beta \hat{\Sigma}_{\beta\theta} \mathbf{D}\) (where \(\mathbf{D} = \operatorname{diag}(r_1, \ldots, r_J)\)), which is generally nonzero but not targeted.

title: 6  Cholesky Calibration Correction
text:
6.4 Beta-Block CCC Algorithm

6.4.1 Algorithm Steps
The beta-block applies full Cholesky CCC to the \(K\)-dimensional fixed-effects posterior:
Step B1. Extract posterior draws \(\mathbf{B} = [\boldsymbol{\beta}^{(1)}, \ldots, \boldsymbol{\beta}^{(S)}]^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{S \times K}\).
Step B2. Compute the centering point \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = S^{-1}\sum_{s=1}^{S}\boldsymbol{\beta}^{(s)}\).
Step B3. Compute the MCMC sample covariance:
\[
\hat{\Sigma}_{\text{MCMC}}^{(\beta)} = \frac{1}{S-1}\sum_{s=1}^{S}(\boldsymbol{\beta}^{(s)} - \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}})(\boldsymbol{\beta}^{(s)} - \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}})^\top.
\tag{6.8}\]
Step B4. Obtain the marginal sandwich target \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{sand,marg}}^{(\beta)}\) from the bm_sandwich object.
Step B5. Compute Cholesky factors:
\[
L_1^{(\beta)} = \operatorname{chol}(\hat{\Sigma}_{\text{MCMC}}^{(\beta)}), \quad L_2^{(\beta)} = \operatorname{chol}(\mathbf{V}_{\text{sand,marg}}^{(\beta)}).
\tag{6.9}\]
Step B6. Form the transformation matrix \(\mathbf{A}_\beta = L_2^{(\beta)} (L_1^{(\beta)})^{-1}\) and apply the affine transformation:
\[
\boldsymbol{\beta}^{*(s)} = \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} + \mathbf{A}_\beta(\boldsymbol{\beta}^{(s)} - \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}), \quad s = 1, \ldots, S.
\tag{6.10}\]


6.4.2 Implementation Details
R’s chol() returns the upper-triangular factor \(R\) satisfying \(R^\top R = M\). The lower-triangular factor needed for CCC is obtained via t(chol(M)). The inverse \(L_1^{-1}\) is computed via forwardsolve(L1, diag(K)), which exploits the triangular structure for \(O(K^2)\)-per-column numerical stability rather than general matrix inversion. The affine transformation is applied via vectorized BLAS operations:
draws_centered <- sweep(draws_beta, 2L, center)         # S x K
draws_star <- sweep(draws_centered %*% t(A_beta),
                    2L, -center)                         # S x K
The MCMC covariance receives a nearPD fallback via .safe_chol() if it is not positive definite (possible due to floating-point noise). The sandwich target \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{sand,marg}}\) never receives nearPD treatment: a non-PD sandwich indicates structural issues (\(J \leq K\)) that should trigger a beta-block skip, not artificial repair.


6.4.3 Diagnostics
Two diagnostics quantify the calibration magnitude:

Frobenius norm \(\|\mathbf{A}_\beta - \mathbf{I}_K\|_F\): measures overall departure from identity. Values below 0.1 indicate negligible calibration; values above 0.5 indicate substantial model misspecification.
Per-parameter SD ratios \(r_k = \sqrt{[\mathbf{V}_{\text{sand,marg}}]_{kk} / [\hat{\Sigma}_{\text{MCMC}}]_{kk}}\): quantify marginal rescaling for each coefficient.

The computational cost of the entire beta-block is \(O(SK^2 + K^3)\). With typical values \(S = 4000\) and \(K \leq 10\), this amounts to sub-millisecond computation.

title: 6  Cholesky Calibration Correction
text:
6.5 Theta-Block CCC Algorithm

6.5.1 Diagonal Rescaling
Because the conditional sandwich target \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{sand,cond}}^{(\theta)}\) is itself diagonal—reflecting the conditional independence of \(\theta_j\) given \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) and \(\tau^2\) in the working model—the theta-block CCC reduces to \(J\) independent scalar rescalings, each an instance of Equation 6.3:
\[
\theta_j^{*(s)} = \hat{\theta}_j + r_j(\theta_j^{(s)} - \hat{\theta}_j), \quad r_j = \sqrt{\frac{V_{\text{sand,cond}}^{(\theta_j)}}{\hat{\Sigma}_{\text{MCMC},jj}^{(\theta)}}}, \quad j = 1, \ldots, J.
\tag{6.11}\]
The rescaling factor \(r_j\) admits the same interpretation as the scalar case: \(r_j > 1\) means the posterior is too narrow for study \(j\) (CCC widens); \(r_j < 1\) means too wide (CCC narrows); \(r_j = 1\) means no correction needed.
The implementation is fully vectorized via sweep() operations, requiring no loops or matrix factorizations:
draws_centered <- sweep(draws_theta, 2L, center)                   # S x J
draws_star_centered <- sweep(draws_centered, 2L, r_theta, `*`)     # S x J
draws_star <- sweep(draws_star_centered, 2L, -center)              # S x J
Computational cost is \(O(SJ)\) — a single pass over the \(S \times J\) draw matrix.


6.5.2 Off-Diagonal Covariance
Diagonal rescaling calibrates each \(\operatorname{Var}(\theta_j^*)\) individually but does not target the off-diagonal covariances \(\operatorname{Cov}(\theta_j^*, \theta_{j'}^*)\). After CCC:
\[
\operatorname{Cov}(\theta_j^*, \theta_{j'}^*) = r_j \, r_{j'} \cdot \hat{\Sigma}_{\text{MCMC},jj'}^{(\theta)}.
\]
This is a consequence of the diagonal target design, not a defect. The conditional sandwich target \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{CCC}}^{(\theta)}\) is itself diagonal because \(\theta_j \perp \theta_{j'} \mid \boldsymbol{\beta}, \tau^2\) under the working model, so no off-diagonal sandwich target exists. The off-diagonal covariances reflect the posterior’s borrowing-of-strength structure, scaled by the CCC factors.
The \(r_j\) distribution provides a diagnostic summary of study-level calibration heterogeneity: if all \(r_j \approx 1\), the working model is well-specified; if \(r_j \approx c \neq 1\) uniformly, there is systematic miscalibration in the variance component; if \(r_j\) values are highly heterogeneous, study-level characteristics drive differential miscalibration.


6.5.3 Worked Example: Blockwise CCC (\(K=2\), \(J=3\))
A minimal numerical example makes the blockwise mechanics concrete. We use exact algebraic verification (Theorem 2 is a finite-sample identity, not an approximation).

# ── Inputs ──────────────────────────────────────────────
# Beta-block: K = 2
Sigma_MCMC_beta <- matrix(c(4, 1,
                             1, 2), nrow = 2)   # MCMC covariance
V_CR2_beta      <- matrix(c(9, 3,
                             3, 4), nrow = 2)   # CR2 sandwich target

# Theta-block: J = 3 (scalar variance pairs per study)
sigma2_MCMC_theta <- c(1.0, 0.5, 2.0)           # MCMC variances
V_sand_theta      <- c(1.5, 1.0, 1.0)           # conditional sandwich targets

# ── Beta-block: Cholesky CCC ───────────────────────────
L1 <- t(chol(Sigma_MCMC_beta))                   # lower-triangular
L2 <- t(chol(V_CR2_beta))
A_beta <- L2 %*% solve(L1)
cat("A_beta:\n"); print(round(A_beta, 4))

A_beta:


       [,1]   [,2]
[1,] 1.5000 0.0000
[2,] 0.1727 1.3093

# Theorem 2 verification: A Sigma A' == V_CR2 (exact)
V_check <- A_beta %*% Sigma_MCMC_beta %*% t(A_beta)
cat("\nA * Sigma_MCMC * A' =\n"); print(round(V_check, 10))


A * Sigma_MCMC * A' =


     [,1] [,2]
[1,]    9    3
[2,]    3    4

cat("V_CR2 target       =\n"); print(V_CR2_beta)

V_CR2 target       =


     [,1] [,2]
[1,]    9    3
[2,]    3    4

cat("Max error:", max(abs(V_check - V_CR2_beta)), "\n")

Max error: 4.440892e-16 

# ── Theta-block: diagonal rescaling ────────────────────
r_j <- sqrt(V_sand_theta / sigma2_MCMC_theta)
cat("\nTheta rescaling r_j:", round(r_j, 4), "\n")


Theta rescaling r_j: 1.2247 1.4142 0.7071 

cat("r_j^2 * sigma2_MCMC:", r_j^2 * sigma2_MCMC_theta,
    "== V_sand_theta:", V_sand_theta, "\n")

r_j^2 * sigma2_MCMC: 1.5 1 1 == V_sand_theta: 1.5 1 1 

# ── Full 5x5 blockwise target ──────────────────────────
V_CCC <- matrix(0, 5, 5)
V_CCC[1:2, 1:2] <- V_CR2_beta                   # beta-block (full 2x2)
diag(V_CCC)[3:5] <- V_sand_theta                 # theta-block (diagonal)
cat("\nFull blockwise CCC target V_CCC:\n"); print(V_CCC)


Full blockwise CCC target V_CCC:


     [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5]
[1,]    9    3  0.0    0    0
[2,]    3    4  0.0    0    0
[3,]    0    0  1.5    0    0
[4,]    0    0  0.0    1    0
[5,]    0    0  0.0    0    1


Three features of this example:

Beta-block uses full Cholesky, matching all four entries of \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{CR2}}^{(\beta)}\)—including the off-diagonal covariance—at machine precision (\(< 10^{-14}\)).
Theta-block uses scalar rescaling per study. Study 1 has \(r_1 = 1.225 > 1\) (posterior too narrow, CCC widens); study 3 has \(r_3 = 0.707 < 1\) (posterior too wide, CCC shrinks).
Cross-block entries in \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{CCC}}\) are zero by design (Decision D2). This does not imply that \(\operatorname{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\beta}^*, \boldsymbol{\theta}^*)\) vanishes in the calibrated draws: the block-independent transformations preserve whatever correlations exist in the original posterior through the shared draw index \(s\).

title: 6  Cholesky Calibration Correction
text:
6.6 CE vs. CHE Model Dispatch

6.6.1 CE Model: Blockwise Hybrid
For the CE (Correlated Effects) model, CCC applies the full blockwise hybrid: beta-block Cholesky CCC followed by theta-block diagonal rescaling. The theta draws \(\theta_j^{(s)}\) are explicitly sampled in the MCMC, and both blocks are calibrated independently.


6.6.2 CHE Model: Beta-Block Only
For the CHE (Correlated-Hierarchical Effects) model, the study-specific effects \(\theta_j\) are analytically integrated out of the marginal likelihood. The MCMC therefore does not produce theta draws. CCC applies only to the beta-block, with no theta-block.
The dispatch logic in ccc.bm_fit() is:
model_type <- x$model %||% "ce"

if (model_type == "che") {
  # Lightweight provenance check (fingerprint check cannot use
  # .extract_draws(x, "theta") because CHE has no theta draws)
  .assert_che_sandwich_matches_fit(x, sandwich_obj,
                                    arg_name = "sandwich_obj")
  return(.ccc_che(x, sandwich_obj, center, K, J))
}

# CE path: full blockwise hybrid
.assert_sandwich_matches_fit(x, sandwich_obj, arg_name = "sandwich_obj")
Inside .ccc_che(), the implementation:

Extracts beta draws only (\(S \times K\) matrix).
Applies the standard .ccc_beta_block() to the beta draws with the sandwich’s V_sand_marg as target.
Creates dummy theta draws (zeros) and dummy r_theta (zeros) for API compatibility, so that downstream methods (summary(), confint(), print()) can operate uniformly on bm_ccc objects regardless of model type.
Sets model = "che" on the returned bm_ccc object, enabling clean downstream CHE/CE dispatch.

The dummy theta values are quarantined: all downstream methods check the model field and suppress theta output for CHE objects. This design ensures a single bm_ccc class serves both model types without requiring separate class hierarchies.
The CHE provenance check uses a lightweight comparison of \(K\), \(J\), study IDs, and model type, rather than the full fingerprint check used for CE models. The full fingerprint requires .extract_draws(x, "theta"), which fails for CHE because theta is integrated out. This is a known design constraint documented in the v0.6.0 post-review (Issue #2).

title: 6  Cholesky Calibration Correction
text:
6.7 Three-Layer Calibration: CCC-t Intervals

6.7.1 The Three Layers
The CCC-t interval implements Three-Layer Calibration, combining three independent sources of frequentist robustness:

Layer 1 (Point Estimate): The Bayesian posterior mean, which under BvM conditions is asymptotically equivalent to the MLE.
Layer 2 (Standard Error): The CCC-calibrated posterior SD, which by Theorem 2 equals the CR2 sandwich standard error.
Layer 3 (Reference Distribution): The \(t(df_k)\) distribution with Satterthwaite degrees of freedom, accounting for the finite number of clusters (studies).

The CCC-t interval for coefficient \(\beta_k\) is:
\[
\hat{\beta}_k \;\pm\; t_{1-\alpha/2}(df_k) \times \operatorname{SD}_{\text{CCC}}(\beta_k),
\tag{6.12}\]
where \(\operatorname{SD}_{\text{CCC}}(\beta_k) = \operatorname{SD}(\beta_k^{*(1)}, \ldots, \beta_k^{*(S)})\) is the standard deviation of the CCC-calibrated draws, and \(df_k\) is the Satterthwaite degrees of freedom from the CR2 sandwich estimator.
The alternative CCC-q (quantile-based) interval uses quantiles of the calibrated draws directly:
\[
\left[\operatorname{quantile}_{\alpha/2}(\beta_k^{*(\cdot)}), \;\operatorname{quantile}_{1-\alpha/2}(\beta_k^{*(\cdot)})\right].
\tag{6.13}\]
CCC-q uses a normal reference distribution implicitly (posterior draws are approximately normal under BvM), while CCC-t uses a heavier-tailed \(t\) reference that accounts for sandwich estimation uncertainty.


6.7.2 Worked Numerical Example
Consider an intercept-only meta-analysis with \(K = 1\) fixed effect.
Scenario A: Large degrees of freedom (\(df = 47\)).
Suppose \(\hat{\beta} = 0.15\), \(\operatorname{SD}_{\text{CCC}} = 0.028\), and the Satterthwaite degrees of freedom are \(df = 47\) (from a meta-analysis with \(J = 50\) studies).

CCC-t critical value: \(t_{0.975}(47) = 2.012\)
CCC-t interval: \(0.15 \pm 2.012 \times 0.028 = (0.094, 0.206)\)
CCC-q critical value: \(z_{0.975} = 1.960\)
CCC-q interval: \(0.15 \pm 1.960 \times 0.028 = (0.095, 0.205)\)

The difference is small: CCC-t is 2.7% wider than CCC-q. At \(df = 47\), the \(t\) distribution is very close to the standard normal.
Scenario B: Small degrees of freedom (\(df = 6\)).
Now consider a meta-analysis with only \(J = 8\) studies and \(K = 2\) moderators, yielding \(df = 6\).

CCC-t critical value: \(t_{0.975}(6) = 2.447\)
CCC-t interval: \(0.15 \pm 2.447 \times 0.028 = (0.081, 0.219)\)
CCC-q critical value: \(z_{0.975} = 1.960\)
CCC-q interval: \(0.15 \pm 1.960 \times 0.028 = (0.095, 0.205)\)

The difference is now substantial: CCC-t is 24.9% wider than CCC-q. The half-width grows from 0.055 (CCC-q) to 0.069 (CCC-t), reflecting the heavier tails of \(t(6)\). This is precisely the regime where Layer 3 matters most — few studies means high sandwich estimation uncertainty, and the \(t\) reference distribution prevents under-coverage.
Simulation results in Section 14.2 confirm this pattern empirically: CCC-q shows under-coverage (91–94%) at \(J \leq 20\), while CCC-t maintains nominal 95% coverage (94.5–95.3%) across all conditions in sim3.


6.7.3 Implementation in confint.bm_ccc()
The confint() method for bm_ccc objects accepts a df argument with three options:

"satterthwaite" (default): Per-coefficient Satterthwaite df from the CR2 sandwich. Falls back to "naive" if unavailable.
"naive": Scalar \(df = J - K\) for all coefficients.
"normal": Quantile-based (CCC-q), equivalent to \(df = \infty\).

For beta parameters, the interval is computed as:
est_i <- mean(all_draws[, i])
sd_i  <- sd(all_draws[, i])
crit_i <- qt(1 - alpha / 2, df = nu_i)
ci <- c(est_i - crit_i * sd_i, est_i + crit_i * sd_i)
Theta-block intervals are always quantile-based, regardless of the df argument, because study-specific effects do not have meaningful Satterthwaite degrees of freedom.

\(J = K\) and PD

6.8.2 Boundary Posterior (\(\tau \approx 0\))
When \(\tau^2\) concentrates near zero, the theta-block MCMC variance \(\hat{\Sigma}_{\text{MCMC},jj}^{(\theta)} = O(\tau^2)\), while the conditional sandwich variance \(V_{\text{sand,cond}}^{(\theta_j)} = O(\tau^4)\). The rescaling factor therefore \(r_j = O(\tau) \to 0\), shrinking theta draws toward their center. This is not a failure mode: it reflects the sandwich’s assessment that \(\theta_j\) has very little conditional variability. CCC is still applied (always-on design), and .check_tau_boundary() issues a diagnostic warning when more than 10% of tau draws fall below 0.01. No clamping of \(r_j\) values is performed, as clamping would break the exact variance matching of Theorem 2.


6.8.3 Positive Definiteness Requirements
The beta-block CCC requires both \(\hat{\Sigma}_{\text{MCMC}}^{(\beta)}\) and \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{sand,marg}}^{(\beta)}\) to be positive definite. The implementation handles four cases:

\(J < K\) (structural singularity): The marginal sandwich meat has rank at most \(J < K\), so \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{sand,marg}}\) is singular. Beta-block CCC is unconditionally skipped.
\(J = K\) and sandwich not PD: Skipped.
\(J = K\) and sandwich PD: Proceeds with a zero-excess-degrees-of-freedom warning (the sandwich is PD but barely so).
\(J > K\) and sandwich not PD: Skipped (near-collinearity or numerical issue).

For the MCMC covariance, .safe_chol() applies a nearPD fallback (preserving diagonal) if floating-point noise causes marginal non-PD. For the sandwich, nearPD is never applied.
In all skip cases, the theta-block CCC proceeds normally — theta-block rescaling has no rank requirement.


6.8.4 Over-Dispersed Posterior (\(r_j < 1\))
When \(r_j < 1\), the MCMC posterior is wider than the sandwich target, and CCC narrows the credible interval. This is valid calibration: the working model may produce an overly conservative posterior, and CCC corrects it. bayesRVE applies CCC unconditionally for \(r_j\) in any range, eliminating the DER-based gating present in earlier implementations.

title: 6  Cholesky Calibration Correction
