6.9.2 Interpretation
The CCC-calibrated Bayesian framework preserves what is useful about both paradigms:

From the Bayesian side: The posterior mean as the point estimate, the full posterior distribution for visualization and diagnostics, and the ability to incorporate prior information.
From the frequentist side: Sandwich-robust standard errors that do not depend on correct specification of the within-study correlation structure, small-sample corrections via CR2, and appropriate degrees of freedom via Satterthwaite.

CCC does not choose between Bayesian and frequentist inference. It recalibrates the Bayesian posterior so that its spread matches the frequentist sandwich standard error, then uses the frequentist \(t\)-distribution for interval construction. The result is a posterior distribution whose marginal credible intervals have correct frequentist coverage under working-model misspecification.
This hybrid property is not unconditional: it requires the four coverage conditions (BvM regularity, sandwich consistency, positive definiteness, and adequate degrees of freedom) detailed in Chapter 8. When these conditions are satisfied, CCC-t delivers coverage rates empirically indistinguishable from the gold-standard CR2 intervals, as demonstrated in Section 14.2. The sim3 comprehensive simulation (24 conditions, 11,600 fits) confirms CCC-t coverage of 94.5–95.3% across all conditions, matching CR2 coverage of 94.8–95.3%.






Neyman, J., & Scott, E. L. (1948). Consistent estimates based on partially consistent observations. Econometrica, 16(1), 1–32. https://doi.org/10.2307/1914288

title: 7  Operating Envelope and Boundary Behavior
text:
8 Operating Envelope and Boundary Behavior
The Cholesky Calibration Correction (CCC) framework, as developed in Section 6.1, produces exact covariance matching under the assumptions stated in Theorem 2. However, these guarantees rest on regularity conditions that can fail in small or degenerate meta-analytic datasets. This chapter formally states the conditions required for valid CCC inference, delineates the operating envelope within which bayesRVE is designed to function reliably, taxonomizes the boundary cases that arise when these conditions are violated, and documents the fallback semantics that govern the software’s behavior at the margins.
crumbs: 7  Operating Envelope and Boundary Behavior

title: 7  Operating Envelope and Boundary Behavior
text:
8.1 Coverage Conditions and Regularity Assumptions
The Three-Layer Calibration framework (Layer 1: posterior mean centering; Layer 2: CR2 sandwich target; Layer 3: Satterthwaite degrees of freedom) provides frequentist-calibrated intervals from Bayesian posterior draws. The validity of this procedure rests on four regularity conditions.
Condition 1 (Posterior Approximate Normality). The marginal posterior for \(\beta\) is approximately multivariate normal: \[
p(\beta \mid y) \approx N_K\!\bigl(\hat\beta,\; \Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}}\bigr),
\] where \(\hat\beta\) is the posterior mean and \(\Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}} = \mathrm{Cov}(\beta \mid y)\). This condition holds by Bayesian CLT arguments when the number of studies \(J\) is large relative to \(K\), the prior is not overly informative, and the likelihood is regular. The CCC affine transformation preserves any departures from normality (it maps quantiles linearly), but the CCC-\(t\) interval construction in Layer 3 presumes approximate normality for the \(t\)-distribution pivotal argument to hold.
Condition 2 (\(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) Positive Definite). The bias-corrected cluster-robust sandwich variance \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) is positive definite. Since \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) is assembled from \(J\) outer-product contributions (see Chapter 6), this requires:

\(J > K\) (more clusters than parameters, so the meat matrix has rank \(\geq K\));
No perfect multicollinearity in the between-study design matrix;
Finite and non-degenerate marginal variances \(v_j = \bar{s}^2_j / n_j + \tau^2 > 0\) for all \(j\).

When \(J \leq K\), the meat matrix is rank-deficient, and \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) is necessarily singular. The Cholesky factorization \(L_2 = \mathrm{chol}(V_{\mathrm{CR2}})\) required for the CCC transformation does not exist, and the beta-block CCC must be skipped.
Condition 3 (\(\Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}}\) Positive Definite). The MCMC posterior covariance \(\Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}}\) is positive definite. This is the sample covariance of \(S\) posterior draws: \[
\Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}} = \frac{1}{S-1}\sum_{s=1}^{S} (\beta^{(s)} - \hat\beta)(\beta^{(s)} - \hat\beta)^\top.
\] The matrix \(\Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}}\) has rank \(\min(S - 1, K)\), so it is PD whenever \(S > K\). With typical MCMC sample sizes (\(S = 4000\) in bayesRVE defaults), this condition is virtually always satisfied. It can fail with pathologically few draws or when the sampler becomes stuck in a mode. The Cholesky factorization \(L_1 = \mathrm{chol}(\Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}})\) required for CCC fails when this condition is violated; bayesRVE applies a nearPD correction as a fallback (see Section 8.4).
Condition 4 (Consistent Centering). The posterior mean \(\hat\beta\) converges to the true \(\beta_0\) as \(J \to \infty\): \[
\hat\beta \xrightarrow{p} \beta_0 \quad \text{as } J \to \infty.
\] This is the standard Bayesian consistency requirement under model misspecification: even when the assumed model (CE or CHE) is not the true data-generating process, the posterior mean of \(\beta\) converges to the pseudo-true value that the sandwich variance targets. The CCC transformation centers at \(\hat\beta\) and the CR2 sandwich estimates the variance of \(\hat\beta\) around this limit, so consistency of both components is required for the CCC-\(t\) interval to achieve nominal coverage.
These four conditions are grounded in the Calibrated Bayes framework of Little (2012), which establishes that Bayesian procedures can achieve frequentist coverage properties when the posterior concentrates appropriately. The CR2 correction and Satterthwaite degrees of freedom follow Pustejovsky & Tipton (2018), ensuring finite-sample accuracy of the variance target even when \(J\) is moderate.
crumbs: 7  Operating Envelope and Boundary Behavior

title: 7  Operating Envelope and Boundary Behavior
text:
8.2 Exact versus Asymptotic Guarantees
A critical distinction must be maintained between what the CCC transformation guarantees exactly and what it guarantees only asymptotically.
The CCC transformation is exact. Theorem 2 (Section 6.1) establishes that for any number of posterior draws \(S\), the CCC-calibrated draws \(\beta^{*(s)} = \hat\beta + A(\beta^{(s)} - \hat\beta)\) satisfy: \[
\mathrm{Cov}_S(\boldsymbol{\beta}^*) = \mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{CR2}}^{(\beta)},
\] where \(\mathrm{Cov}_S\) denotes the sample covariance across the \(S\) draws. This is not an asymptotic statement; it holds exactly for any \(S \geq K + 1\) (so that \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}_{\mathrm{MCMC}}^{(\beta)}\) is nonsingular). The proof follows from the matrix identity \(\mathbf{L}_2 \mathbf{L}_1^{-1} \hat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}_{\mathrm{MCMC}}^{(\beta)} \mathbf{L}_1^{-\top} \mathbf{L}_2^\top = \mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{CR2}}^{(\beta)}\).
The CCC-\(t\) interval is asymptotic. The Three-Layer Calibration interval for coefficient \(\beta_k\) takes the form: \[
\hat\beta_k \;\pm\; t_{\nu_k,\, 1-\alpha/2} \times \mathrm{SD}_{\mathrm{CCC}}(\beta_k^*),
\] where \(\nu_k\) is the Satterthwaite degrees of freedom for the \(k\)-th coefficient and \(\mathrm{SD}_{\mathrm{CCC}}\) is the standard deviation of the calibrated draws. This interval achieves nominal coverage only in the limit as \(J \to \infty\) (where \(\nu_k \to \infty\) and the \(t\)-distribution converges to the normal). For finite \(J\), the coverage guarantee is approximate, with the quality of approximation depending on:

The accuracy of the Satterthwaite moment-matching approximation for \(\nu_k\) (Satterthwaite, 1946);
The validity of the normal approximation to the posterior (Condition 1);
The quality of the plug-in variance component estimates (\(\tau^2\), \(\omega^2\)) used in constructing \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\).

The Satterthwaite degrees of freedom \(\nu_k\) are computed by matching the first two moments of a quadratic form to a scaled \(\chi^2\) distribution (see Chapter 6 for the computational formula). This is itself an approximation: the exact sampling distribution of the sandwich variance estimator is unknown in closed form, and the moment-matching procedure is most accurate when the underlying scores are approximately normal. As documented in the simulation study (Section 14.2), the CCC-\(t\) interval achieves 94.5–95.3% empirical coverage across the 24 conditions in sim3, validating the approximation quality for the configurations studied.
In summary: the covariance calibration is an algebraic identity; the inferential coverage is a statistical approximation that improves with \(J\).
crumbs: 7  Operating Envelope and Boundary Behavior

title: 7  Operating Envelope and Boundary Behavior
text:
8.3 Operating Envelope: \(J_{\min}\)
The operating envelope defines the minimum number of studies \(J\) required for the full CCC pipeline to function. The threshold is governed by Decision D7:
\[
J_{\min} = \max(8,\; 2K + 2),
\]
where \(K\) is the number of fixed-effect coefficients (including the intercept).

8.3.1 Rationale for the \(2K + 2\) Component
The \(2K + 2\) threshold is a conservative heuristic motivated by rank requirements, residual degrees of freedom, and the small-cluster CRVE literature. The meat matrix \(M = \sum_{j=1}^J s_j s_j^\top\) is a sum of \(J\) rank-one outer products, giving \(\mathrm{rank}(M) \leq J\). For \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) to be full-rank (\(K \times K\)), we need \(J \geq K\). However, the CR2 bias correction involves the adjustment matrices \(A_j = (I - H_{jj})^{-1/2}\), where \(H_{jj}\) is the leverage block for study \(j\). The aggregate leverage satisfies \(\sum_j \mathrm{tr}(H_{jj}) = K\), so the effective residual degrees of freedom are \(J - K\). Setting \(J - K \geq K + 2\) yields \(J \geq 2K + 2\), which provides:

\(K\) degrees of freedom consumed by the fixed effects;
\(K\) degrees of freedom for the \(K \times K\) sandwich to be estimable;
\(2\) additional degrees of freedom as a stability margin for the CR2 bias correction.

This requirement controls the average study leverage: since \(\sum_j \mathrm{tr}(H_{jj}) = K\), we have \(\bar{h} = K/J \leq K/(2K+2) < 1/2\) when \(J \geq 2K+2\). Note, however, that the bound applies to the average, not to any individual study. With highly unbalanced study sizes, a single study can still have \(\mathrm{tr}(H_{jj})\) near or above \(1/2\), making its CR2 adjustment \(A_j\) ill-conditioned. The software guards against this at runtime: .compute_hat_blocks() issues a warning when the maximum per-study leverage exceeds \(0.5\) (see Section 8.4).


8.3.2 The Floor of 8
The floor \(J_{\min} \geq 8\) is imposed regardless of \(K\) and reflects simulation evidence from Tipton (2015), who demonstrated that cluster-robust inference with fewer than approximately 8–10 clusters exhibits substantial coverage distortion even for intercept-only models (\(K = 1\)). The value 8 is chosen as a pragmatic lower bound below which the asymptotic sandwich theory provides unreliable guidance.


8.3.3 \(J_{\min}\) for Common Configurations



Table 8.1: \(J_{\min}\) values as a function of the number of fixed-effect coefficients \(K\). The floor of 8 binds for \(K \leq 3\).





\(K\) (coefficients)
\(2K + 2\)

8.3.4 Worked Example
Consider a researcher with \(J = 6\) studies estimating a model with an intercept and two categorical moderators (\(K = 3\)).
Step 1. Compute \(J_{\min} = \max(8, 2 \times 3 + 2) = 8\).
Step 2. Since \(J = 6 < 8 = J_{\min}\), the operating envelope is violated.
Step 3. bayesRVE response:

sandwich() sets fallback = TRUE, fallback_reason = "J_too_small"
ccc() skips beta-block CCC; method = "ccc_partial"
confint() returns quantile-based intervals for \(\beta\)

Step 4. User options (in order of preference):

Expand the literature search to bring \(J \geq 8\).
Reduce model complexity (e.g., drop a moderator to \(K = 2\)); but note the floor of 8 still binds.
Report raw Bayesian credible intervals with a note that sandwich calibration was not applied.



8.3.5 Behavior Below \(J_{\min}\)
When \(J < J_{\min}\), the sandwich() function sets fallback = TRUE and emits an informational message. The downstream consequences are:

The beta-block sandwich \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) is still computed (and stored in V_sand_marg), but it is flagged as unreliable;
The ccc() function detects sandwich_obj$fallback == TRUE and skips the beta-block Cholesky transformation, returning the original (uncalibrated) posterior draws for \(\beta\);
For CE models, the theta-block diagonal rescaling proceeds normally (it depends on per-study conditional sandwich variances, which do not require \(J > J_{\min}\));
The confint() method forces df = "normal" (quantile-based intervals), since the CCC-\(t\) infrastructure requires a fully calibrated beta block.

The method field in the resulting bm_ccc object is set to "ccc_partial", indicating that the theta-block was calibrated but the beta-block was not.






NoteSmall-\(J\) Alternatives Beyond bayesRVE



When the operating envelope cannot be met, alternatives include the wild cluster bootstrap (Cameron, Gelbach, and Miller, 2008), the jackknife cluster-robust estimator (MacKinnon, Nielsen, and Webb, 2023), and randomization inference under the sharp null. These methods relax the large-\(J\) asymptotics that CCC inherits from the sandwich estimator, but are not currently implemented in bayesRVE.
crumbs: 7  Operating Envelope and Boundary Behavior

title: 7  Operating Envelope and Boundary Behavior
text:
8.4 Boundary Cases and PD Failures
Even when \(J \geq J_{\min}\), boundary conditions can cause the CCC pipeline to degrade. This section catalogs the principal failure modes and the software’s response to each.

8.4.1 Case 1: \(J \leq K\) (Structural Singularity)
When \(J \leq K\), the between-study design matrix has more columns than rows, and the meat matrix is structurally rank-deficient. The sandwich \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) is singular regardless of the data values. The ccc() function detects this condition and unconditionally skips the beta-block CCC:

For \(J < K\): a warning is emitted noting structural singularity, and the method is set to "fallback_raw".
For \(J = K\): the sandwich may or may not be PD depending on the specific data configuration. If PD, ccc() proceeds with a zero-excess-df warning. If not PD, the beta-block is skipped.

In either case, the theta-block (CE models only) proceeds normally, as conditional per-study variances require only \(n_j \geq 1\).


8.4.2 Case 2: \(\tau^2 \to 0\) (Degenerate Heterogeneity)
As \(\tau^2 \to 0\), the between-study variance vanishes and the marginal weights \(W_j \to (1/\bar{s}^2_j) I_{n_j}\) reduce to the inverse sampling variance. In this limit:

The hat matrix converges to the standard OLS leverage, and high-leverage studies can produce \(H_{jj}\) with eigenvalues near 1;
The CR2 adjustment \((I - H_{jj})^{-1/2}\) becomes ill-conditioned for high-leverage clusters;
Satterthwaite degrees of freedom may collapse to very small values, producing extremely wide CCC-\(t\) intervals.

The software monitors this boundary through the tau_diag component returned by .check_tau_boundary(), which flags when a large fraction of posterior draws have \(\tau\) near zero. While no automatic fallback is triggered (the user may genuinely have a near-homogeneous meta-analysis), the summary output includes a diagnostic warning when \(\tau\) boundary behavior is detected.


8.4.3 Case 3: \(\Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}}\) Not Positive Definite
The MCMC posterior covariance \(\Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}}\) can fail to be PD when the sampler generates too few effective draws or becomes stuck. The .ccc_beta_block() function handles this via .safe_chol(), which:

Attempts direct Cholesky decomposition of \(\Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}}\);
On failure, applies Matrix::nearPD() to project to the nearest PD matrix;
Retries the Cholesky decomposition on the projected matrix;
Aborts only if the projected matrix also fails decomposition.

This nearPD correction is applied only to \(\Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}}\) (the source covariance), never to \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) (the target). Applying nearPD to the sandwich target would alter the frequentist calibration target and defeat the purpose of the CCC correction. This asymmetry is a deliberate design decision documented in the codebase.


8.4.4 Case 4: \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) Not Positive Definite
When \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) fails the PD check (.is_pd() with tolerance \(10^{-8}\)), the Cholesky factorization \(L_2 = \mathrm{chol}(V_{\mathrm{CR2}})\) cannot proceed. Unlike \(\Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}}\), no nearPD correction is applied. Instead, the beta-block CCC is skipped entirely:

The sandwich() function sets diagnostics$beta_pd = FALSE;
The ccc() function detects !beta_pd and returns uncalibrated beta draws;
The method field is set to "fallback_raw".

This can occur when numerical cancellation in the sandwich computation produces small negative eigenvalues (typically \(|{\lambda_{\min}}| < 10^{-8}\)). It is most likely when \(J\) is close to \(K\), the study sizes are highly unbalanced, or the design matrix is near-collinear.


8.4.5 Case 5: Singular CHE Hessian
For the CHE model, the posterior Hessian \(H\) is \((K+2) \times (K+2)\), incorporating both \(\tau^2\) and \(\omega^2\) components. When this Hessian is singular (or has negative eigenvalues due to numerical issues), the .build_che_hessian() function applies a nearPD correction with a warning. If the Hessian is truly singular, MASS::ginv() (Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse) is used as a fallback for the sandwich inversion. Similarly, when the WLS bread matrix \(X^\top W X\) is singular, .compute_wls_bread() falls back to MASS::ginv().
crumbs: 7  Operating Envelope and Boundary Behavior

title: 7  Operating Envelope and Boundary Behavior
text:
8.5 Singletons and CHE-Specific Warnings

8.5.1 Singleton Non-Identifiability (Decision D22)
In the CHE model, the between-study variance \(\tau^2\) and the within-study heterogeneity \(\omega^2\) enter the marginal variance as \(v_{ij} = s^2_{ij} + \omega^2 + \tau^2\). For singleton studies (studies contributing exactly one effect size, \(n_j = 1\)), there is no within-study replication to distinguish \(\omega^2\) from \(\tau^2\). The variance components are confounded: any increase in \(\omega^2\) can be offset by a corresponding decrease in \(\tau^2\) without changing the marginal likelihood.
The bayesRVE implementation enforces Decision D22 with a two-tier warning system in .che_sandwich():

All singletons (\(n_j = 1\) for all \(j = 1, \ldots, J\)): A warning is emitted stating that \(\tau^2\) and \(\omega^2\) are not separately identified. Posterior separation relies entirely on the prior distributions. The CR2 sandwich benchmark from a frequentist multi-level analysis may diverge from the Bayesian posterior because the frequentist estimator (typically REML) faces the same identification problem but resolves it differently (often setting one component to zero).
Majority singletons (\(> 50\%\) of studies have \(n_j = 1\)): A warning is emitted stating that \(\tau^2\) and \(\omega^2\) may be weakly identified. The posterior is prior-sensitive, and a sensitivity analysis varying the half-Cauchy scale parameters is recommended.

These warnings do not trigger fallback behavior; the sandwich and CCC computations proceed normally. The warnings serve to alert the analyst that the variance decomposition is driven primarily by the prior rather than the data, and that the CCC calibration (which plugs in posterior mean variance components) inherits this prior sensitivity.


8.5.2 CHE Prior Sensitivity
When singletons dominate, the half-Cauchy prior scale for \(\omega\) Polson & Scott (2012) becomes a critical tuning parameter. A wide prior (e.g., \(\omega \sim \mathrm{half\text{-}Cauchy}(0, 1)\)) allows \(\omega^2\) to absorb substantial variance, potentially inflating the marginal weights and altering the sandwich. A narrow prior concentrates \(\omega\) near zero, effectively reducing the CHE to a CE model. The bm_sensitivity_rho() function provides systematic tools for assessing sensitivity to the working correlation \(\rho\); prior scale sensitivity for \(\omega\) requires refitting the model with different bm_prior() specifications and comparing the resulting CCC intervals.


8.5.3 CHE Theta Quarantine
In the CHE model, the study-specific effects \(\theta_j\) are analytically integrated out of the marginal likelihood (see Chapter 4). No posterior draws of \(\theta_j\) exist. For API compatibility with the CE pathway, the bm_ccc object returned by .ccc_che() contains dummy zero-valued theta draws. These dummy values are quarantined in all downstream methods:

coef(cal, type = "theta") returns zeros with a warning for CHE objects;
confint() applies CCC-\(t\) intervals only to the beta block; theta intervals are meaningless;
summary() and print() suppress theta output when model = "che".

The bm_ccc$model field (set to "che") enables clean dispatch in all downstream methods, preventing accidental interpretation of the dummy theta values as real posterior summaries.
crumbs: 7  Operating Envelope and Boundary Behavior

title: 7  Operating Envelope and Boundary Behavior
text:
8.6 Fallback Semantics
The bayesRVE pipeline implements a cascading fallback system across the three main functions: sandwich(), ccc(), and confint(). Table 8.2 provides a comprehensive decision table summarizing the behavior under each boundary condition.

"ccc"
Warning: not identified







8.6.1 The method Field
The bm_ccc object carries a method field that records the CCC path taken. This field governs downstream inference:

"ccc": Full CCC was applied. The beta-block Cholesky transformation matched the posterior covariance to \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\). The confint() method uses CCC-\(t\) intervals with Satterthwaite degrees of freedom by default.
"ccc_partial": The beta-block was skipped due to the \(J_{\min}\) operating envelope check (sandwich$fallback == TRUE), but the theta-block diagonal rescaling was applied (CE models only). Beta intervals fall back to quantile-based (CCC-\(q\)). The confint() method forces df = "normal" when method is "ccc_partial".
"fallback_raw": The beta-block was skipped for a reason other than the \(J_{\min}\) check—typically because \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) was not positive definite or \(J \leq K\). The confint() method forces df = "normal". The beta_skip_reason field provides a human-readable explanation.



8.6.2 Pipeline Flow
The fallback logic propagates through the pipeline as follows:

sandwich(x) evaluates \(J \geq J_{\min}\). If not, it sets fallback = TRUE and fallback_reason = "J_too_small". It computes the sandwich regardless (for diagnostic purposes) but flags the result.
ccc(x, sandwich_obj) inspects sandwich_obj$fallback. If TRUE, it skips the beta-block Cholesky and sets method = "ccc_partial". If FALSE, it inspects sandwich_obj$diagnostics$beta_pd. If FALSE, it skips the beta-block and sets method = "fallback_raw". Otherwise, it applies the full Cholesky CCC and sets method = "ccc".
confint(cal) inspects cal$method. If the method is "ccc_partial" or "fallback_raw", it forces df = "normal" with a warning, yielding quantile-based intervals from the (uncalibrated) posterior draws. If the method is "ccc", it proceeds with the default Satterthwaite degrees of freedom to produce CCC-\(t\) intervals.

This cascade ensures that no layer of the pipeline silently produces invalid results. Every degradation is accompanied by an explicit warning message and is encoded in the method field, enabling programmatic detection by downstream analysis scripts.


8.6.3 The Three Interval Types
The fallback semantics produce three distinct interval types, listed in decreasing order of inferential quality:

CCC-\(t\) (Three-Layer Calibration): Full Cholesky CCC with CR2 target and Satterthwaite degrees of freedom. Available when method = "ccc" and df = "satterthwaite". This is the recommended default.
CCC-\(t\) (naive df): Full Cholesky CCC with scalar \(\mathrm{df} = J - K\). Available when method = "ccc" and df = "naive". Less precise than Satterthwaite df but still uses the calibrated posterior.
CCC-\(q\) (quantile-based): Raw posterior quantiles, with or without partial CCC calibration. Used when method is "ccc_partial" or "fallback_raw". The confint() method computes the \(\alpha/2\) and \(1 - \alpha/2\) quantiles directly from the (possibly uncalibrated) posterior draws.

The simulation study in Section 14.2 demonstrates that CCC-\(t\) achieves near-nominal coverage (94.5–95.3%) while CCC-\(q\) exhibits undercoverage (91–94%) at moderate \(J\), confirming that the Satterthwaite adjustment in Layer 3 is essential for reliable inference.
crumbs: 7  Operating Envelope and Boundary Behavior

title: 7  Operating Envelope and Boundary Behavior
text:
8.7 Quick Reference: When to Worry






NoteSafe (Standard Operation)



\(J \geq 20\), \(K \leq 3\), no singletons or singletons a small minority (\(<25\%\)). Console output is silent (no warnings). cal$method == "ccc", sw$diagnostics$beta_pd == TRUE, all sw$df_source entries are "satterthwaite". Simulation evidence (sim3) shows CCC-\(t\) coverage between 94.5% and 95.3% under these conditions.








WarningCaution (Verify Results)



\(8 \leq J < 20\), especially with \(K \geq 3\). More than 50% singleton studies (CHE). Some df_source entries are "naive". `sw\(diagnostics\)H_condition > 10^6$. The pipeline completes but operates near its limits. Recommended: (1) compare CCC-calibrated intervals with raw posterior, (2) leave-one-out sensitivity, (3) prior sensitivity for CHE variance components.








ImportantInvestigate (Results May Be Unreliable)



\(J < 8\), or \(J \leq K\), or PD failure warnings from sandwich() or ccc(). cal$method == "fallback_raw" or cal$beta_skipped == TRUE. The frequentist calibration guarantee of CCC does not apply. Options: (1) simplify the model, (2) expand literature search, (3) report raw Bayesian credible intervals with appropriate caveats.








Gelman, A. (2006). Prior distributions for variance parameters in hierarchical models (comment on article by Browne and Draper). Bayesian Analysis, 1(3), 515–534. https://doi.org/10.1214/06-BA117A


Little, R. J. (2012). Calibrated Bayes, an alternative inferential paradigm for official statistics. Journal of Official Statistics, 28(3), 309–334.


Polson, N. G., & Scott, J. G. (2012). On the half-Cauchy prior for a global scale parameter. Bayesian Analysis, 7(4), 887–902. https://doi.org/10.1214/12-BA730


Pustejovsky, J. E., & Tipton, E. (2018). Small-sample methods for cluster-robust variance estimation and hypothesis testing in fixed effects models. Journal of Business & Economic Statistics, 36(4), 672–683. https://doi.org/10.1080/07350015.2016.1247004


Satterthwaite, F. E. (1946). An approximate distribution of estimates of variance components. Biometrics Bulletin, 2(6), 110–114. https://doi.org/10.2307/3002019


Tipton, E. (2015). Small sample adjustments for robust variance estimation with meta-regression. Psychological Methods, 20(3), 375–393. https://doi.org/10.1037/met0000011
crumbs: 7  Operating Envelope and Boundary Behavior

title: 8  Software Architecture
text:
8.1 Package Module Map
The bayesRVE package is organized around a three-stage pipeline—bm_fit, sandwich, ccc—with strict S3 class contracts at each boundary. Every object carries provenance metadata so that downstream stages can reject mismatched inputs at construction time rather than failing silently during computation. This chapter documents the module layout, object lifecycle, class contracts, provenance system, and dependency strategy.
The package comprises 16 R source files, 3 Stan model files, and supporting infrastructure. The files are grouped into four functional layers.
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

title: 8  Software Architecture
text:
8.1.1 Core Pipeline
These three files implement the statistical pipeline described in Section 6.1 and Chapter 6.







File
Role




fit.R
Entry point bm_fit() for Bayesian model fitting via CmdStan. Parses arguments, delegates to data-prep.R for formula/data processing, invokes Stan sampling, runs post-sampling diagnostics, and constructs the bm_fit S3 object. Also provides .extract_draws(), the sole interface for posterior draw access.


sandwich.R
Exports the sandwich() S3 generic and sandwich.bm_fit() method. Implements both CE and CHE marginal/conditional sandwich estimators, CR2 bias correction with Satterthwaite degrees of freedom, the fingerprint provenance system, and the .assemble_bm_sandwich() constructor.


ccc.R
Exports the ccc() S3 generic and ccc.bm_fit() method. Implements blockwise hybrid CCC for CE (beta-block Cholesky + theta-block diagonal rescaling) and beta-only CCC for CHE. Contains .ccc_beta_block(), .ccc_theta_block(), .ccc_che(), .verify_ccc(), and .assemble_bm_ccc().





8.1.2 Methods Layer
These files provide the standard R model-object interface and downstream analysis tools.







File
Role




methods.R
S3 methods for all three pipeline classes: nobs, coef, vcov, confint, summary, and print methods for bm_fit, bm_sandwich, and bm_ccc. Implements Three-Layer Calibration output (CCC-t intervals with Satterthwaite df) in summary.bm_ccc() and confint.bm_ccc().


diagnostics.R
Unified diagnostics via bm_diagnose() S3 generic dispatching on all three classes. Extracts MCMC convergence (Rhat, ESS), sandwich conditioning, and CCC calibration quality. Defensive design: every CmdStan interaction is wrapped in tryCatch.


sensitivity.R
Sensitivity analysis for the working correlation parameter rho. Sweeps a grid of rho values to show how the Design Effect Ratio (DER) decomposition changes, exploiting the fact that the posterior is fixed across rho.


plot.R
plot() S3 methods for bm_fit, bm_ccc, and bm_sensitivity_rho. Produces posterior density overlays, CCC dumbbell plots, R_k vs. df scatterplots, and DER sensitivity curves. Requires ggplot2 (Suggests).





8.1.3 Infrastructure
These files provide foundational services consumed by the pipeline.







File
Role




data-prep.R
Formula parsing (LHS y | se(v) syntax), data validation, design matrix construction, study-level aggregation, and Stan input assembly. All functions are internal (dot-prefixed).


validate.R
S3 object validators: .assert_bm_fit(), .assert_bm_sandwich(), .assert_bm_ccc(), plus the unified provenance dispatcher .assert_sandwich_matches_fit() (auto-routes CE vs. CHE) and its CHE-specific helper .assert_che_sandwich_matches_fit(). Every public method calls the relevant validator before proceeding.


priors.R
Distribution constructors (normal(), half_cauchy(), half_normal(), half_t()), the bm_prior() constructor, default_prior(), and Stan prior-to-data mapping. Implements the prior-via-data-block pattern.


stan-cache.R
Two-tier compilation cache: session-level (in-memory CmdStanModel objects in .bm_cache) and disk-level (compiled executables with MD5 integrity checks). Thread-safe via file-based locking. Exports bm_compile() and bm_clear_cache().


utils.R
Internal numerical helpers: .is_pd() (positive-definiteness check via eigendecomposition), .symmetrize() (floating-point symmetry enforcement), and related utilities.


data.R
Roxygen documentation for the example dataset bundled with the package.


bayesRVE-package.R
Package-level documentation, @importFrom declarations, and the .bm_cache environment initialization.


zzz.R
.onLoad() and .onAttach() hooks. Prints the package version on attach.





8.1.4 Interoperability







File
Role




metafor-interop.R
Bridges bayesRVE with the metafor (Viechtbauer, 2010) + clubSandwich (Pustejovsky & Tipton, 2022) ecosystem. compare_with_cr2() runs a side-by-side comparison of bayesRVE sandwich SEs vs. frequentist CR2 SEs. from_metafor() extracts data from a fitted rma.mv object into the format expected by bm_fit(). Both metafor and clubSandwich are Suggests-only.





8.1.5 Stan Models
Three Stan (Carpenter et al., 2017) programs reside in inst/stan/:








Model
File
Description




CE NCP
bm_ce_ncp.stan
Correlated-effects model with non-centered parameterization (default). Single \(\tau^2\) random effect, \(\rho_\text{work} = 0\).


CE CP
bm_ce_cp.stan
Correlated-effects model with centered parameterization. Selected when the data suggest low posterior concentration.


CHE NCP
bm_che_ncp.stan
Correlated-hierarchical-effects model. Two variance components (\(\tau^2 + \omega^2\)) with \(\theta_j\) analytically integrated out in the marginal likelihood.





8.1.6 Dependency Flow
The inter-file dependency structure follows the pipeline topology. Functions in downstream files call into upstream files but never the reverse.
bayesRVE-package.R    ← package-level imports, .bm_cache
     │
     ├── priors.R         ← distribution constructors, default_prior()
     ├── data-prep.R      ← formula parsing, Stan input assembly
     ├── stan-cache.R     ← model compilation, bm_compile()
     ├── validate.R       ← all assertion functions
     ├── utils.R          ← .is_pd(), .symmetrize()
     │
     ├── fit.R            ← calls data-prep, stan-cache, priors, validate, utils
     │    │
     │    ├── sandwich.R  ← calls fit.R (.extract_draws), validate, utils
     │    │    │
     │    │    └── ccc.R  ← calls fit.R, sandwich.R (assembler), validate, utils
     │    │
     │    └── methods.R   ← calls fit.R (.extract_draws), validate
     │         │
     │         ├── diagnostics.R   ← calls validate, methods.R helpers
     │         ├── sensitivity.R   ← calls sandwich.R, validate
     │         ├── plot.R          ← calls methods.R (summary), validate
     │         └── metafor-interop.R ← calls validate; metafor/clubSandwich optional
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

title: 8  Software Architecture
text:
8.2 Object Lifecycle
The bayesRVE pipeline produces three S3 objects in strict sequence. Each stage validates its inputs, performs computation, and emits an immutable output object consumed by the next stage.

8.2.1 Typical Usage
A complete analysis session consists of four calls:
# Stage 1: Fit the Bayesian meta-analytic model
fit <- bm_fit(yi | se(vi) ~ x1, data = dat, study = ~ studyid, model = "che")

# Stage 2: Compute CR2 sandwich variance with Satterthwaite df
sw  <- sandwich(fit, rho = 0.8, small_sample = "CR2")

# Stage 3: Apply Cholesky Calibration Correction
cal <- ccc(fit, sandwich_obj = sw)

# Stage 4: Extract calibrated inference
summary(cal)   # Three-Layer Calibration table with CCC SD, df, R_k
confint(cal)   # CCC-t confidence intervals
Each call produces an immutable S3 object whose fields are fully determined at construction time. No method modifies an object after creation. The following subsections trace the internal construction of each object.


8.2.2 Stage 1: Model Fitting
bm_fit(formula, data, study, model, prior, ...)
  │
  ├── .parse_formula(formula)           [data-prep.R]
  ├── .prepare_bm_data(formula, data)   [data-prep.R]
  ├── .build_stan_input(bm_data, prior) [data-prep.R]
  ├── bm_compile(model_name)            [stan-cache.R]
  ├── mod$sample(stan_input, ...)       [CmdStan]
  ├── .validate_cmdstan_fit(...)        [fit.R]
  │
  └── structure(..., class = "bm_fit")
Input requirements. A two-sided formula with y | se(v) ~ predictors syntax, a data frame containing the named columns, and a one-sided formula identifying the study grouping variable. The model argument selects CE or CHE; prior defaults to default_prior().
Output. A bm_fit object (named list with class attribute) containing the live CmdStanMCMC handle, the Stan input data, the parsed bm_data structure, and metadata (K, J, model name, parameterization, timing).
Validation. .assert_bm_fit() checks class inheritance and that all 11 required slots are non-NULL.


8.2.3 Stage 2: Sandwich Variance Estimation
sandwich(fit, rho = 0.8, small_sample = "CR2")
  │
  ├── .assert_bm_fit(fit)                [validate.R]
  ├── Dispatch: CE → .marginal_sandwich() + .conditional_sandwich()
  │             CHE → .che_sandwich()
  ├── CR2: .compute_cr2_adjustment()     [sandwich.R]
  ├── Satterthwaite: .satterthwaite_df() [sandwich.R]
  ├── .make_fit_fingerprint(fit, ...)    [sandwich.R]
  │
  └── structure(..., class = "bm_sandwich")
Input requirements. A validated bm_fit object. The rho parameter is the assumed within-study working correlation; small_sample selects the bias correction method.
Output. A bm_sandwich object containing the corrected variance matrices, raw and CR2-adjusted variants, the MCMC posterior covariance, scoring matrices, Satterthwaite degrees of freedom, and a cryptographic fingerprint linking this sandwich to its parent fit.
Validation. .assert_bm_sandwich() checks class inheritance, 9 always-required slots, and (when CR2 fields are present) type/dimension constraints on 6 additional fields.


8.2.4 Stage 3: Cholesky Calibration Correction
ccc(fit, sandwich_obj = NULL, rho = 0.8, center = "posterior_mean")
  │
  ├── .assert_bm_fit(fit)                          [validate.R]
  ├── Provenance: .assert_sandwich_matches_fit()    [validate.R]
  │               (auto-dispatches CE full hash / CHE data hash)
  ├── Dispatch: CE  → .ccc_beta_block() + .ccc_theta_block()
  │             CHE → .ccc_che()
  ├── .verify_ccc(...)                              [ccc.R]
  │
  └── structure(..., class = "bm_ccc")
Input requirements. A validated bm_fit and an optional bm_sandwich (computed internally if not supplied). The provenance check ensures the sandwich was computed from this exact fit snapshot.
Output. A bm_ccc object containing calibrated and original posterior draws, the CCC transformation matrix, per-parameter rescaling ratios, verification diagnostics, and the embedded sandwich for downstream methods.
Validation. .assert_bm_ccc() checks class inheritance, 10 required slots, and (when present) that the method field takes one of three valid values.


8.2.5 Downstream Consumption
The bm_ccc object feeds into the standard R model interface:
summary(cal)   → Three-Layer Calibration table (CCC-t)
confint(cal)   → CCC-t confidence intervals (Satterthwaite df)
coef(cal)      → CCC posterior means (beta, theta, or all)
vcov(cal)      → CCC posterior covariance (K x K beta-block)
plot(cal)      → Posterior overlay, dumbbell, R_k plots
bm_diagnose(cal) → CCC calibration diagnostics
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

Mathematical invariants. Centering preservation (Theorem 1, Section 6.1): \(\bar\beta^* = \bar\beta\) (CCC preserves the posterior mean). Exact covariance matching (Theorem 2, Section 6.1): \(\text{Cov}(\beta^*_{1:K}) = V_{\text{sand,marg}}\) exactly for any \(S\). Frobenius diagnostic: \(\|A_\beta - I_K\|_F\) quantifies calibration magnitude; values near zero indicate the posterior already approximates the sandwich. \(R_k\) interpretation: \(R_k = \text{SD}(\beta_k^*) / \text{SD}(\beta_k)\) gives per-coefficient variance inflation (\(R_k > 1\)) or deflation (\(R_k < 1\)).
CHE-specific invariants. When model = "che", the theta columns of draws_star and draws_orig are zero matrices (dummy compatibility layer), r_theta is a zero vector, and tau_diag is NULL. The model field enables downstream methods to suppress theta-related output and select the appropriate provenance check.

title: 8  Software Architecture
text:
8.4 Provenance and Compatibility Checks
A recurring failure mode in multi-stage pipelines is passing an object from one fit into a function expecting another. For example, fitting two models on different subsets and accidentally pairing a sandwich from model A with the draws from model B would produce silently wrong CCC output. The provenance system prevents this.

8.4.1 The Fingerprint
Every bm_sandwich object carries a fingerprint list created by .make_fit_fingerprint() at construction time. The fingerprint contains:

Structural metadata: K (integer), J (integer), study_ids (character vector).
Version tag: integer version number (currently 2) for forward compatibility.
Exact-match hash: an MD5 digest computed over a canonical list that includes the data (yi, vi, X), model specification (model_name, param, prior), parameter labels, the posterior center vector, and the MCMC covariance matrix Sigma_MCMC.

The hash captures both the data provenance and the MCMC realization. Two fits on identical data with different random seeds produce different hashes because their posterior draws (and hence Sigma_MCMC and posterior_center) differ.


8.4.2 CE Provenance: .assert_sandwich_matches_fit()
.assert_sandwich_matches_fit() is the unified provenance dispatcher. It auto-routes to the CE or CHE check path based on fit$model. For CE models, it performs the full-strength provenance check. The check proceeds in four stages:

Class validation. Both fit and sandwich_obj pass their respective .assert_bm_*() validators.
Dimension check. fingerprint$K and fingerprint$J must match fit$K and fit$J exactly (as integers).
Study ID check. fingerprint$study_ids must be identical (as character vectors) to fit$bm_data$study_ids.
Hash check. The sandwich’s stored fit_hash is compared against a freshly computed hash from the current fit. This recomputation calls .extract_draws(fit, "beta") and .extract_draws(fit, "theta") to rebuild the canonical list.

If any stage fails, cli_abort() produces a diagnostic message directing the user to recompute the sandwich.


8.4.3 CHE Provenance: .assert_che_sandwich_matches_fit()
The full CE provenance hash cannot be used for CHE models because .make_fit_fingerprint() internally calls .extract_draws(x, "theta"), which returns a zero matrix for CHE (theta is analytically integrated out in the marginal likelihood). A hash that includes a zero matrix would match any CHE fit regardless of data, defeating the purpose.
The CHE-specific check (called automatically by the unified dispatcher) uses a data_hash—an MD5 digest over 9 fields (K, J, study_ids, yi, vi, X, model_name, param, prior)—instead of the full posterior-state hash:

Class validation. Same as CE.
Dimension check. Same as CE.
Study ID check. Same as CE.
Data-hash check. The sandwich’s stored data_hash is compared against a freshly computed hash from the current fit via .make_data_hash(). This catches dataset swaps and model-specification changes without requiring posterior draws.

This provides full data-level and model-specification provenance without requiring theta draws. The trade-off is that two CHE fits on identical data with different seeds produce the same data_hash and are not distinguished by this check, because the hash excludes posterior draws (Sigma_MCMC, posterior_center). In practice this is acceptable because the CHE CCC path is beta-only. Legacy sandwiches created before the data_hash field was introduced receive a warning (not an error) and fall back to the structural checks (stages 1–3).


8.4.4 Check Sequence in ccc.bm_fit()
When ccc() is called with a user-supplied sandwich_obj, the following sequence executes before any computation:
ccc(fit, sandwich_obj, ...)
  │
  ├── .assert_bm_fit(fit)
  ├── .assert_bm_sandwich(sandwich_obj)
  │
  ├── .assert_sandwich_matches_fit(fit, sandwich_obj)
  │     (auto-dispatches: CE → full fingerprint hash,
  │                        CHE → data hash)
  │
  └── [proceed to CCC computation]
When sandwich_obj = NULL (the default), ccc() calls sandwich(fit, rho) internally, and the resulting object is guaranteed to match because it was just constructed from the same fit. In this common case, the provenance check is effectively a no-op but is still executed for defense in depth.


8.4.5 Failure Examples
The provenance system produces informative errors when the user passes a mismatched sandwich:
# Two models with different specifications
fit1 <- bm_fit(yi | se(vi) ~ x1, data = dat, study = ~ studyid, model = "ce")
fit2 <- bm_fit(yi | se(vi) ~ x1 + x2, data = dat, study = ~ studyid, model = "ce")

sw2 <- sandwich(fit2, rho = 0.8)
ccc(fit1, sandwich_obj = sw2)
#> Error in `ccc()`:
#> ! Sandwich dimensions (K=3, J=20) do not match the supplied bm_fit
#>   object (K=2, J=20).
#> i Recompute via `sandwich(fit)` or pass `sandwich_obj = NULL`.
When dimensions match but the fits differ (same model, different MCMC run):
fit_a <- bm_fit(yi | se(vi) ~ x1, data = dat, study = ~ studyid, model = "ce")
fit_b <- bm_fit(yi | se(vi) ~ x1, data = dat, study = ~ studyid, model = "ce")

sw_a <- sandwich(fit_a, rho = 0.8)
ccc(fit_b, sandwich_obj = sw_a)
#> Error in `ccc()`:
#> ! Sandwich provenance does not match the supplied bm_fit object.
#> i A structurally matching sandwich from a different fit/data snapshot
#>   was detected.
#> ! Recompute via `sandwich(fit)` or pass `sandwich_obj = NULL`.
This second case demonstrates why a hash over the posterior state is necessary: structural compatibility alone (matching K, J, study_ids) is insufficient.

title: 8  Software Architecture
text:
8.5 Dependency Strategy

8.5.1 Hard Dependencies (Imports)
The package declares five hard dependencies in DESCRIPTION:








Package
Role
Minimum Version




checkmate
Input validation (assert_* functions) throughout the codebase
2.1.0


cli
User-facing messages, warnings, and errors (cli_abort, cli_warn, cli_inform)
3.4.0


digest
MD5 fingerprint computation for provenance system
0.6.29


Matrix
nearPD() for nearest positive-definite projection when MCMC covariance is non-PD
1.5.0


rlang
.data pronoun, check_installed() for optional dependency gating, condition handling
1.1.0



The package depends on R >= 4.1.0 (for the native pipe and lambda syntax).


8.5.2 Runtime Dependencies (Suggests with Gating)
Two packages are essential at runtime but declared as Suggests to avoid CRAN compilation issues:

cmdstanr (>= 0.8.0): Required for bm_fit() (Stan sampling). Gated via rlang::check_installed("cmdstanr") at the point of use, producing a helpful installation message if missing.
posterior (>= 1.4.0): Required for .extract_draws() (posterior draw extraction). Gated identically.

This pattern—Suggests with check_installed() gating—allows the package to be installed and loaded without CmdStan being available, which is important for documentation builds and downstream package checks.


8.5.3 Benchmark-Only Dependencies

clubSandwich (>= 0.5.10): Used exclusively in compare_with_cr2() and test files for validating bayesRVE’s CR2 implementation against the reference implementation. Never called in the core pipeline (Decision D11). This is a deliberate design choice: bayesRVE implements CR2 sandwich estimation independently, and clubSandwich serves only as an external benchmark.
metafor (>= 4.0.0): Used in compare_with_cr2() and from_metafor() for interoperability. Not required for the core pipeline.



8.5.4 Visualization Dependencies

ggplot2 (>= 3.4.0), patchwork (>= 1.1.0), bayesplot (>= 1.10.0): Required for plot() methods. Gated via check_installed().



8.5.5 Stan Model Compilation and Caching
Stan model management is handled by stan-cache.R with a two-tier architecture described in Chapter 8:

Tier 1 (Session cache). Compiled CmdStanModel objects are stored in the .bm_cache environment (defined in bayesRVE-package.R). Subsequent calls to bm_compile() or bm_fit() within the same session retrieve the in-memory object without disk access.
Tier 2 (Disk cache). Compiled Stan executables are stored in tools::R_user_dir("bayesRVE", "cache") alongside MD5 hashes of the .stan source files. If the hash matches, the pre-compiled executable is loaded; if not, recompilation is triggered. A file-based lock mechanism prevents race conditions when multiple R sessions compile simultaneously.

The three valid model names are enumerated in .bm_valid_models: bm_ce_ncp, bm_ce_cp, and bm_che_ncp. The bm_compile() function accepts either the canonical name (with bm_ prefix) or the short name and normalizes internally. bm_clear_cache() removes both tiers, forcing recompilation on the next call.


8.5.6 Missing Dependency Impact









Dependency Missing
Impact
Functions Affected
User Action




cmdstanr
Cannot fit any model
bm_fit()
Install from stan-dev r-universe


CmdStan toolchain
cmdstanr loads but sampling fails
bm_fit()
cmdstanr::install_cmdstan()


posterior
Cannot extract draws
bm_fit(), sandwich(), ccc()
install.packages("posterior")


metafor
Cannot use interop functions
from_metafor(), compare_with_cr2()
Optional


clubSandwich
Cannot run benchmark tests
Test suite (Tier 2/3 tests)
Optional


ggplot2/bayesplot
No plotting
plot.*() methods
Optional; numeric output unaffected



The complete inference pipeline (bm_fit → sandwich → ccc → summary/confint) requires only Tier 1 imports plus cmdstanr and posterior from Tier 2. All other packages serve visualization, benchmarking, or interoperability purposes.






Carpenter, B., Gelman, A., Hoffman, M. D., Lee, D., Goodrich, B., Betancourt, M., Brubaker, M., Guo, J., Li, P., & Riddell, A. (2017). Stan: A probabilistic programming language. Journal of Statistical Software, 76(1), 1–32. https://doi.org/10.18637/jss.v076.i01


Pustejovsky, J. E., & Tipton, E. (2022). Meta-analysis with robust variance estimation: Expanding the range of working models. Prevention Science, 23(3), 425–438. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11121-021-01246-3


Viechtbauer, W. (2010). Conducting meta-analyses in R with the metafor package. Journal of Statistical Software, 36(3), 1–48. https://doi.org/10.18637/jss.v036.i03
