title: 9  Model Fitting Pipeline
text:
9.1 Formula Parsing and Data Preparation
This chapter traces the complete path from a user’s R call to posterior draws stored in memory. The bm_fit() function executes an eight-step pipeline: call capture, input validation, CmdStan availability check, data preparation, Stan (Carpenter et al., 2017) data assembly, model compilation, MCMC sampling, and post-sampling diagnostics. Each step is documented here with the actual source code that implements it, so that readers can audit the math-to-code mapping against the model definitions in Chapter 4 and the object contracts in Section 8.3.
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

title: 9  Model Fitting Pipeline
text:
9.1.1 The yi | se(vi) ~ predictors Syntax
bayesRVE adopts a formula interface that encodes three pieces of information in a single expression:

Outcome column (yi): the effect-size estimates.
Variance column (vi): the known sampling variances, wrapped in se() to indicate that the column contains variances (not standard errors). Alternatively, sd() wraps a standard-error column, which is internally squared.
Predictor specification (~ predictors): a standard R model formula for the fixed-effect design matrix \(\mathbf{X}\).

A typical call looks like:
fit <- bm_fit(yi | se(vi) ~ 1, data = dat, study = ~ studyid)
For meta-regression with moderators:
fit <- bm_fit(yi | se(vi) ~ intervention + dosage,
              data = dat, study = ~ studyid)


9.1.2 How .parse_formula() Works
The internal function .parse_formula() decomposes the two-sided formula into its constituent parts. It deparses the left-hand side, strips whitespace, and applies a regular expression to extract three named groups:
.parse_formula <- function(formula) {
  lhs <- deparse(formula[[2]], width.cutoff = 500L)
  lhs <- gsub("\\s+", "", lhs)

  lhs_pattern <- paste0(
    "^([a-zA-Z._][a-zA-Z0-9._]*)\\|",
    "(se|sd)\\(([a-zA-Z._][a-zA-Z0-9._]*)\\)$"
  )
  m <- regmatches(lhs, regexec(lhs_pattern, lhs))[[1L]]
  # m[2] = y_name, m[3] = var_type ("se"/"sd"), m[4] = var_name

  rhs_formula <- formula
  rhs_formula[[2]] <- NULL  # drop LHS to make one-sided

  list(y_name = m[2], var_name = m[4], var_type = m[3],
       rhs_formula = rhs_formula)
}

Note: Error-handling and validation code omitted for clarity. See the source file for the complete implementation.

The regex enforces that column names follow R’s identifier rules. If the match fails, an informative error message directs the user to the expected y | se(v) or y | sd(s) format.
The right-hand side formula is obtained by dropping element [[2]] from the original two-sided formula, yielding a one-sided formula suitable for model.matrix().


9.1.3 Study Identifier Parsing
The study argument accepts either a one-sided formula (~ studyid) or a bare string ("studyid"). The internal .parse_study() function normalizes both forms to a character column name and validates its presence in the data:
.parse_study <- function(study, data) {
  if (inherits(study, "formula")) {
    study_name <- deparse(study[[2]], width.cutoff = 500L)
    study_name <- trimws(study_name)
  } else if (is.character(study) && length(study) == 1L) {
    study_name <- study
  }
  # Validate column exists in data
  if (!study_name %in% names(data))
    cli_abort("Column {study_name} not found in data.")
  study_name
}
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

title: 9  Model Fitting Pipeline
text:
9.2 Study Ordering and Cluster Metadata
The .prepare_data() function is the central data-preparation orchestrator. After parsing the formula and study identifier, it sorts the data frame by study, constructs the design matrix, and computes all study-level summary statistics that Stan and the sandwich estimator require.

9.2.1 Sort-by-Study Guarantee
All observations are reordered so that rows belonging to the same study are contiguous:
ord <- order(study_ids_raw)
data <- data[ord, , drop = FALSE]
yi <- yi[ord]
vi <- vi[ord]
This sort order is a structural invariant: the Stan models use segment() and block() to extract contiguous sub-vectors and sub-matrices for each study, indexed by study_starts[j] and study_sizes[j]. The sort-by-study guarantee ensures that these array slices correspond to the correct observations.


9.2.2 Study-Level Quantities
After sorting, .prepare_data() factorizes the study identifiers and computes four study-level vectors:








Quantity
R Code
Mathematical Definition




\(n_j\)
n_j <- as.integer(table(study_factor))
Number of effect sizes in study \(j\)


\(\bar{y}_j\)
y_bar_j <- vapply(study_idx, function(idx) mean(yi[idx]), double(1L))
\(n_j^{-1} \sum_{i} y_{ij}\)


\(\bar{s}^2_j\)
s_bar_j2 <- vapply(study_idx, function(idx) mean(vi[idx]), double(1L))
\(n_j^{-1} \sum_{i} v_{ij}\)


\(w_j\)
w_j <- n_j / s_bar_j2
Precision weight for WLS


\(\bar{\mathbf{X}}_j\)
X_study <- do.call(rbind, lapply(study_idx, function(idx) colMeans(X[idx, , drop = FALSE])))
\(n_j^{-1} \sum_{i} \mathbf{x}_{ij}\)



The study_idx object is a named list of integer vectors produced by split(seq_len(N_total), study_factor), providing the row indices for each study. All subsequent study-level operations—in data preparation, sandwich estimation, and CCC calibration—use this index list.


9.2.3 Cluster-Constant Detection
The .is_cluster_constant() function determines whether all predictors are constant within each study:
.is_cluster_constant <- function(X, study_idx) {
  tol <- .Machine$double.eps^0.5
  for (idx in study_idx) {
    if (length(idx) > 1L) {
      X_sub <- X[idx, , drop = FALSE]
      col_ranges <- apply(X_sub, 2L, function(col) max(col) - min(col))
      if (any(col_ranges > tol)) return(FALSE)
    }
  }
  TRUE
}
This flag drives the use_obs_V toggle in the Stan data. When all predictors are cluster-constant (e.g., intercept-only models or study-level moderators), the CE model uses the study-level average variance \(\bar{s}^2_j\) as the observation-level variance, which simplifies the likelihood. When moderators vary within a study (e.g., different treatment arms), the observation-level variances \(v_{ij}\) are passed through V_obs and the generated-quantities block computes observation-level weighted scores for diagnostic verification (the production sandwich computes its own marginal scores analytically in R; see Section 11.2). This automatic dispatch was introduced in v0.3.0 to handle within-study varying moderators without requiring user intervention.
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

title: 9  Model Fitting Pipeline
text:
9.3 Stan Data Assembly

9.3.1 Data Block Mapping
The .prepare_data() function constructs a stan_data list inside the bm_data object. The .build_stan_input() function then merges this data with prior hyperparameters and control flags to produce the complete input for CmdStan’s $sample() method.
The mapping from R objects to Stan variables is:








Stan Variable
R Source
Description




J
length(unique(study))
Number of studies


N_total
nrow(X)
Total number of observations


study_sizes
n_j (from table())
\(n_j\): effect sizes per study


K
ncol(X)
Number of predictors (including intercept)


X
model.matrix(rhs_formula, data)
\(N \times K\) design matrix


y
yi (sorted)
Observed effect sizes \(y_{ij}\)


s_bar_sq
s_bar_j2
Study-level average sampling variance \(\bar{s}^2_j\)


V_obs
vi (sorted)
Per-observation sampling variance \(v_{ij}\)


use_obs_V
as.integer(!cluster_constant)
0 = study-level, 1 = observation-level


compute_scores
1L (always)
Enable diagnostic score gradient computation



The data list is assembled in .prepare_data():
stan_data <- list(
  J           = J,
  N_total     = N_total,
  study_sizes = n_j,
  K           = K,
  X           = X,
  y           = yi,
  s_bar_sq    = unname(s_bar_j2),
  V_obs       = as.array(vi),
  use_obs_V   = as.integer(!cluster_constant)
)


9.3.2 Prior-via-Data-Block Pattern
bayesRVE passes all prior hyperparameters as Stan data variables rather than hardcoding them in the Stan model. This design—known as the prior-via-data-block pattern—allows users to change priors without recompiling the Stan model.
The .prior_to_stan_data() function extracts numeric hyperparameters from a bm_prior object:
.prior_to_stan_data <- function(prior, model = "ce") {
  tau_df <- prior$tau$df
  if (is.infinite(tau_df)) tau_df <- 200  # approximate Inf with large df

  result <- list(
    prior_beta_mean    = prior$beta$mean,
    prior_beta_sd      = prior$beta$sd,
    prior_tau_df       = tau_df,
    prior_tau_location = prior$tau$location,
    prior_tau_scale    = prior$tau$scale
  )

  if (model == "che") {
    omega_prior <- prior$omega
    if (is.null(omega_prior)) omega_prior <- half_t(3, 0, 0.5)
    omega_df <- omega_prior$df
    if (is.infinite(omega_df)) omega_df <- 200

    result$prior_omega_df       <- omega_df
    result$prior_omega_location <- omega_prior$location
    result$prior_omega_scale    <- omega_prior$scale
  }
  result
}
The merging step in .build_stan_input() is a simple concatenation:
.build_stan_input <- function(bm_data, prior, model = "ce") {
  prior_data <- .prior_to_stan_data(prior, model = model)
  c(bm_data$stan_data, prior_data, list(compute_scores = 1L))
}
Note the compute_scores = 1L flag: bayesRVE computes conditional score gradients (\(\partial \ell_j / \partial \boldsymbol{\beta}\)) in the generated quantities block for diagnostic verification against the R-side analytic computation used by the production sandwich estimator (see Section 11.2). The overhead is minimal compared to MCMC sampling.


9.3.3 Prior Constructors
Users specify priors through a composable API. The bm_prior() constructor bundles distribution objects for \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) and \(\tau\) (and optionally \(\omega\) for CHE models):
bm_prior(beta = normal(0, 1), tau = half_t(3, 0, 0.5))
The distribution constructors normal(), half_t(), half_cauchy(), and half_normal() validate their arguments at construction time. The half_t() constructor warns for df < 1 (extremely heavy tails) and scale > 10 (essentially improper prior). Scale-appropriate defaults are provided by default_prior():



es_type
\(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) Prior
\(\tau\) Prior




"smd"
\(\text{Normal}(0, 1)\)
\(\text{Half-}t(3, 0, 0.5)\)


"log_or"
\(\text{Normal}(0, 1.5)\)
\(\text{Half-}t(3, 0, 0.5)\)


"fisher_z"
\(\text{Normal}(0, 1)\)
\(\text{Half-}t(3, 0, 0.3)\)


"generic"
\(\text{Normal}(0, 10)\)
\(\text{Half-}t(3, 0, 1)\)
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

title: 9  Model Fitting Pipeline
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

title: 9  Model Fitting Pipeline
text:
9.5 Generated Quantities
The generated quantities block in each Stan model computes three sets of outputs after each MCMC draw, without affecting the posterior distribution.

9.5.1 Study-Level Log-Likelihoods
Both CE and CHE models produce log_lik_J[j], the log-likelihood contribution of study \(j\) evaluated at the current draw. For the CE model:
\[
\ell_j(\boldsymbol{\beta}, \theta_j) = \sum_{i=1}^{n_j} \log \phi\!\left(y_{ij} \mid \mathbf{x}_{ij}^\top\boldsymbol{\beta} + \theta_j, \; \bar{s}^2_j\right),
\]
where \(\phi(\cdot \mid \mu, \sigma^2)\) is the normal density. For the CHE model, the log-likelihood uses the marginal form with the Woodbury quadratic (the same formula as the model block).
These log-likelihoods support study-level leave-one-out cross-validation via the loo package.


9.5.2 Score Gradients (Stan-Side)
The Stan generated quantities block also computes the conditional score gradient \(\mathbf{u}_j^{(\beta)} = \partial \ell_j / \partial \boldsymbol{\beta}\) at each MCMC draw. These conditional scores (which include the latent \(\theta_j\)) are available for diagnostics; the sandwich estimator computes its own marginal scores analytically in R at the posterior mean (Section 11.2). The CE model computes conditional scores in two modes:
Study-level mode (use_obs_V = 0): when predictors are cluster-constant, the score simplifies to:
\[
u_{jk}^{(\beta)} = \frac{1}{\bar{s}^2_j} \sum_{i=1}^{n_j} r_{ij} \, x_{ijk},
\]
where \(r_{ij} = y_{ij} - \mathbf{x}_{ij}^\top\boldsymbol{\beta} - \theta_j\).
Observation-level mode (use_obs_V = 1): when moderators vary within a study, each observation receives its own weight:
\[
u_{jk}^{(\beta)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n_j} \frac{r_{ij}}{v_{ij} + \tau^2} \, x_{ijk}.
\]
The CHE model uses the marginal-form score. Defining \(\mathbf{S}_j^{-1}\mathbf{r}_j\) via the Woodbury inversion, the score is \(\mathbf{X}_j^\top \mathbf{S}_j^{-1} \mathbf{r}_j\):
// bm_che_ncp.stan — score computation in generated quantities
vector[n_j] Sinv_r = r_j / a_j
                      - (tau2 * sum_r / (a_j * c_j))
                        * rep_vector(1.0, n_j);
score_beta[j] = (block(X, start, 1, n_j, K)' * Sinv_r)';


9.5.3 CHE Sufficient Statistics
The CHE model also exports four vectors of sufficient statistics (ss_sum_r, ss_sum_r2, ss_a, ss_c) in the generated quantities block, which can serve as diagnostic cross-checks. However, the production sandwich estimator computes all scores—including \(\partial \ell_j / \partial \tau^2\) and \(\partial \ell_j / \partial \omega^2\)—entirely in R at the posterior mean, recomputing residuals and Woodbury quantities from the original data rather than consuming Stan-side sufficient statistics (Section 11.2). This design keeps the full score computation in one place (R), making it easier to maintain and test.


9.5.4 Posterior Predictive Draws
Both models produce y_rep, a vector of posterior predictive draws for each observation. The CE model draws \(y_{\text{rep},ij} \sim \text{Normal}(\mathbf{x}_{ij}^\top\boldsymbol{\beta} + \theta_j, \bar{s}_j)\). The CHE model first draws a study-level random effect \(\theta_j \sim \text{Normal}(0, \tau)\), then generates \(y_{\text{rep},ij} \sim \text{Normal}(\mathbf{x}_{ij}^\top\boldsymbol{\beta} + \theta_j, \sqrt{a_j})\) where \(a_j = \bar{s}^2_j + \omega^2\). These draws support posterior predictive checks via pp_check()-style graphical diagnostics.
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

title: 9  Model Fitting Pipeline
text:
9.6 Posterior Draw Extraction

9.6.1 The .extract_draws() Function
All downstream computation in bayesRVE—the sandwich estimator, CCC calibration, and S3 methods—accesses posterior draws exclusively through the internal .extract_draws() function. This centralized interface ensures consistent column naming, handles model-specific edge cases, and wraps all CmdStan file access in tryCatch for robustness.
The function accepts a bm_fit object and a block argument specifying which parameter group to extract:
.extract_draws <- function(x, block = c("beta", "theta", "tau", "omega")) {
  block <- match.arg(block)

  K <- x$K
  J <- x$J

  # CHE omega draws
  if (block == "omega") {
    if (!identical(x$model, "che"))
      cli_abort("omega draws only available for CHE models.")
    mat <- posterior::as_draws_matrix(x$fit$draws("omega"))
    return(unname(as.matrix(mat)))
  }

  # CHE has no explicit theta (integrated out)
  if (block == "theta" && identical(x$model, "che")) {
    cli_warn("CHE model has no explicit theta parameters.")
    n_draws <- nrow(posterior::as_draws_matrix(x$fit$draws()))
    return(matrix(0, nrow = n_draws, ncol = J))
  }

  all_draws <- posterior::as_draws_matrix(x$fit$draws())

  param_names <- switch(block,
    beta  = sprintf("beta[%d]", seq_len(K)),
    theta = sprintf("theta[%d]", seq_len(J)),
    tau   = "tau"
  )

  out <- all_draws[, param_names, drop = FALSE]
  matrix(as.numeric(out), nrow = nrow(out), ncol = ncol(out),
         dimnames = list(NULL, param_names))
}

Note: Error-handling and validation code omitted for clarity. See the source file for the complete implementation.



9.6.2 Column Naming Convention
The returned matrix uses CmdStan’s bracket-indexed naming: beta[1], beta[2], …, beta[K] for fixed effects and theta[1], theta[2], …, theta[J] for study-level random effects. The tau block returns a single-column matrix with column name "tau". For CHE models, omega returns a single-column matrix.
This naming convention matters because the sandwich estimator indexes into the draw matrix by parameter name when computing \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}_{\text{MCMC}}\) (the posterior covariance of \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\)).


9.6.3 Sigma_MCMC Computation
The posterior covariance matrix \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}_{\text{MCMC}}\) is the \(K \times K\) sample covariance of the \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) draws:
\[
\hat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}_{\text{MCMC}} = \frac{1}{S-1}\sum_{s=1}^{S}\left(\boldsymbol{\beta}^{(s)} - \bar{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\right)\left(\boldsymbol{\beta}^{(s)} - \bar{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\right)^\top,
\]
where \(S\) is the total number of posterior draws (chains \(\times\) iterations). In R, this is computed as cov(.extract_draws(x, "beta")). This matrix serves as \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}_{\text{MCMC}}\) in the CCC formula (see Section 6.1), representing what the Bayesian posterior believes about the uncertainty in \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) before sandwich correction.


9.6.4 CHE-Specific: omega and omega2_hat
For CHE models, the \(\omega\) draws are extracted via .extract_draws(x, "omega"). The plug-in estimate \(\hat{\omega}^2\) used in the sandwich estimator follows Decision D20:
\[
\hat{\omega}^2 = \frac{1}{S}\sum_{s=1}^{S} \omega^{(s)2}.
\]
This is the posterior mean of \(\omega^2\), not the square of the posterior mean of \(\omega\). The distinction matters because Jensen’s inequality implies \(E[\omega^2] \geq (E[\omega])^2\), and the sandwich estimator needs the mean of the variance, not the variance of the mean. This is consistent with the CE convention where \(\hat{\tau}^2 = \bar{\tau}^{2}\), not \(\bar{\tau}^2 = (\bar{\tau})^2\).
To illustrate the practical magnitude, consider a typical CHE fit on the TSL15 data:
omega_draws <- .extract_draws(fit, "omega")

# Correct (Decision D20): posterior mean of the variance
omega2_hat <- mean(omega_draws^2)   # = 0.00372

# Incorrect: square of the posterior mean of the SD
mean(omega_draws)^2                 # = 0.00314
The discrepancy of approximately 18% arises from the non-negligible spread in the posterior of \(\omega\). Using the Jensen-biased estimate would systematically underestimate within-study heterogeneity, producing anti-conservative sandwich standard errors.


9.6.5 The CHE Theta Placeholder
When .extract_draws() is called with block = "theta" on a CHE model, it returns a zero matrix with \(S\) rows and \(J\) columns, accompanied by a warning. The CHE model has no latent \(\theta_j\) parameters—they were analytically integrated out in the marginal likelihood. This zero matrix serves as a compatibility layer so that downstream code that expects a theta block (e.g., bm_diagnose()) does not fail. The score_theta vector in the CHE generated quantities block is similarly set to zero.
The CCC calibration for CHE operates only on the \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\)-block (see Section 6.1), so the absence of \(\theta_j\) draws does not limit the Three-Layer Calibration pipeline.
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

title: 9  Model Fitting Pipeline
text:
9.7 Verification: Recovering Known Values
A Bayesian fitting procedure satisfies calibration if, when data are generated from known parameters, the posterior intervals cover the true values at the nominal rate. This section describes a simulation-recovery check that validates bm_fit() independently of the downstream sandwich and CCC machinery.

9.7.1 Design
Data are generated from a known CHE model with \(\beta_0 = 0.3\), \(\beta_1 = 0.1\), \(\tau = 0.2\), \(\omega = 0.15\). The DGP creates \(J = 30\) studies with \(n_j \sim \text{Uniform}\{2, 5\}\) effect sizes per study. Sampling variances are \(v_{ij} \sim \text{Uniform}(0.01, 0.10)\). A single study-level moderator \(x_{1j} \sim N(0, 1)\) is included.


9.7.2 Single-run check
fit <- bm_fit(yi | se(vi) ~ x1, data = dat, study = ~ studyid,
              model = "che", seed = 42)

# 95% posterior intervals
quantile(.extract_draws(fit, "beta")[, 1], c(0.025, 0.975))
#>  2.5% 97.5%
#> 0.071 0.525   # True beta_0 = 0.30 → covered ✓

quantile(.extract_draws(fit, "tau"), c(0.025, 0.975))
#>  2.5% 97.5%
#> 0.092 0.345   # True tau = 0.20 → covered ✓


9.7.3 Replicated coverage
Over \(R = 200\) replications at 95% nominal level, expected coverage is:



Parameter
Expected Coverage
Acceptable Range




\(\beta_0\), \(\beta_1\)
94–96%
\(\geq 93\%\)


\(\tau\), \(\omega\)
92–96%
\(\geq 90\%\)



Variance components may show slightly lower coverage because posterior intervals for standard deviations are asymmetric and bounded below at zero—a well-known property of half-\(t\) priors.


9.7.4 Scope
This simulation-recovery check validates the Stan model, prior specification, and posterior sampler. It does not validate the sandwich estimator, CCC transformation, or Satterthwaite degrees of freedom—those are verified separately through algebraic tests (Section 6.1), plug-in parity checks, and the TSL15 real-data analysis (Section 14.2).






Carpenter, B., Gelman, A., Hoffman, M. D., Lee, D., Goodrich, B., Betancourt, M., Brubaker, M., Guo, J., Li, P., & Riddell, A. (2017). Stan: A probabilistic programming language. Journal of Statistical Software, 76(1), 1–32. https://doi.org/10.18637/jss.v076.i01
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

title: 10  Sandwich Implementation
text:
11 Sandwich Implementation
This chapter traces the implementation of the cluster-robust sandwich variance estimator in bayesRVE, covering the full pipeline from S3 dispatch through HC0 computation, CR2 bias correction, and Satterthwaite degrees of freedom. The theoretical foundations appear in Chapter 6; here we show how each formula translates into working R code inside bayesRVE/R/sandwich.R.
The implementation serves two roles. First, the HC0 and scalar-corrected sandwich provide a fast classical benchmark for CCC calibration targets (Section 6.1). Second, the CR2 sandwich with Satterthwaite degrees of freedom constitutes the Three-Layer Calibration target—the gold standard against which CCC inference is validated.
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

title: 10  Sandwich Implementation
text:
11.1 Entry Points and Dispatch Logic
The sandwich module exposes a single S3 generic:
sandwich <- function(x, ...) UseMethod("sandwich")
The method sandwich.bm_fit() accepts three arguments beyond the fitted object: a working correlation rho, a small_sample correction selector, and dots reserved for future extensions. The rho parameter is stored in the output object for provenance tracking but does not enter the CE or CHE marginal sandwich computation—both models use the compound-symmetric working covariance \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j = a_j \mathbf{I} + \tau^2 \mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}'\), which fully determines the within-cluster correlation structure. The stored rho value is available to downstream consumers (e.g., sensitivity analyses) without affecting the sandwich estimate.
sandwich.bm_fit <- function(x, rho = 0.8,
                             small_sample = c("CR2", "scalar", "none"), ...) {
  checkmate::assert_class(x, "bm_fit")
  checkmate::assert_number(rho, lower = 0, upper = 1)
  small_sample <- match.arg(small_sample)
  ...
}
The small_sample argument controls the correction hierarchy:







Value
Behavior




"CR2"
Bell & McCaffrey (2002) CR2 adjustment matrices + Satterthwaite df (default since v0.5.0)


"scalar"
Scalar inflation \(c_\beta = J / (J - p_\text{eff})\) via Bayesian leverage


"none"
Raw HC0 sandwich, no correction




11.1.1 CE vs CHE Dispatch
The first branching point is model type. The bm_fit object carries a $model field ("ce" or "che"); for backward compatibility with pre-v0.4.0 objects that lack this field, NULL defaults to "ce":
model_type <- x$model %||% "ce"
if (model_type == "che") {
  return(.che_sandwich(x, rho = rho, small_sample = small_sample))
}
For CE models, the function continues with the operating-envelope check (Decision D7), moderator-scope dispatch (cluster-constant vs. observation-level), posterior draw extraction, and sequential computation of the marginal sandwich, CR2 upgrade, and conditional sandwich before assembly.


11.1.2 Operating Envelope
Decision D7 defines the minimum number of studies for reliable sandwich estimation:
\[
J_\min = \max(8,\; 2K + 2)
\]
When \(J < J_\min\), the beta-block sandwich is unreliable. Rather than aborting, bayesRVE enters a fallback mode where the beta-block CCC uses raw posterior draws and only the theta-block proceeds with sandwich calibration. The fallback flag propagates through the entire pipeline, skipping HC0 inversion, CR2 upgrade, and Satterthwaite computation for the beta block.


11.1.3 Moderator Scope
The obs_level flag, derived from bm_data$cluster_constant (set during data preparation), controls whether score and Hessian computation uses study-level aggregated quantities or observation-level Woodbury expansions. When all predictors are cluster-constant—the common case in meta-analysis with study-level moderators—the study-level path provides an efficient closed-form computation. When within-study varying predictors are present (e.g., outcome-type moderators), the observation-level path handles the full \(n_j \times K\) design matrices per study.
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

title: 10  Sandwich Implementation
text:
11.2 CE Sandwich Path
The CE sandwich decomposes into two independent blocks: a marginal sandwich for \(\boldsymbol{\phi} = (\boldsymbol{\beta}, \tau^2)\) and a conditional sandwich for \(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\). This blockwise structure (Decision D2) allows the CCC to apply different calibration strategies to each parameter group.

11.2.1 Marginal Sandwich: HC0 Computation
The function .marginal_sandwich() computes the HC0 sandwich at the posterior mean \((\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}, \hat\tau^2)\). For the study-level path, the computation follows the classical sandwich formula.
Residuals and scores. The marginal variance of the study mean is \(v_j = \bar{s}_j^2 / n_j + \hat\tau^2\). Study-level residuals are \(r_j = \bar{y}_j - \bar{\mathbf{x}}_j'\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\). The likelihood score matrix \(\mathbf{S}\) has rows indexed by study \(j\) and columns indexed by parameters \((\beta_1, \ldots, \beta_K, \tau^2)\):
\[
s_j^{(\beta)} = \frac{r_j}{v_j}\,\bar{\mathbf{x}}_j, \qquad
s_j^{(\tau^2)} = -\frac{1}{2v_j} + \frac{r_j^2}{2v_j^2}
\]
In code:
score_beta <- (r_j / v_j) * x_bar            # J x K
score_tau2 <- -1 / (2 * v_j) + r_j^2 / (2 * v_j^2)
S <- cbind(score_beta, score_tau2)            # J x (K+1)
Meat. The meat matrix accumulates outer products of the per-study score vectors:
\[
\mathbf{M} = \sum_{j=1}^{J} \mathbf{s}_j \mathbf{s}_j' = \mathbf{S}'\mathbf{S}
\]
The meat is a \((K{+}1) \times (K{+}1)\) matrix. Only likelihood scores contribute; prior scores are deliberately excluded from the meat to preserve the sandwich’s frequentist robustness properties.
Bread. The bread is the posterior Hessian \(\mathbf{H}_\text{post} = \mathbf{H}_\text{lik} + \mathbf{H}_\text{prior}\). The likelihood Hessian .build_marginal_hessian() assembles the \((K{+}1) \times (K{+}1)\) matrix from the beta–beta, beta–\(\tau^2\), and \(\tau^2\)–\(\tau^2\) blocks using the study-level summaries. The stabilized prior Hessian .build_prior_hessian() adds precision from the beta and \(\tau^2\) priors.
HC0 assembly.
\[
\mathbf{V}_\text{HC0} = \mathbf{H}_\text{post}^{-1}\,\mathbf{M}\,\mathbf{H}_\text{post}^{-1}
\]
The beta-block is extracted as \(\mathbf{V}_\beta = \mathbf{V}_\text{HC0}[1{:}K,\; 1{:}K]\). A positive-definiteness check is performed on \(\mathbf{V}_\beta\); if it fails, the beta-block CCC is skipped (no nearPD correction is ever applied—see Section 11.6).


11.2.2 Observation-Level Path
When within-study varying predictors are detected (obs_level = TRUE), scores use the Woodbury expansion of \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{-1}\) applied to the observation-level residual vector \(\mathbf{r}_j\):
\[
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{-1}\mathbf{r}_j = \frac{\mathbf{r}_j}{\bar{s}_j^2} - \frac{\tau^2\,\bar{r}_j}{\bar{s}_j^2\,v_j}\,\mathbf{1}_{n_j}
\]
The beta score for study \(j\) becomes \(\mathbf{s}_j^{(\beta)} = \mathbf{X}_j'\,\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{-1}\mathbf{r}_j\), using the full \(n_j \times K\) design matrix. The \(\tau^2\) score collapses to the same study-mean expression regardless of path, since \(\tau^2\) is a between-study parameter.


11.2.3 Conditional Sandwich: Theta Block
The function .conditional_sandwich() computes per-study conditional variances \(V_{\text{cond},j}\) for the CE theta parameters \(\theta_j \mid \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\). With beta fixed, each \(\theta_j\) is conditionally independent:
\[
H_{\theta,j} = \frac{n_j}{\bar{s}_j^2} + \frac{1}{\hat\tau^2}, \qquad
s_{\theta,j} = \frac{n_j}{\bar{s}_j^2}\,r_{\text{cond},j}, \qquad
V_{\text{cond},j} = \frac{s_{\theta,j}^2}{H_{\theta,j}^2}
\]
where \(r_{\text{cond},j} = \bar{y}_j - \bar{\mathbf{x}}_j'\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} - \hat\theta_j\). These per-study scalar variances serve as the CCC theta-block calibration targets.


11.2.4 Scalar Small-Sample Correction
The small_sample = "scalar" path inflates the HC0 beta-block by a scalar factor derived from Bayesian leverage. The effective degrees of freedom \(p_\text{eff}\) are computed from the trace of the Bayesian hat matrix, yielding:
\[
c_\beta = \frac{J}{J - p_\text{eff}}, \qquad \mathbf{V}_\text{scalar} = c_\beta \cdot \mathbf{V}_\text{HC0}
\]
This provides a simple correction that improves coverage when \(J\) is small relative to \(K\), but lacks the per-study bias adjustment of CR2 and does not provide degrees of freedom for \(t\)-based inference.
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

title: 10  Sandwich Implementation
text:
11.3 CHE Sandwich Path
The CHE model analytically integrates out the study-specific effects \(\theta_j\), yielding a marginal parameter vector \(\boldsymbol{\phi} = (\boldsymbol{\beta}, \tau^2, \omega^2)\) of dimension \(K + 2\). The function .che_sandwich() handles this distinct pipeline.

11.3.1 Architectural Principle: Reuse via omega2_hat
The central insight of the v0.6.0 implementation is that the CHE working covariance differs from CE only by a substitution in the within-study variance component. In the CE model, the marginal covariance for study \(j\) is:
\[
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^\text{CE} = \bar{s}_j^2\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j} + \tau^2\,\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}'
\]
In the CHE model, integrating out \(\theta_j\) with \(\theta_j \sim N(\mathbf{x}_j'\boldsymbol{\beta},\, \omega^2)\) yields:
\[
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^\text{CHE} = (\bar{s}_j^2 + \omega^2)\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j} + \tau^2\,\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}'
\]
The substitution \(a_j = \bar{s}_j^2 + \hat\omega^2\) (where \(\hat\omega^2 = \text{E}[\omega^2 \mid \text{data}]\) per Decision D20) allows all CE CR2 infrastructure to be reused without modification. Every helper function—.compute_W_j(), .compute_wls_bread(), .compute_hat_blocks(), .compute_cr2_adjustments(), .compute_vcr2(), .satterthwaite_df()—accepts an omega2_hat parameter that defaults to 0 for CE.


11.3.2 CHE HC0 Sandwich
The CHE HC0 path constructs the full \((K{+}2) \times (K{+}2)\) sandwich directly. Scores for \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\), \(\tau^2\), and \(\omega^2\) are computed analytically at the posterior mean using the Woodbury-expanded covariance. The bread uses .build_che_hessian(), which assembles the full Hessian including the \(\omega^2\) block. Unlike the CE path, which uses the observed likelihood Hessian (including residual-dependent terms), .build_che_hessian() uses expected information—the Hessian with residual terms dropped, retaining only trace-based curvature. This choice is deliberate: the observed information for the \((K{+}2)\)-dimensional Hessian can produce negative diagonal entries for variance-component blocks, yielding a non-PD bread. Expected information is always PD for well-identified models and is standard practice in REML-type estimation.
The expected-information Hessian has three nonzero block types:

Beta–beta: \(\sum_j \mathbf{X}_j' \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{-1} \mathbf{X}_j\) (identical to CE, with \(a_j = \bar{s}_j^2 + \hat\omega^2\)).
Variance–variance: \(H_{\tau^2,\tau^2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_j n_j^2 / c_j^2\); \(H_{\omega^2,\omega^2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_j [(n_j{-}1)/a_j^2 + 1/c_j^2]\); \(H_{\tau^2,\omega^2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_j n_j/c_j^2\).
Beta–variance cross-blocks: identically zero under expected information.

The stabilized prior Hessian adds diagonal precision: \(1/\sigma_\beta^2\) for each beta coefficient, and ridge-capped entries \(\min(1/(2\hat\tau^4),\, 10^6)\) and \(\min(1/(2\hat\omega^4),\, 10^6)\) for the variance components (see the analogous CE ridge in Chapter 6). If the resulting \(\mathbf{H}_\text{post}\) is not positive definite despite expected information, a nearPD correction is applied—the only location in the sandwich module where this fallback is used, justified because the bread (unlike \(\mathbf{V}_{\text{CR2}}\)) is an auxiliary quantity whose slight perturbation does not mask a diagnostic signal (see also Section 11.6).


11.3.3 CHE CR2 Upgrade
When small_sample = "CR2", the CHE path mirrors the CE CR2 pipeline with one difference: the omega2_hat parameter flows through:
bread_info_cr2 <- .compute_wls_bread(bm_data, tau2_hat, K, J,
                                      omega2_hat = omega2_hat)
All subsequent steps—hat blocks, CR2 adjustments, V_CR2, and Satterthwaite df—call the same generic functions as CE. The CHE V_CR2 replaces the HC0 beta-block as the CCC calibration target.


11.3.4 CHE Conditional Sandwich
Since the CHE model integrates \(\theta_j\) out analytically, there is no explicit theta parameter to calibrate. The CHE sandwich object sets V_sand_cond = 0 for all \(J\) studies, maintaining structural compatibility with downstream CCC and method dispatch while signaling that no theta-block calibration is needed.


11.3.5 Decision D22: Singleton Warning
Before any computation, .che_sandwich() checks for singleton studies (\(n_j = 1\)), where \(\tau^2\) and \(\omega^2\) are not separately identified from the data. If all studies are singletons, a warning indicates that posterior separation relies entirely on the prior. If more than 50% of studies are singletons, a weaker warning flags potential sensitivity.
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

title: 10  Sandwich Implementation
text:
11.4 Woodbury Linear Algebra and \(\mathbf{W}^{1/2}\)
The Woodbury matrix identity is the computational backbone of the sandwich module. Two functions encapsulate the key linear algebra: .compute_W_j() for the weight matrix and .compute_W_j_sqrt() for its matrix square root.

11.4.1 Weight Matrix: .compute_W_j()
The working covariance for study \(j\) has compound-symmetric structure:
\[
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j = a_j\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j} + \tau^2\,\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}'
\]
where \(a_j = \bar{s}_j^2 + \hat\omega^2\) (reducing to \(\bar{s}_j^2\) for CE when \(\hat\omega^2 = 0\)). The Woodbury inverse gives the weight matrix in closed form:
\[
\mathbf{W}_j = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{-1} = \frac{1}{a_j}\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j} - \frac{\tau^2}{a_j\,c_j}\,\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}'
\]
where \(c_j = a_j + n_j\tau^2\). For singletons (\(n_j = 1\)), this simplifies to the scalar \(1/(a_j + \tau^2)\).
The implementation:
.compute_W_j <- function(s_bar2_j, tau2_hat, n_j, omega2_hat = 0) {
  a_j <- s_bar2_j + omega2_hat
  a_j <- max(a_j, .Machine$double.eps)
  if (n_j == 1L) return(matrix(1 / (a_j + tau2_hat), 1L, 1L))

  c_j <- a_j + n_j * tau2_hat
  diag_val <- 1 / a_j
  off_val  <- -tau2_hat / (a_j * c_j)
  W_j <- matrix(off_val, n_j, n_j)
  diag(W_j) <- diag_val + off_val
  W_j
}
The floor on \(a_j\) guards against degenerate cases where both sampling variance and \(\omega^2\) are near zero.


11.4.2 Matrix Square Root: .compute_W_j_sqrt() (Decision D24)
The CR2 adjustment requires the symmetrization transform \(\tilde{\mathbf{H}}_{jj} = \mathbf{W}_j^{1/2}\,\mathbf{X}_j\,\mathbf{Q}\,\mathbf{X}_j'\,\mathbf{W}_j^{1/2}\) (Decision D16). Rather than computing the general eigendecomposition of \(\mathbf{W}_j\) at every study, Decision D24 exploits the compound-symmetric structure.
The matrix \(\mathbf{W}_j = a\,\mathbf{I} + b\,\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}'\) (where \(a = 1/a_j - \tau^2/(a_j c_j)\) and \(b = -\tau^2/(a_j c_j)\)) has exactly two distinct eigenvalues:
\[
\lambda_1 = a + n_j b \quad (\text{eigenvector: } \mathbf{1}/\sqrt{n_j}), \qquad
\lambda_2 = a \quad (n_j - 1 \text{ times})
\]
The square root and inverse square root inherit the same rank-1 structure:
\[
\mathbf{W}_j^{1/2} = \sqrt{\lambda_2}\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j} + \frac{\sqrt{\lambda_1} - \sqrt{\lambda_2}}{n_j}\,\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}'
\]
\[
\mathbf{W}_j^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda_2}}\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j} + \frac{1/\sqrt{\lambda_1} - 1/\sqrt{\lambda_2}}{n_j}\,\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}'
\]
This avoids the \(O(n_j^3)\) eigendecomposition cost per study. The implementation constructs both matrices directly from the two eigenvalues:
sa <- sqrt(lam2)                              # sqrt of repeated eigenvalue
s1 <- sqrt(lam1)                              # sqrt of rank-1 eigenvalue
W_sqrt <- matrix((s1 - sa) / n_j, n_j, n_j)
diag(W_sqrt) <- sa + (s1 - sa) / n_j
A guard checks that both eigenvalues are positive; if not, the function falls back to a general eigendecomposition with eigenvalue flooring.
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

title: 10  Sandwich Implementation
text:
11.5 CR2 Assembly and Satterthwaite df
The CR2 pipeline consists of four steps executed sequentially after the HC0 computation: WLS bread, hat matrix blocks, CR2 adjustment matrices, and V_CR2 assembly, followed by Satterthwaite degrees of freedom.

11.5.1 Step A.1: WLS Bread (Decision D15)
Decision D15 mandates that CR2 uses the fixed-effect WLS bread \(\mathbf{Q} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{W}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\), not the posterior Hessian. The posterior Hessian includes prior precision, which is appropriate for the MCMC covariance used in CCC but would distort the frequentist leverage computations that CR2 relies on.
The function .compute_wls_bread() handles both study-level and observation-level paths:
Study-level (cluster-constant predictors): Each study contributes a scalar weight \(w_j = 1/v_j\) where \(v_j = (\bar{s}_j^2 + \hat\omega^2)/n_j + \hat\tau^2\):
\[
\mathbf{Q} = \left(\sum_{j=1}^{J} w_j\,\bar{\mathbf{x}}_j\,\bar{\mathbf{x}}_j'\right)^{-1}
\]
Observation-level (within-study varying predictors): Each study contributes the full Woodbury weight matrix:
\[
\mathbf{Q} = \left(\sum_{j=1}^{J} \mathbf{X}_j'\,\mathbf{W}_j\,\mathbf{X}_j\right)^{-1}
\]
The function returns \(\mathbf{Q}\) along with the per-study lists \(\mathbf{W}_j\) and \(\mathbf{X}_j\), which are reused by all downstream CR2 functions. If \(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{W}\mathbf{X}\) is singular, a generalized inverse via MASS::ginv() is used with a diagnostic warning.


11.5.2 Step A.2a: Hat Matrix Blocks
The function .compute_hat_blocks() computes the diagonal blocks of the hat matrix for each study:
\[
\mathbf{H}_{jj} = \mathbf{X}_j\,\mathbf{Q}\,\mathbf{X}_j'\,\mathbf{W}_j
\]
For the study-level path, this reduces to the scalar leverage \(h_j = \bar{\mathbf{x}}_j'\,\mathbf{Q}\,\bar{\mathbf{x}}_j \cdot w_j\). A warning is issued if any \(h_j > 0.5\), indicating a high-leverage study that may destabilize CR2.
Note that \(\mathbf{H}_{jj}\) is generally not symmetric for the observation-level path. The symmetrization needed for the CR2 eigendecomposition is handled in the next step via the \(\mathbf{W}^{1/2}\) transform.


11.5.3 Step A.2b: CR2 Adjustment Matrices (Decision D16)
The function .compute_cr2_adjustments() computes the Bell & McCaffrey (2002) adjustment matrices \(\mathbf{A}_j\) using the \(\mathbf{W}^{1/2}\) symmetrization approach (Decision D16).
For the study-level or singleton case, the adjustment is a scalar:
\[
A_j = (1 - h_j)^{-1/2}
\]
For the observation-level case, the three-step procedure is:

Symmetrize: \(\tilde{\mathbf{H}}_{jj} = \mathbf{W}_j^{1/2}\,\mathbf{X}_j\,\mathbf{Q}\,\mathbf{X}_j'\,\mathbf{W}_j^{1/2}\)
Eigendecompose: \(\mathbf{I} - \tilde{\mathbf{H}}_{jj} = \mathbf{V}\,\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\,\mathbf{V}'\), then \(\tilde{\mathbf{A}}_j = (\mathbf{I} - \tilde{\mathbf{H}}_{jj})^{-1/2} = \mathbf{V}\,\boldsymbol{\Lambda}^{-1/2}\,\mathbf{V}'\)
Back-transform: \(\mathbf{A}_j = \mathbf{W}_j^{-1/2}\,\tilde{\mathbf{A}}_j\,\mathbf{W}_j^{1/2}\)

Eigenvalues of \(\mathbf{I} - \tilde{\mathbf{H}}_{jj}\) below \(10^{-10}\) are truncated with a warning, preventing division by near-zero values in the inverse square root. Leverage values exceeding 0.999 are capped for the scalar path.


11.5.4 Step A.3: V_CR2 Assembly
The function .compute_vcr2() assembles the CR2 variance estimator (Decision D12):
\[
\mathbf{V}_\text{CR2} = \mathbf{Q}\left(\sum_{j=1}^{J} \tilde{\mathbf{s}}_j\,\tilde{\mathbf{s}}_j'\right)\mathbf{Q}
\]
where \(\tilde{\mathbf{s}}_j = \mathbf{X}_j'\,\mathbf{W}_j\,\mathbf{A}_j\,\hat{\mathbf{e}}_j\) are the CR2-adjusted scores and \(\hat{\mathbf{e}}_j\) are the WLS residuals.
For the study-level path, the residual is the scalar \(r_j = \bar{y}_j - \bar{\mathbf{x}}_j'\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\), the adjustment is the scalar \(A_j\), and the adjusted score simplifies to \(\tilde{\mathbf{s}}_j = \bar{\mathbf{x}}_j \cdot w_j \cdot A_j \cdot r_j\).
For the observation-level path, the full matrix computation applies: \(\hat{\mathbf{e}}_j = \mathbf{y}_j - \mathbf{X}_j\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) (the \(n_j\)-vector of residuals), \(\tilde{\mathbf{e}}_j = \mathbf{A}_j\hat{\mathbf{e}}_j\), and \(\tilde{\mathbf{s}}_j = \mathbf{X}_j'\,\mathbf{W}_j\,\tilde{\mathbf{e}}_j\).
The assembled V_CR2 replaces the HC0 beta-block as the CCC calibration target. This is the critical integration point identified during v0.5.0 development: marg$V_beta (not V_sand_marg) must be overwritten, since .assemble_bm_sandwich() exports V_beta as the final V_sand_marg field.


11.5.5 Step B.1: Satterthwaite Degrees of Freedom (Decision D17)
The function .satterthwaite_df() computes per-coefficient degrees of freedom using the trace formula from Bell & McCaffrey (2002) and Tipton (2015).
For each coefficient \(k\), define the \(J \times J\) matrix \(\mathbf{P}_k\) with elements:
\[
P_{k,ii} = \mathbf{g}_{ik}'\,\mathbf{W}_i^{-1}\,\mathbf{g}_{ik} - \mathbf{g}_{ik}'\,\mathbf{X}_i\,\mathbf{Q}\,\mathbf{X}_i'\,\mathbf{g}_{ik}
\]
\[
P_{k,ij} = -(\mathbf{X}_i'\mathbf{g}_{ik})'\,\mathbf{Q}\,(\mathbf{X}_j'\mathbf{g}_{jk}), \quad i \neq j
\]
where \(\mathbf{g}_{jk} = \mathbf{A}_j'\,\mathbf{W}_j\,\mathbf{X}_j\,\mathbf{Q}\,\mathbf{c}_k\) and \(\mathbf{c}_k\) is the \(k\)-th standard basis vector.
The Satterthwaite df is then:
\[
\text{df}_k = \frac{[\text{tr}(\mathbf{P}_k)]^2}{\text{tr}(\mathbf{P}_k^2)}
\]
The implementation pre-computes \(\mathbf{G}_j = \mathbf{A}_j'\,\mathbf{W}_j\,\mathbf{X}_j\,\mathbf{Q}\) for all studies, then extracts column \(k\) as \(\mathbf{g}_{jk}\). The \(J \times J\) matrix \(\mathbf{P}_k\) is built with diagonal terms involving \(\mathbf{W}_j^{-1}\mathbf{g}_{jk}\) (computed via solve() for numerical stability) and off-diagonal terms from the cross-products.
The trace \(\text{tr}(\mathbf{P}_k^2)\) is computed efficiently as sum(P_k * P_k) (the Frobenius inner product of \(\mathbf{P}_k\) with itself, which equals the sum of squared elements for a symmetric matrix).
Diagnostic safeguards handle three failure modes:

Computation failure (singular \(\mathbf{W}_j\)): df set to NA, replaced by naive \(\max(J - K, 1)\).
\(\text{df}_k < 1\): Replaced by naive df with a warning.
\(\text{df}_k < 4\): Retained but flagged following Tipton (2015), who showed that \(t\)-based inference is unreliable below this threshold.



11.5.6 Decision D25: Plug-In Parity
The plug-in parity test (Decision D25) validates the CR2 linear algebra by injecting REML estimates of \(\hat\tau^2\) (and \(\hat\omega^2\) for CHE) into the bayesRVE sandwich pipeline and comparing the resulting V_CR2 against clubSandwich::vcovCR() (Pustejovsky & Tipton, 2022) applied to a metafor::rma.mv() (Viechtbauer, 2010) fit. Agreement at \(10^{-6}\) tolerance isolates the linear algebra from MCMC noise, confirming that the Woodbury, CR2, and Satterthwaite implementations exactly match the reference. This test is a cornerstone of the verification strategy (Section 12.1).
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

title: 10  Sandwich Implementation
text:
11.6 Numerical Safeguards
The sandwich module incorporates multiple defensive measures to ensure numerical stability across the wide range of meta-analytic datasets encountered in practice.

11.6.1 Generalized Inverse Fallback
When \(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{W}\mathbf{X}\) is singular or near-singular (e.g., due to collinear moderators or extreme weight imbalance), solve() will fail. The bread computation wraps the inversion in tryCatch and falls back to MASS::ginv():
Q <- tryCatch(
  solve(XtWX),
  error = function(e) {
    cli::cli_warn("X'WX is singular; using generalized inverse.")
    MASS::ginv(XtWX)
  }
)


11.6.2 Positive-Definiteness Diagnostics
The beta_pd flag records whether \(\mathbf{V}_\beta\) is positive definite. This diagnostic propagates to the CCC: if beta_pd = FALSE, the beta-block CCC transformation is skipped because the Cholesky factorization \(\mathbf{L}_2 = \text{chol}(\mathbf{V}_\text{CR2})\) would fail. The PD check is recomputed after each correction stage (HC0, scalar, CR2) so the flag reflects the final export target.


11.6.3 Score Balance Check
The score sum norm \(\|\sum_j \mathbf{s}_j\|\) is stored in diagnostics$score_sum_norm. At the posterior mean, the score equation \(\sum_j \mathbf{s}_j + \mathbf{s}_\text{prior} = \mathbf{0}\) should hold approximately (exactly for MAP, approximately for posterior mean with informative priors). Large values indicate numerical issues in score computation.


11.6.4 Condition Number Monitoring
The condition number \(\kappa(\mathbf{H}_\text{post})\) is computed from the eigenvalue ratio and stored in diagnostics. Values exceeding \(10^{12}\) trigger a warning suggesting stronger priors. For the CR2 path, \(\kappa(\mathbf{Q})\) is similarly monitored via diagnostics$H_condition.


11.6.5 The nearPD Anti-Pattern
A deliberate design choice: nearPD() is never applied to \(\mathbf{V}_\text{CR2}\) or any sandwich variance output. If the sandwich estimate is not positive definite, this signals a genuine problem—insufficient studies, extreme leverage, or model misspecification—that should be reported to the user, not silently corrected. Applying nearPD() would mask the diagnostic signal and produce artificially smooth confidence intervals with no theoretical backing. Instead, non-PD beta-blocks cause the CCC to fall back to raw posterior draws, preserving valid Bayesian inference at the cost of losing the sandwich calibration. (The one exception is .build_che_hessian(), which applies nearPD to the posterior bread matrix \(\mathbf{H}_\text{post}\)—an auxiliary intermediate, not a sandwich variance estimate—to ensure a valid bread exists for the HC0 inversion.)
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

title: 10  Sandwich Implementation
text:
11.7 Design Decision Registry
The following table summarizes all design decisions that directly affect the sandwich() module. Each decision is referenced by its identifier (Dn) from the project development log.



Table 11.1: Design decisions affecting the sandwich variance estimation module.










Decision
Choice
Rationale




D2
Blockwise hybrid CCC: beta-block marginal sandwich, theta-block diagonal rescaling
Avoids Neyman-Scott inconsistency for variance components


D5
rho handled in sandwich(), not in bm_fit()
User controls working correlation at analysis time


D6
Within-study varying moderators trigger obs-level scores
Auto-dispatch via cluster_constant; no user action needed


D7
Operating envelope \(J_{\min} = \max(8, 2K+2)\); fallback to raw posterior
Prevents ill-conditioned sandwiches with too few clusters


D11
CR2 + Satterthwaite implemented independently
No clubSandwich runtime dependency; full numerical control


D12
CCC target = \(V_{\text{CR2}}\) (not \(V_{\text{HC0}} \cdot c_\beta\))
CR2 is less biased, especially for heterogeneous cluster sizes


D13
Default small_sample = "CR2", default df = Satterthwaite
Best available finite-sample correction as package default


D15
WLS bread \((X'WX)^{-1}\), not posterior Hessian
Frequentist CR2 requires frequentist bread; prior precision distorts leverage


D16
\(W^{1/2}\) symmetrization for hat matrix
Ensures real eigendecomposition for \(A_j\)


D17
Satterthwaite df via \(P_k\) trace formula
Deterministic, matches Tipton (2015)


D20
\(\hat\omega^2 = E[\omega^2]\) (Jensen-safe)
Correct variance plug-in; consistent with CE convention


D22
Singleton non-identifiability warning
Protects users from misleading CHE results


D24
Analytical \(W^{1/2}\) via rank-1 structure
\(O(n_j)\) closed form vs \(O(n_j^3)\) eigendecomposition


D25
Plug-in parity: REML injection matches clubSandwich at \(10^{-6}\)
Isolates linear algebra from MCMC noise












Bell, R. M., & McCaffrey, D. F. (2002). Bias reduction in standard errors for linear regression with multi-stage samples. Survey Methodology, 28(2), 169–181.


Pustejovsky, J. E., & Tipton, E. (2022). Meta-analysis with robust variance estimation: Expanding the range of working models. Prevention Science, 23(3), 425–438. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11121-021-01246-3


Tipton, E. (2015). Small sample adjustments for robust variance estimation with meta-regression. Psychological Methods, 20(3), 375–393. https://doi.org/10.1037/met0000011


Viechtbauer, W. (2010). Conducting meta-analyses in R with the metafor package. Journal of Statistical Software, 36(3), 1–48. https://doi.org/10.18637/jss.v036.i03

title: 11  CCC Implementation and Inferential Outputs
text:
11.1 The CCC Affine Transformation
The Cholesky Calibration Correction transforms raw MCMC posterior draws so that their covariance structure matches the CR2 sandwich variance target. The core equation is an affine map applied to each draw:
\[
\boldsymbol{\beta}^{*(s)} \;=\; \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \;+\; A_{\beta} \bigl(\boldsymbol{\beta}^{(s)} - \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\bigr),
\qquad A_{\beta} = L_2\, L_1^{-1},
\tag{11.1}\]
where \(L_1 = \operatorname{chol}(\hat\Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}})\) and \(L_2 = \operatorname{chol}(V_{\mathrm{CR2}})\) are lower-triangular Cholesky factors. This preserves the posterior mean exactly (Theorem 1, Section 6.1) and re-scales the covariance from \(\hat\Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}}\) to \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\).
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification
11  CCC Implementation and Inferential Outputs

title: 11  CCC Implementation and Inferential Outputs
text:
11.1.1 Step-by-step numerical trace
Consider a concrete scenario with \(S = 4{,}000\) draws, \(K = 2\) fixed effects (intercept + one moderator), and \(J = 20\) studies.



Table 11.1: Numerical trace of the beta-block Cholesky CCC.











Step
Operation
Dimensions
Notes




1
Extract draws_beta from posterior
\(4000 \times 2\)
Raw MCMC draws of \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\)


2
Compute \(\hat\Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}} = \operatorname{cov}(\texttt{draws\_beta})\)
\(2 \times 2\)
Sample covariance of the \(S\) draws


3
Cholesky factor \(L_1 = \operatorname{chol}(\hat\Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}})\)
\(2 \times 2\)
Lower-triangular; .safe_chol() applies nearPD fallback if needed


4
Obtain \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) from the sandwich object
\(2 \times 2\)
sandwich_obj$V_sand_marg


5
Cholesky factor \(L_2 = \operatorname{chol}(V_{\mathrm{CR2}})\)
\(2 \times 2\)
Lower-triangular; never apply nearPD to the sandwich target


6
Solve \(L_1^{-1}\) via forwardsolve(L1, diag(K))
\(2 \times 2\)
Numerically stable triangular solve


7
Compute \(A_{\beta} = L_2 \, L_1^{-1}\)
\(2 \times 2\)
The full transformation matrix


8
Center: \(D = \texttt{draws\_beta} - \mathbf{1}_S \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^T\)
\(4000 \times 2\)
sweep(draws_beta, 2, center)


9
Transform: \(D^* = D \, A_{\beta}^T\)
\(4000 \times 2\)
BLAS matrix multiply, \(O(S \cdot K^2)\)


10
Uncenter: \(\texttt{draws\_star} = D^* + \mathbf{1}_S \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^T\)
\(4000 \times 2\)
CCC-calibrated draws






After Step 10, \(\operatorname{cov}(\texttt{draws\_star}) \approx V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) by construction, with approximation error bounded by \(O(1/S)\). The Frobenius norm \(\|A_{\beta} - I_K\|_F\) measures how far the transformation departs from the identity.


11.1.2 The .safe_chol() asymmetry
A critical design choice: nearPD() is applied to \(\hat\Sigma_{\mathrm{MCMC}}\) (the source covariance) when it is not positive definite, but never to \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) (the target). The rationale is that the source is a sample covariance that may be non-PD due to MCMC issues (fixable by projection), while the target encodes the frequentist calibration guarantee (modifying it would corrupt the inference). If \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) is not PD, the beta-block CCC is skipped entirely.
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification
11  CCC Implementation and Inferential Outputs

title: 11  CCC Implementation and Inferential Outputs
text:
11.2 CE vs CHE Dispatch
The ccc.bm_fit() method dispatches to one of two internal paths based on the model type stored in x$model:
CE path (blockwise hybrid, Decision D2).

Beta-block (\(K \times K\) Cholesky CCC): Full affine transformation targeting \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\).
Theta-block (\(J\)-vector diagonal rescaling): \(\theta_j^{*} = \hat\theta_j + r_j(\theta_j - \hat\theta_j)\) where \(r_j = \sqrt{V^{\mathrm{cond}}_{\mathrm{sand},j} / \operatorname{Var}_{\mathrm{MCMC}}(\theta_j)}\).

CHE path (beta-block only). Study effects \(\theta_j\) are analytically integrated out. Only the beta-block Cholesky CCC is applied. A dummy theta matrix of zeros is appended for API compatibility.

11.2.1 Beta-block skip conditions



Table 11.2: Beta-block CCC decision logic.










Condition
Action
method field




\(J < K\)
Unconditional skip (structural singularity)
"fallback_raw"


\(J = K\), \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) not PD
Skip with warning
"fallback_raw"


\(J = K\), \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) PD
Proceed with zero-excess-df warning
"ccc"


\(J > K\), \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) not PD
Skip with warning
"fallback_raw"


\(J > K\), \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) PD
Normal CCC
"ccc"


Sandwich fallback (D7)
Beta skipped, theta proceeds
"ccc_partial"

title: 11  CCC Implementation and Inferential Outputs
text:
11.4 Verification Checks
After assembly, .verify_ccc() runs six post-hoc checks:



Table 11.4: CCC verification checks. Tolerances are relative to the target.










Check
What it verifies
Tolerance




V1
Mean preservation: \(\|\bar{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^* - \bar{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\| < \epsilon\)
\(10^{-6}\)


V2
Beta variance: diagonal of \(\operatorname{cov}(\boldsymbol{\beta}^*)\) vs \(\operatorname{diag}(V_{\mathrm{CR2}})\)
0.05 (5%)


V3
Theta variance: \(\operatorname{Var}(\theta_j^*)\) vs \(V^{\mathrm{cond}}_j\)
0.05 (5%)


V4
Beta covariance: off-diagonal (relative Frobenius)
0.05 (5%)


V5
Center preservation: centering point is correct
\(10^{-6}\)


V6
Diagnostics: \(\|A_\beta - I_K\|_F\) is finite and non-negative
structural






For CHE models, V3 is skipped (theta block is dummy zeros). The verification outcome is stored in object$verification and printed in summary output.






Note



These are algebraic sanity checks, not hypothesis tests. A V2 failure at 5% indicates Monte Carlo noise from finite \(S\), not a bug—but it signals that the user may want more posterior draws.

text:
11.5 Inferential Methods
The bm_ccc object supports four S3 methods. Each checks the model field to handle the CHE theta quarantine.

11.5.1 coef.bm_ccc()
Returns the posterior mean of CCC-calibrated fixed effects by default.
coef(cal)
#>    Intercept          x1
#>       0.1342      0.0872








type
CE model
CHE model




"beta" (default)
\(K\)-vector of calibrated beta means
Same


"theta"
\(J\)-vector of calibrated theta means
Returns NULL with warning


"all"
\((K+J)\)-vector
Returns beta only



When beta_skipped = TRUE, returns original (uncalibrated) posterior means with a warning.


11.5.2 vcov.bm_ccc()
Returns the \(K \times K\) covariance of CCC-calibrated beta draws: cov(draws_star[, 1:K]). By Theorem 2, this equals \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) exactly (up to Monte Carlo noise).


11.5.3 confint.bm_ccc()
Constructs confidence intervals:
confint(cal)
#>                 2.5%     97.5%
#>   Intercept   0.0793    0.1891
#>   x1         -0.0236    0.1980
#>   attr(,"df")
#>   [1] 76.7 76.5
#>   attr(,"method")
#>   [1] "CCC-t (Satterthwaite df, per-coefficient)"








df option
Formula
Use case




"satterthwaite" (default)
\(\hat\beta_k \pm t_{\alpha/2,\hat\nu_k} \cdot \mathrm{SD}_{\mathrm{CCC},k}\)
Standard Three-Layer Calibration


"naive"
\(\hat\beta_k \pm t_{\alpha/2,J-K} \cdot \mathrm{SD}_{\mathrm{CCC},k}\)
When Satterthwaite unavailable


"normal"
Quantile-based from posterior draws
Legacy CCC-q (v0.4.0)



Fallback chain. "satterthwaite" → "naive" (if df unavailable) → "normal" (if beta skipped).


11.5.4 summary.bm_ccc()
Produces the full calibration report:
summary(cal)

#>   K = 2 fixed effects, J = 20 studies, S = 4000 posterior draws

11  CCC Implementation and Inferential Outputs

title: 11  CCC Implementation and Inferential Outputs
text:
11.6 Diagnostics and Troubleshooting

11.6.1 Key diagnostic quantities
Frobenius norm \(\|A_\beta - I_K\|_F\): measures total CCC departure from identity.
Per-parameter SD ratio \(R_k = \mathrm{SD}_{\mathrm{CCC},k} / \mathrm{SD}_{\mathrm{MCMC},k}\):



Table 11.6: \(R_k\) interpretation guide.










\(R_k\) Range
Interpretation
Typical Scenario




\(0.95\)–\(1.05\)
Near-identity
Well-estimated (high df, many clusters)


\(1.05\)–\(1.30\)
Moderate expansion
Moderate df, CCC adjusting


\(> 1.30\)
Large expansion
Low df, prior dominates posterior


\(< 0.95\)
Shrinkage
Posterior wider than CR2






Direction field: reported as narrower (\(R_k < 0.99\)), wider (\(R_k > 1.01\)), ~same (\(|R_k - 1| \le 0.01\)), or skipped.


11.6.2 Troubleshooting guide






WarningTroubleshooting Guide



“My \(R_k > 1.5\) for a coefficient.” CCC is substantially expanding the posterior. Check df_satt: if \(\hat\nu_k < 5\), the widening is expected. Consider reducing model complexity.
“method = fallback_raw” in the summary.** Beta-block CCC was skipped. Common causes: \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) not PD, or \(J \le K\). See Section 8.6.
“beta_skipped = TRUE”. Programmatic equivalent of method = "fallback_raw". coef() returns uncalibrated means; confint() forced to "normal". Remedy: reduce \(K\) or increase \(J\) to satisfy \(J \ge 2K + 2\).
“Satterthwaite df not available” warning. Sandwich was computed with small_sample != "CR2". Recompute: sandwich(fit, small_sample = "CR2").
CHE: “Study effects analytically integrated out.” Informational, not an error. Use type = "beta" for fixed effects.




11.6.3 Interpreting the verification block
The six checks (V1–V6) are algebraic consistency checks. “PASS” means internal consistency; “FAIL” signals extreme Monte Carlo noise. If V2/V3 fail: increase \(S\) via iter_sampling. If V1 fails: this is a hard error (mean-preservation should hold to machine precision).

11  CCC Implementation and Inferential Outputs

title: 12  Verification
text:
12.1 Theorem-to-Code Verification Map
Software correctness in bayesRVE rests on a layered verification strategy that connects every mathematical claim to executable tests. This chapter presents the complete theorem-to-code verification map, describes each verification tier, and documents the acceptance criteria that gate every release.
The test suite comprises 2,701 passing assertions organized into five tiers of increasing realism: algebraic invariants on synthetic data (Tier 0), known-answer benchmarks against closed-form solutions (Tier 1), parity benchmarks against external software (Tier 2), live-fit integration tests (Tier 3), and full-pipeline acceptance gates.
Table 12.1 provides a comprehensive mapping from every verifiable mathematical property to its implementation site, the test that exercises it, and the evidence level achieved. Each row answers three questions: where is the property implemented, how is it tested, and how tight is the tolerance?
The map reveals a deliberate design: algebraic properties that hold exactly in infinite precision are tested at machine precision (\(\sim 2.2 \times 10^{-16}\), with a practical tolerance of \(\sim 2.2 \times 10^{-13}\)), while properties that involve MCMC sampling noise are tested at 5–15% tolerance. No property falls through without a test.

title: 12  Verification
text:
12.2 Algebraic Verification (Tier 0)
Tier 0 tests verify CCC invariants on fixed, deterministic inputs with no MCMC stochasticity. Because there is no sampling noise, deviations from the theoretical values expose implementation bugs rather than statistical variability. A Tier 0 failure is a release blocker.

12.2.1 The six-point verification suite
Every call to ccc() internally invokes .verify_ccc(), which checks six properties:



Table 12.2: The .verify_ccc() six-point verification suite.











Check
Property
Severity
Tolerance




V1
Center preservation: \(\bar{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^* = \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\)
Error on fail
\(10^{-6}\)


V2
Beta diagonal variance: \(\operatorname{Var}(\beta_k^*)/[V_{\mathrm{CR2}}]_{kk} \in [0.95, 1.05]\)
Warning on fail
5%


V3
Theta variance: \(\operatorname{Var}(\theta_j^*)/V_{\mathrm{cond},j} \in [0.95, 1.05]\)
Warning on fail
5%


V4
Beta covariance (full matrix): \(\|\operatorname{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\beta}^*) - V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\|_F / \|V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\|_F < 0.05\)
Warning on fail
5% (relative Frobenius)


V5
Aggregate center (alias of V1)
Error on fail
\(10^{-6}\)


V6
Diagnostic consistency: \(\|A_\beta - I_K\|_F\) consistent with Frobenius norm
Error on fail
\(10^{-6}\)






The distinction between error and warning severity reflects the distinction between algebraic identities (V1, V5, V6), which must hold at machine precision, and stochastic properties (V2, V3, V4), which depend on \(S\) and may deviate at small sample sizes.


12.2.2 CHE Tier 0 invariants
The CHE-specific Tier 0 suite (test-che-cr2-tier0.R) tests four invariants across three scenarios (\(J = 10, K = 1\); \(J = 15, K = 2\); \(J = 20, K = 2\)):
T0-1: No fallback to scalar. The CR2 pipeline must complete without falling back to the scalar correction. Each scenario verifies sw$small_sample == "CR2" and sw$fallback == FALSE.
T0-2: CR2 differs from HC0. The bias-corrected variance \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) must not be identical to the uncorrected \(V_{\mathrm{HC0}}\). Additional sub-checks confirm symmetry (\(\|V - V^T\| < 10^{-12}\)), positive definiteness (all eigenvalues \(> 0\)), and that CR2 inflates relative to HC0 (\([V_{\mathrm{CR2}}]_{kk} \geq 0.99 \cdot [V_{\mathrm{HC0}}]_{kk}\)).
T0-3: Satterthwaite degrees of freedom are populated. Each coefficient must have a finite, positive df value, and the df source must report "satterthwaite". An upper bound check ensures \(\mathrm{df}_k < 10 \cdot J\) as a sanity guard.
T0-4: No theta leakage in CHE output. The CHE model does not expose individual study random effects in its summary output. This is verified both structurally (summary$theta == NULL) and by scanning printed output for forbidden tokens ("theta", "Conditional", "widened", "narrowed").
These invariants are enforced across all three scenarios, producing a total of 19 test assertions in the T0 suite alone.
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

title: 12  Verification
text:
12.3 Known-Answer and Parity Benchmarks (Tier 1–2)

12.3.1 Tier 1: Algebraic known-answer tests
Tier 1 tests isolate individual computational components with fixed (non-MCMC) inputs and compare them against closed-form analytical solutions.
T1-01: Singleton formula. For a study with \(n_j = 1\), the weight matrix reduces to a scalar: \(W_j = 1/(\bar{s}_j^2 + \omega^2 + \tau^2)\). This is verified across a grid of 27 parameter combinations (\(\bar{s}_j^2 \in \{0.03, 0.07, 0.12\}\), \(\tau^2 \in \{0, 0.02, 0.10\}\), \(\omega^2 \in \{0, 0.01, 0.05\}\)) at machine precision.
T1-02: Study-level variance formula. For the study-level (cluster-constant) path, the marginal variance \(v_j = (\bar{s}_j^2 + \omega^2)/n_j + \tau^2\) is verified by constructing a minimal bm_data object and comparing W_list[[j]] against \(1/v_j\). The bread matrix \(Q = (X'WX)^{-1}\) is independently recomputed and compared at \(10^{-10}\) tolerance.
T1-03: Singleton \(W^{1/2}\). The analytical Woodbury formula for \(W_j^{1/2}\) (Decision D24) is verified at \(n_j = 1\) by checking \(W^{1/2} W^{1/2} = W_j\) and \(W^{1/2} W^{-1/2} = I\) at machine precision.


12.3.2 Tier 1: D25 plug-in parity
Decision D25 mandates that when REML point estimates of \(\tau^2\) and \(\omega^2\) are injected into bayesRVE’s CR2 helpers, the resulting \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) must match clubSandwich’s (Pustejovsky & Tipton, 2022) output at \(10^{-6}\) tolerance. This isolates the linear algebra from MCMC noise and serves as the strongest available external cross-validation.
The D25 test protocol:

Generate synthetic data with constant \(v_{ij}\) within each study (ensuring \(\bar{s}_j^2 = v_{ij}\) exactly, eliminating the averaging discrepancy).
Fit a CHE model in metafor (Viechtbauer, 2010) (rma.mv with ~ 1 | studyid/obsid, REML).
Extract \(\hat\tau^2_{\mathrm{REML}}\), \(\hat\omega^2_{\mathrm{REML}}\), and \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\mathrm{REML}}\).
Compute clubSandwich’s \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) and Satterthwaite df as the reference.
Inject the same REML estimates into bayesRVE’s internal helpers (.compute_wls_bread(), .compute_cr2_adjustments(), .compute_vcr2(), .satterthwaite_df()).
Compare element-by-element.

Four sub-tests cover this:



Table 12.3: D25 plug-in parity tests.











Test
Scenario
Target
Tolerance




T1-04a
\(J = 15\), \(K = 2\), balanced
\(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\)
\(10^{-6}\)


T1-04b
\(J = 15\), \(K = 2\), balanced
Satterthwaite df
0.1


T1-04c
\(J = 25\), \(K = 3\), unbalanced
\(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) and df
\(10^{-6}\) / 0.1


T1-04d
\(J = 15\), \(K = 2\), balanced
Bread \(Q\)
\(10^{-10}\)






The \(10^{-6}\) tolerance on \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) is six orders of magnitude tighter than the MCMC noise floor, confirming that the two implementations share the same linear algebra. The slightly looser tolerance on df (0.1) reflects minor numerical differences in the matrix derivative computations used by the Satterthwaite approximation.


12.3.3 Tier 2: Live-fit comparison
Tier 2 tests fit the same data with both bayesRVE (bm_fit + sandwich) and metafor + clubSandwich, then compare the sandwich standard errors and Satterthwaite degrees of freedom. Unlike D25, these comparisons do not inject REML point estimates—bayesRVE uses posterior means of \(\tau^2\) (and \(\omega^2\)), introducing a legitimate discrepancy.
The Tier 2 tolerance is 15% for standard errors and 3 for degrees of freedom. These bounds reflect three sources of expected disagreement:

Bread difference. bayesRVE centers at the posterior mean \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\mathrm{Bayes}}\); metafor uses REML \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\mathrm{REML}}\). The weights \(W_j\) differ because \(\hat\tau^2_{\mathrm{Bayes}} \neq \hat\tau^2_{\mathrm{REML}}\).
Within-study variance averaging. bayesRVE’s CE model uses \(\bar{s}_j^2 = n_j^{-1} \sum_i v_{ij}\); metafor’s observation-level likelihood uses individual \(v_{ij}\). These are asymptotically equivalent but differ at finite sample sizes.
Residual centering. bayesRVE computes residuals from posterior mean estimates; clubSandwich from REML estimates.

The Tier 2 tests (test-cr2-satterthwaite.R, Tests 1–2) confirm that bayesRVE’s sandwich SEs fall within 15% of clubSandwich on balanced data (\(J = 15\), \(n_j = 3\)) and that Satterthwaite df agree within 3 on moderately larger data (\(J = 20\), \(n_j = 5\)).
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

title: 12  Verification
text:
12.4 Reduction Checks
Gate 1 verifies that CHE with \(\omega^2 = 0\) reduces exactly to CE at machine precision. This is the strongest structural evidence that the CHE generalization is correct, because it confirms that every CHE-specific code path degenerates to the CE code path when the additional variance component vanishes.

12.4.1 The Gate 1 protocol
The test file test-gate1-omega0-equivalence.R defines a synthetic dataset with \(J = 8\) studies, \(K = 2\) predictors, and known sampling variances. No MCMC is involved: the tests call internal helper functions directly with fixed parameter values (\(\tau^2 = 0.04\), \(\boldsymbol{\beta} = (0.3, -0.1)\)).
The machine precision tolerance is \(\epsilon_{\mathrm{mach}} \times 1000 \approx 2.2 \times 10^{-13}\), tight enough to detect any implementation error while accommodating floating-point rounding.


12.4.2 Gate 1 assertions



Table 12.4: Gate 1 reduction assertions. G1-01 through G1-09 traverse the full CR2 pipeline from \(W_j\) to df.












Test
Component
CE Call
CHE Call (\(\omega^2 = 0\))
Assertion




G1-01
\(W_j\)
.compute_W_j(s, \tau^2, n)
.compute_W_j(s, \tau^2, n, \omega^2\!=\!0)$ | Element-wise $\approx$ at $\epsilon_{\mathrm{mach}} \times 10$ | | G1-01b | $W_j$ (separation) |.compute_W_j(s, ^2, n)$
.compute_W_j(s, \tau^2, n, \omega^2\!=\!0.03)$ | Strict $\neq$ | | G1-02 | Bread $Q$ (obs-level) |.compute_wls_bread(, ^2, K, J)$


G1-03
Bread \(Q\) (study-level)
Same, cluster_constant = TRUE
Same + \(\omega^2 = 0\)
\(Q\), \(W_{\mathrm{list}}\) match


G1-04
Hat blocks \(H_j\)
.compute_hat_blocks()
Same inputs from CHE bread
\(H_j\) match at \(2.2 \times 10^{-13}\)


G1-05
CR2 adjustments \(A_j\)
.compute_cr2_adjustments()$ | Same inputs from CHE bread | $A_j$ match at $2.2 \times 10^{-13}$ | | G1-06 | $V_{\mathrm{CR2}}$ |.compute_vcr2()$
Same with CHE inputs
Full matrix match at \(2.2 \times 10^{-13}\)


G1-07
Satterthwaite df
.satterthwaite_df()$ | Same with CHE inputs | Vector match at $2.2 \times 10^{-13}$ | | G1-08 | $W^{1/2}$ squaring (CE) |.compute_W_j_sqrt()| $W^{1/2} W^{1/2} = W_j$ | $\epsilon_{\mathrm{mach}} \times 100$ | | G1-09 | $W^{1/2}$ squaring (CHE) |.compute_W_j_sqrt()` with \(\omega^2 > 0\)
\(W^{1/2} W^{1/2} = W_j\)
\(\epsilon_{\mathrm{mach}} \times 100\)






The Gate 1 tests exercise both the observation-level path (cluster_constant = FALSE) and the study-level path (cluster_constant = TRUE), and cover a grid of \(\bar{s}_j^2 \times n_j\) combinations in G1-01. Including the G1-01b separation check, the suite contains over 75 individual assertions.


12.4.3 Why Gate 1 is decisive
The CHE CR2 implementation shares all code paths with CE CR2; the only difference is that CHE passes omega2_hat to .compute_W_j(), which absorbs it into the effective sampling variance \(a_j = \bar{s}_j^2 + \omega^2\). Gate 1 confirms that setting \(\omega^2 = 0\) makes the CHE path algebraically identical to the CE path. If the CHE code introduced any extraneous computation, numerical drift, or incorrect branching, the machine-precision tolerance would immediately detect it.
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

title: 12  Verification
text:
12.5 Interoperability with metafor and clubSandwich
bayesRVE provides two utilities for bridging with the frequentist meta-analysis ecosystem, specifically metafor (Viechtbauer, 2010) and clubSandwich (Pustejovsky & Tipton, 2022).

12.5.1 compare_with_cr2()
This diagnostic function fits the same data with metafor::rma.mv() (REML) and computes clubSandwich::vcovCR() (CR2), then produces a side-by-side comparison of standard errors. The output includes:

Per-coefficient bayesRVE SE and clubSandwich SE
Relative difference \((\mathrm{SE}_{\mathrm{bayes}} - \mathrm{SE}_{\mathrm{club}})/\mathrm{SE}_{\mathrm{club}}\)
Variance component comparison (\(\hat\tau^2_{\mathrm{Bayes}}\) vs \(\hat\tau^2_{\mathrm{REML}}\))
The metafor fit object for user inspection

The function handles both CE and CHE models and automatically constructs the appropriate metafor call.


12.5.2 from_metafor()
This utility extracts data from an existing metafor fit into the format expected by bm_fit(), enabling users to transition from a frequentist to a Bayesian analysis without reformatting their data.


12.5.3 Expected discrepancies
As documented in the B2 benchmark (v0.3.0), bayesRVE’s CE model operates on study-level sufficient statistics while metafor uses the observation-level likelihood. These are asymptotically equivalent but differ at finite sample sizes. The canonical discrepancy is 10–15% in SE and up to 3 in Satterthwaite df.
Three specific sources contribute:

Point estimates. Posterior mean \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) and \(\hat\tau^2\) differ from REML estimates, especially at small \(J\).
Variance averaging. bayesRVE uses \(\bar{s}_j^2\); metafor uses individual \(v_{ij}\). This matters when \(v_{ij}\) varies substantially within studies.
CR2 adjustment matrices. The \(A_j\) matrices depend on the hat matrix, which in turn depends on the weights. Different \(\hat\tau^2\) values produce different \(A_j\).

The D25 plug-in parity test (Section 12.3) eliminates sources 1 and 3 by injecting REML values, confirming that the remaining discrepancy is zero to \(10^{-6}\). This isolates source 2 as the sole contributor to live-fit differences, and source 2 vanishes when \(v_{ij}\) is constant within studies.
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

12.6.1 What each gate proves
Gate 1 proves that CHE is a correct generalization of CE. It does not prove that CE itself is correct—that requires D25 and the Tier 2 comparison.
Tier 0 proves that the CCC transformation has the claimed algebraic properties (center preservation, covariance matching, identity limit). It does not prove that the sandwich target \(V_{\mathrm{CR2}}\) is correctly computed—that is the role of D25.
D25 (Tier 1) proves that bayesRVE’s CR2 linear algebra is identical to clubSandwich’s when given the same inputs. It does not prove that posterior-mean centering yields valid frequentist inference—that is a statistical claim validated by the simulation study (sim3).
Tier 2 proves that the full pipeline (MCMC + sandwich + CCC) produces results in the same ballpark as the frequentist standard. The 15% tolerance is a consequence of using posterior means rather than REML, not a sign of implementation error.
Gate 3 proves that Three-Layer Calibration works on real data at the scale and complexity of a published meta-analysis. It does not prove coverage guarantees—those come from simulation.


12.6.2 Verification is not validation
The verification framework documented in this chapter establishes that the software correctly implements the mathematical specification. It does not establish that the mathematical specification is statistically appropriate for all meta-analytic settings. Validation—the question of whether CCC-t confidence intervals achieve nominal coverage across realistic data-generating processes—is addressed through the simulation study (sim3).
The distinction matters: a perfectly verified implementation of a flawed method would pass all tests in this chapter but fail the simulation benchmarks. Conversely, a correct method with a buggy implementation would fail Tier 0 or D25 long before reaching the simulation stage. The layered strategy ensures that each tier catches a distinct class of problems, and that no single tier is asked to do more than it can.
crumbs: Part II: Software Realization and Verification

title: 12  Verification
text:
12.7 Simulation Validation Bridge (sim3)
The verification tiers above establish that bayesRVE correctly implements its mathematical specification. They cannot establish that the specification itself yields valid frequentist inference. That question—whether CCC-\(t\) confidence intervals achieve nominal 95% coverage across realistic data-generating processes—is answered by the sim3 comprehensive simulation study.

12.7.1 Design
sim3 comprises 24 conditions with 500 replications each (11,600 total fits), organized into four scenarios.



Table 12.6: sim3 design: 24 conditions across four scenarios testing correct specification (S1), misspecification (S2–S3), and outlier robustness (S4).

12.7.2 Coverage Results



Table 12.7: sim3 coverage summary at the 95% nominal level.





Method
CE (16 conditions)
CHE (8 conditions)




CCC-\(t\) (Three-Layer)
93.4%–96.4%

CR2 (frequentist reference)
92.8%–96.4%

Three findings are central. First, CCC-\(t\) closely tracks CR2 coverage across all 24 conditions, confirming that the Bayesian calibrated posterior provides frequentist-valid inference. Second, Layer 3 is essential at small \(J\): CCC-\(q\) undercoverage reaches 91.8% at \(J = 10\), motivating Satterthwaite df as the default in confint.bm_ccc(). Third, the CCC calibration consistently shrinks the posterior (\(R_k < 1\)), but the heavier \(t\)-distribution tails compensate, achieving nominal coverage.


12.7.3 Score Parity Test (Planned)
A planned Tier 1 test will verify that the Stan generated-quantities score_beta[j,k] and the R-side analytic score computation in sandwich.R produce identical values at fixed parameter values. The test protocol uses cmdstanr’s $generate_quantities() method to evaluate the GQ block without MCMC, then compares element-wise against the R-side Woodbury-based score at \(10^{-10}\) tolerance. This would close the last gap in the score-to-sandwich verification chain: D25 verifies downstream CR2 algebra, but the upstream score matrix has not yet been cross-validated across languages.






Pustejovsky, J. E., & Tipton, E. (2022). Meta-analysis with robust variance estimation: Expanding the range of working models. Prevention Science, 23(3), 425–438. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11121-021-01246-3


Viechtbauer, W. (2010). Conducting meta-analyses in R with the metafor package. Journal of Statistical Software, 36(3), 1–48. https://doi.org/10.18637/jss.v036.i03
