title: 13  Real-Data Validation: Experimental Design
text:
13.1 The Tanner-Smith & Lipsey (2015) Dataset
This chapter documents the experimental design of the real-data validation study used to evaluate bayesRVE’s Three-Layer Calibration against established frequentist robust variance estimation methods. The validation uses a well-characterized meta-analytic dataset that has served as a benchmark in the RVE literature, enabling direct comparison against published results.

title: 13  Real-Data Validation: Experimental Design
text:
13.1.1 Overview
The validation dataset comes from the meta-analysis by Tanner-Smith & Lipsey (2015), which synthesized evidence on the effectiveness of brief alcohol interventions for adolescents and young adults. This dataset was subsequently reanalyzed by Pustejovsky & Tipton (2022) as a primary showcase for robust variance estimation methods in meta-analysis with dependent effect sizes, making it an ideal benchmark for evaluating bayesRVE.


13.1.2 Data Dimensions
After applying the inclusion filters from Pustejovsky & Tipton (2022)—removing observations with missing values on percoll, attrition_all, or permale—the analytic sample comprises:



Dimension
Value




Total effect sizes (\(N\))
1,198


Independent studies (\(J\))
107


Effect size metric
Standardized mean difference (Hedges’ \(g\))


Sampling variances
Reported or estimated via standard formulas


Effect sizes per study
1 to 45 (median approximately 5)



A note on study counts: the original Tanner-Smith & Lipsey (2015) publication reports a larger sample. The \(J = 107\) figure reflects the analytic subset after the Pustejovsky & Tipton (2022) filtering criteria are applied and studies with missing covariate data are excluded. In the model design matrix, some sources report \(J = 117\) unique study clusters because certain studies contribute effect sizes across multiple dependent variable categories, creating additional effective clusters in the cross-classified structure.


13.1.3 Dependency Structure
The dataset exhibits substantial within-study dependence: individual studies contribute between 1 and 45 effect sizes, arising because a single intervention trial can measure multiple outcomes (e.g., frequency of use, quantity of use, peak consumption) at multiple follow-up time points. This dependence structure makes robust variance estimation essential for valid statistical inference, as standard methods that assume independence would understate uncertainty.


13.1.4 Rationale for Dataset Selection
The Tanner-Smith & Lipsey (2015) dataset was selected for the following reasons:

Large number of studies. With \(J = 107\) independent studies, the dataset falls well within bayesRVE’s operating envelope (\(J_{\min} = \max(8, 2K + 2)\); see Chapter 8) for all moderator specifications considered, ensuring that CR2 corrections and Satterthwaite degrees of freedom are well-identified.
Heterogeneous cluster sizes. The range of 1 to 45 effect sizes per study exercises the sandwich estimator across a variety of cluster configurations, from singletons to large clusters.
Established benchmark. Published results from Pustejovsky & Tipton (2022) using robumeta and clubSandwich provide a ground truth against which bayesRVE output can be directly compared.
Substantive moderators. The dataset includes both categorical moderators (dependent variable category, control type) and continuous covariates, enabling validation of bayesRVE’s CCC machinery under realistic meta-regression specifications with \(K > 1\) predictors.

title: 13  Real-Data Validation: Experimental Design
text:
13.2 Model Specifications
Two moderator specifications are fit to the dataset, each using a cell-means parameterization (no intercept term, no reference category). This parameterization directly estimates the conditional mean effect size for each category level, facilitating interpretable comparisons across all groups.

13.2.1 DV Category Model
The DV Category model estimates mean effect sizes for six outcome categories, adjusted for five continuous covariates:
\[
g_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{6} \beta_k \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{DV}_{ij} = k]
       + \beta_7 \cdot \text{postwks\_c}_{ij}
       + \beta_8 \cdot \text{postwks\_long}_{ij}
       + \beta_9 \cdot \text{percoll\_c}_{ij}
       + \beta_{10} \cdot \text{permale\_c}_{ij}
       + \beta_{11} \cdot \text{attrition\_c}_{ij}
       + u_j + \varepsilon_{ij}
\]
The six DV categories are:

BAC (blood alcohol concentration)
Combined measures
Frequency of heavy use
Frequency of use
Peak consumption
Quantity of use

This yields \(K = 11\) total regression coefficients (6 category indicators + 5 covariates).


13.2.2 Control Type Model
The Control Type model replaces the DV category indicators with four control-type indicators, using the same five continuous covariates:
\[
g_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{4} \gamma_k \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{Ctype}_{ij} = k]
       + \gamma_5 \cdot \text{postwks\_c}_{ij}
       + \gamma_6 \cdot \text{postwks\_long}_{ij}
       + \gamma_7 \cdot \text{percoll\_c}_{ij}
       + \gamma_8 \cdot \text{permale\_c}_{ij}
       + \gamma_9 \cdot \text{attrition\_c}_{ij}
       + u_j + \varepsilon_{ij}
\]
This yields \(K = 9\) total regression coefficients (4 control-type indicators + 5 covariates).


13.2.3 Covariate Definitions
All covariates are centered to improve MCMC sampling efficiency and interpretability:








Covariate
Definition
Centering




postwks_c
Post-treatment follow-up (weeks), capped at 26
Centered at 12 weeks


postwks_long
Indicator for follow-up exceeding 26 weeks
Binary (0/1)


percoll_c
Proportion of college-age participants
Centered at 1.0


permale_c
Proportion male in sample
Centered at 0.5


attrition_c
Overall attrition rate
Centered at sample median





13.2.4 Random Effects Structure
Each model specification is fit under two random-effects structures:

CE (Correlated Effects): A single between-study random effect \(u_j \sim N(0, \tau^2)\), inducing within-study correlation \(\rho = \tau^2/(\bar{s}_j^2 + \tau^2)\) structurally through the compound-symmetric covariance. In bayesRVE, \(\rho_{\text{work}} = 0\) during MCMC (D5); \(\rho\) enters only at the sandwich stage.
CHE (Correlated-Hierarchical Effects): Two random effects—a between-study component \(u_j \sim N(0, \tau^2)\) and a within-study component \(w_{ij} \sim N(0, \omega^2)\)—so that \(\text{Var}(g_{ij}) = v_{ij} + \omega^2 + \tau^2\).

In R formula syntax:
# CE model
bm_fit(es | se(V) ~ 0 + dv_cat + postwks_c + postwks_long +
       percoll_c + permale_c + attrition_c,
       data = TSL15, study = ~ studyid, model = "ce")

# CHE model
bm_fit(es | se(V) ~ 0 + dv_cat + postwks_c + postwks_long +
       percoll_c + permale_c + attrition_c,
       data = TSL15, study = ~ studyid, model = "che")
This yields \(2 \times 2 = 4\) bayesRVE model fits per moderator specification (CE/CHE crossed with DV/Control Type), plus the three frequentist baselines per specification, for a total of 14 fitted models.

title: 13  Real-Data Validation: Experimental Design
text:
13.3 Analysis Settings

13.3.1 MCMC Configuration
All bayesRVE models use the following MCMC settings:








Parameter
Value
Rationale




Chains
4
Standard for convergence assessment via \(\hat{R}\) and split-\(\hat{R}\)


Warmup iterations
2,000
Sufficient for NUTS adaptation (step size, mass matrix)


Sampling iterations
2,000
Yields \(S = 8{,}000\) total posterior draws


Random seed
20260324
Exact reproducibility across reruns


adapt_delta
0.95
Conservative target acceptance rate for meta-analysis models


max_treedepth
12
Standard upper bound on leapfrog trajectory length



The total of \(S = 8{,}000\) posterior draws provides adequate effective sample sizes for all parameters of interest given the relatively low dimensionality of the models (\(K \leq 11\) regression coefficients plus 1–2 variance components).


13.3.2 Sandwich and Calibration Settings








Parameter
Value
Rationale




Working correlation (\(\rho\))
0.6
Recommended by Pustejovsky & Tipton (2022) for social science meta-analyses; sandwich estimator is consistent regardless of \(\rho\) choice


Small-sample correction
CR2
Default (Decision D12); bias-reducing leverage adjustment


Degrees of freedom
Satterthwaite
Default (Decision D13); accounts for estimation uncertainty in variance components


Covariance imputation
metafor::impute_covariance_matrix() with smooth_vi = TRUE (Viechtbauer, 2010)
Constructs \(V\) matrix assuming equicorrelation within studies at \(\rho = 0.6\)





13.3.3 Prior Distributions








Parameter
Prior
Rationale




\(\beta_k\) (regression coefficients)
\(N(0, 10)\)
Weakly informative for standardized mean differences; prior SD of 10 is effectively flat on the SMD scale


\(\tau\) (between-study SD)
Half-Cauchy\((0, 0.5)\)
Standard weakly informative prior for heterogeneity in social science; allows substantial heterogeneity while mildly regularizing


\(\omega\) (within-study SD, CHE only)
Half-Cauchy\((0, 0.5)\)
Same specification as \(\tau\); symmetric treatment of variance components



# CE prior specification
prior_ce <- bm_prior(beta = normal(0, 10), tau = half_cauchy(0, 0.5))

# CHE prior specification
prior_che <- bm_prior(beta = normal(0, 10),
                      tau = half_cauchy(0, 0.5),
                      omega = half_cauchy(0, 0.5))
The choice of Half-Cauchy(0, 0.5) for variance component priors follows the recommendations of Gelman (2006) and Polson & Scott (2012) for hierarchical models. The scale parameter of 0.5 is appropriate for standardized effect sizes where heterogeneity SDs rarely exceed 0.5 in most substantive domains, while the heavy tails of the Cauchy distribution allow the data to dominate when heterogeneity is larger.

title: 13  Real-Data Validation: Experimental Design
text:
13.4 Comparator Estimators
The validation compares five estimators that span the frequentist and Bayesian approaches to meta-analysis with dependent effect sizes. Each estimator pair is chosen to isolate specific sources of variation in the comparison.

13.4.1 Estimator Summary











Estimator
Package
Model
Variance Estimation
df Method
Role




CE robumeta
robumeta::robu
CE working model
CR2 (Tipton, 2015)
Satterthwaite
Published baseline


CE metafor
metafor::rma.mv
CE (\(\tau^2\) only)
CR2 via clubSandwich
Satterthwaite
Fair CE comparison


CHE metafor
metafor::rma.mv
CHE (\(\tau^2 + \omega^2\))
CR2 via clubSandwich
Satterthwaite
Fair CHE comparison


bayesRVE CE
bayesRVE::bm_fit
CE (\(\tau^2\) only)
CCC with CR2 target
Satterthwaite
This package (CE)


bayesRVE CHE
bayesRVE::bm_fit
CHE (\(\tau^2 + \omega^2\))
CCC with CR2 target
Satterthwaite
This package (CHE)





13.4.2 Estimator Descriptions
CE robumeta. The robumeta::robu function implements the correlated-effects working model of Hedges et al. (2010), computing study-level aggregated effect sizes under an assumed equicorrelation \(\rho\), then applying CR2 small-sample corrections following Tipton (2015). This estimator serves as the published baseline, replicating the exact results reported in Pustejovsky & Tipton (2022). Because robumeta performs study-mean aggregation internally, its point estimates and standard errors may differ slightly from observation-level estimators.
CE metafor. The metafor::rma.mv function (Viechtbauer, 2010) fits a single-\(\tau^2\) random-effects model at the observation level using REML, with the imputed covariance matrix \(V\) incorporating the working correlation \(\rho = 0.6\). CR2 standard errors and Satterthwaite degrees of freedom are obtained via the clubSandwich package. This estimator provides a fair comparison target for bayesRVE CE because both operate on the same data structure (observation-level, single random effect) and apply the same CR2 + Satterthwaite inference framework.
CHE metafor. The metafor::rma.mv function with a two-level random structure (~ 1 | studyid / esid) fits the correlated-hierarchical effects model, estimating both \(\tau^2\) (between-study) and \(\omega^2\) (within-study) via REML. CR2 corrections and Satterthwaite degrees of freedom are again obtained via clubSandwich. This estimator is the fair comparison target for bayesRVE CHE.
bayesRVE CE. The bayesRVE::bm_fit function with model = "ce" fits a Bayesian CE model via Stan (Carpenter et al., 2017) (NUTS sampler), estimating posterior distributions for all regression coefficients and \(\tau\). The Cholesky Calibration Correction (CCC) maps posterior draws to have the CR2 sandwich covariance as their dispersion target. Satterthwaite degrees of freedom define the reference distribution for inference (Three-Layer Calibration: L1 = posterior mean, L2 = CR2 sandwich, L3 = Satterthwaite \(t\)).
bayesRVE CHE. The bayesRVE::bm_fit function with model = "che" extends the CE model with a within-study variance component \(\omega^2\). The CHE CCC computation uses \(\hat{\omega}^2 = \text{mean}(\omega^{(s)2})\) (Decision D20) to construct the CR2 sandwich with Woodbury-based \(W^{1/2}\) weights (Decision D24). This estimator is the primary new capability introduced in v0.6.0.


13.4.3 Comparison Logic
The five-estimator design enables the following structured comparisons:

CE robumeta vs. CE metafor: Quantifies the effect of study-mean aggregation (robumeta) versus observation-level modeling (metafor) within the CE framework. Differences in point estimates arise from the distinct weighting schemes.
CE metafor vs. bayesRVE CE: The primary validation comparison for CE models. Both use observation-level data with a single random effect. Differences reflect the Bayesian-vs-REML estimation and the CCC calibration procedure. If CCC is working correctly, the CCC SD should approximate the CR2 SE.
CHE metafor vs. bayesRVE CHE: The primary validation comparison for CHE models, introduced in v0.6.0. Both use two random effects. Agreement validates that the CHE extension of CCC (omega-aware weights, Woodbury \(W^{1/2}\)) produces calibrated inference.
CE vs. CHE (within estimator): Reveals the effect of modeling within-study heterogeneity. In the CE model, \(\tau^2\) absorbs \(\omega^2\), producing larger between-study variance estimates. The CHE model decomposes total heterogeneity, generally yielding similar or slightly different standard errors depending on the relative magnitudes of \(\tau^2\) and \(\omega^2\).

title: 13  Real-Data Validation: Experimental Design
text:
13.5 Reproducibility and Decision Rules

13.5.1 Single-Script Reproducibility
The complete analysis—from data preparation through all 14 model fits to summary tables and figures—is contained in a single R script:
dev/log/201_real-data-v060/00_full_analysis.R
This script loads the raw dataset, applies all filters and transformations, fits all frequentist baselines and bayesRVE models, computes all comparison tables, and saves results to disk. No manual intervention or intermediate files are required. Given the fixed random seed (SEED = 20260324), all MCMC results are exactly reproducible on the same hardware and software configuration.


13.5.2 Pre-Specified Decision Rules
All model specifications, MCMC settings, prior distributions, and evaluation criteria were fixed before running the bayesRVE models. Specifically:

The two moderator specifications (DV Category, Control Type) replicate Pustejovsky & Tipton (2022) exactly.
The covariate centering scheme follows the original publication.
Prior distributions and MCMC settings were chosen based on general recommendations in the literature, not tuned to the TSL15 data.
No post-hoc model selection, predictor selection, or prior adjustment was performed.



13.5.3 Gate 3 Criterion
The primary success criterion for the real-data validation is:
\[
R_{\text{SE},k} = \frac{\text{CCC SD}_k}{\text{CR2 SE}_k} \in [0.9, 1.1]
\quad \text{for all DV category coefficients } k = 1, \ldots, 6
\]
This criterion is evaluated separately for CE and CHE models:

CE: CCC SD from bayesRVE CE vs. CR2 SE from metafor CE (via clubSandwich)
CHE: CCC SD from bayesRVE CHE vs. CR2 SE from metafor CHE (via clubSandwich)

Gate 3 is defined exclusively for the six DV category coefficients (\(k = 1, \ldots, 6\)), which serve as intercepts in the DV-moderator specification and each draw information from a large number of contributing studies. Covariate coefficients (e.g., percoll_c, permale_c) are reported separately in Section 16.1.2 and are not subject to the Gate 3 acceptance band; their CCC/CR2 ratios may fall outside [0.9, 1.1] due to lower effective cluster counts and Satterthwaite df.


13.5.4 Rationale for the [0.9, 1.1] Band
The 10% tolerance band around unity accommodates two sources of discrepancy between the Bayesian CCC standard deviations and the frequentist CR2 standard errors:

Variance-component plug-in discrepancy (primary). The CCC uses the Bayesian posterior mean of variance components (\(\tau^2\), \(\omega^2\)) as plug-in estimates for the weight matrix \(W\) and hence the bread matrix (\(X'WX\)), while the frequentist CR2 uses REML estimates. Because the posterior mean and REML estimate differ due to prior influence, the two methods construct different \(V_{\text{CR2}}\) targets. By Theorem 2, CCC achieves its own \(V_{\text{CR2}}\) exactly; the observed ratio \(\neq 1\) reflects two different targets, not a calibration failure. This typically contributes 1–5% discrepancy.
MCMC sampling noise (secondary). Even with \(S = 8{,}000\) posterior draws, the sample covariance of the calibrated draws has finite Monte Carlo error. For well-behaved posteriors, this contributes approximately 1–2% variation and is reducible by increasing \(S\).

A ratio of exactly 1.0 would indicate perfect calibration; the [0.9, 1.1] band reflects the practical precision achievable given these two sources of variation. This tolerance was established a priori based on experience from the simulation studies (sim3, 24 conditions \(\times\) 500 replications), where CCC-\(t\) coverage achieved 94.5–95.3% nominal 95% coverage with SE ratios in this range.


13.5.5 Supplementary Diagnostics
Beyond the primary Gate 3 criterion, the following diagnostics are examined:

Shrinkage ratio \(R_k\). The ratio of CCC SD to raw posterior SD, quantifying how much the calibration correction adjusts each coefficient’s uncertainty. Values below 1.0 indicate CCC shrinkage (the raw posterior is overestimating uncertainty relative to the sandwich target); values above 1.0 indicate CCC inflation.
Satterthwaite df agreement. The Satterthwaite degrees of freedom from bayesRVE vs. those from clubSandwich, verifying that the independent implementation (Decision D11) produces consistent results.
Point estimate concordance. Bayesian posterior means vs. REML point estimates, confirming that prior influence on the location parameters is minimal given the large sample size.
Variance component comparison. Bayesian posterior medians of \(\tau\) and \(\omega\) vs. REML estimates, documenting the expected prior-induced differences and the \(\tau\)-absorption phenomenon in CE models.







Carpenter, B., Gelman, A., Hoffman, M. D., Lee, D., Goodrich, B., Betancourt, M., Brubaker, M., Guo, J., Li, P., & Riddell, A. (2017). Stan: A probabilistic programming language. Journal of Statistical Software, 76(1), 1–32. https://doi.org/10.18637/jss.v076.i01


Gelman, A. (2006). Prior distributions for variance parameters in hierarchical models (comment on article by Browne and Draper). Bayesian Analysis, 1(3), 515–534. https://doi.org/10.1214/06-BA117A


Hedges, L. V., Tipton, E., & Johnson, M. C. (2010). Robust variance estimation in meta-regression with dependent effect size estimates. Research Synthesis Methods, 1(1), 39–65. https://doi.org/10.1002/jrsm.5


Polson, N. G., & Scott, J. G. (2012). On the half-Cauchy prior for a global scale parameter. Bayesian Analysis, 7(4), 887–902. https://doi.org/10.1214/12-BA730


Pustejovsky, J. E., & Tipton, E. (2022). Meta-analysis with robust variance estimation: Expanding the range of working models. Prevention Science, 23(3), 425–438. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11121-021-01246-3


Tanner-Smith, E. E., & Lipsey, M. W. (2015). Brief alcohol interventions for adolescents and young adults: A systematic review and meta-analysis. Journal of Substance Abuse Treatment, 51, 1–18. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jsat.2014.09.001


Tipton, E. (2015). Small sample adjustments for robust variance estimation with meta-regression. Psychological Methods, 20(3), 375–393. https://doi.org/10.1037/met0000011


Viechtbauer, W. (2010). Conducting meta-analyses in R with the metafor package. Journal of Statistical Software, 36(3), 1–48. https://doi.org/10.18637/jss.v036.i03

title: 14  Results
text:
14.1 MCMC Convergence
This chapter presents the empirical results of applying bayesRVE’s Three-Layer Calibration to the Tanner-Smith & Lipsey (2015) dataset, following the experimental design detailed in Section 13.1. Results are organized in six sections: CE calibration results, CHE calibration results, a combined CCC/CR2 ratio analysis with Gate 3 assessment, Satterthwaite degrees of freedom comparison, variance component analysis, and an interim interpretation summarizing the key findings.
All MCMC results in this chapter are based on converged posterior samples. Diagnostics confirm adequate mixing for both primary models: CE (\(\hat{R}_{\max} = 1.008\), ESS\(_{\text{bulk,min}} = 415\), ESS\(_{\text{tail,min}} = 1{,}109\), divergences \(= 0\)) and CHE (\(\hat{R}_{\max} = 1.005\), ESS\(_{\text{bulk,min}} = 1{,}775\), ESS\(_{\text{tail,min}} = 3{,}064\), divergences \(= 0\)). All parameters satisfy recommended thresholds (\(\hat{R} < 1.01\), ESS \(> 400\); Vehtari et al., 2021). Control-type models show similar diagnostics.

14.2.3 CE Posterior Density Overlay

Figure 14.2: CE tau posterior density. The dashed vertical line marks the metafor REML estimate. The Bayesian posterior mean exceeds REML due to the half-Cauchy(0, 0.5) prior, which places more mass on larger values than the constrained REML optimizer.

title: 14  Results

14.3 CHE Three-Layer Calibration Results
The CHE (Correlated-Hierarchical Effects) model decomposes heterogeneity into between-study (\(\tau^2\)) and within-study (\(\omega^2\)) components. Extending Three-Layer Calibration to the CHE model is the central contribution of bayesRVE v0.6.0. This section presents the CHE calibration results, including the tau-omega joint posterior that reveals the decomposition structure.

Table 14.3 presents the CHE model estimates. Point estimates remain concordant across frequentist and Bayesian approaches. CCC SD tracks CR2 SE closely, confirming that the omega-aware CCC machinery (Decision D20: Jensen-safe \(\hat\omega^2\); Decision D24: Woodbury \(W^{1/2}\)) produces calibrated inference.

Gate 3 verdict: ALL PASS — All 6 DV categories fall within the [0.9, 1.1] acceptance band, confirming that the CHE extension of CCC produces calibrated inference. The Bayesian CCC posterior spread matches the frequentist CR2 sandwich standard error for the CHE model, validating the omega-aware weight construction (Decisions D20 and D24).


14.3.3 CHE Posterior Density Overlay








Figure 14.3: CHE beta posterior overlay: original (blue) vs CCC-calibrated (red) for 6 DV categories. Near-identical densities confirm that CCC acts as a near-identity mapping for the CHE model when coefficients are well-estimated with sufficient clusters.







14.3.4 Tau-Omega Joint Posterior
Figure 14.4 is the signature figure for the CHE analysis. The joint posterior of \(\tau\) and \(\omega\) reveals the decomposition structure, the relationship between variance components, and how the Bayesian estimates relate to the REML point estimates.








Figure 14.4: Joint posterior distribution of tau (between-study SD) and omega (within-study SD) from the CHE DV model. Black contours show posterior density levels. The red cross marks the REML point estimate from metafor. The Bayesian posterior for omega is substantially shrunk toward zero relative to REML, reflecting prior regularization of the within-study component.





Three features of the joint posterior merit attention. First, the posterior mass for \(\tau\) is centered around 0.219, substantially higher than the REML estimate of 0.123, reflecting the half-Cauchy prior’s heavier tail. Second, the posterior for \(\omega\) is centered around 0.061, well below the REML estimate of 0.116, because the half-Cauchy prior induces stronger shrinkage for the within-study component, which is harder to identify with limited per-study replications. Third, the posterior correlation between \(\tau\) and \(\omega\) is near zero (\(r = -0.036\)), indicating that partial confounding between variance components is minimal in this well-identified dataset (\(J = 107\)). The slight negative sign is directionally consistent with the theoretical trade-off between between-study and within-study heterogeneity.


14.3.5 Marginal Posteriors: Tau and Omega
Figure 14.5 provides a clearer view of the individual variance component distributions. The left panel compares \(\tau\) posteriors under CE and CHE, showing how the CE model absorbs all heterogeneity into a single component. The right panel shows the CHE-specific \(\omega\) posterior.








Figure 14.5: Marginal posterior densities. Left: tau for CE (blue) and CHE (red), with REML reference lines. Right: omega for CHE only, with REML reference. The CE model absorbs all heterogeneity into tau; CHE partitions it, yielding a smaller tau and a separate omega.





The CE model has no \(\omega\) parameter: all heterogeneity is absorbed into \(\tau\), producing \(\tau_\text{CE} > \tau_\text{CHE}\). The CHE model partitions heterogeneity into two components, yielding a smaller \(\tau_\text{CHE}\) and a separate \(\omega > 0\). The Bayesian \(\omega\) posterior (median 0.061) is substantially below the REML estimate (0.116), reflecting prior shrinkage that is characteristic of half-Cauchy priors on variance components near the identification boundary.

title: 14  Results
text:
14.4 CCC/CR2 Ratio Analysis
This section consolidates the CE and CHE ratio analyses into a unified assessment. The combined view provides the definitive Gate 3 evaluation for bayesRVE v0.6.0 on the TSL15 dataset.

14.4.1 Combined Ratio Bar Chart with Gate 3 Band
Figure 14.6 displays all 12 CCC/CR2 ratios (6 DV categories \(\times\) 2 models) with the Gate 3 acceptance band. This is the primary validation figure for the technical report.








Figure 14.6: CCC SD / CR2 SE ratio for each DV category, separately for CE (blue) and CHE (red). Segments show deviation from parity (1.0). The green band marks the Gate 3 acceptance range [0.9, 1.1]. All 12 ratios fall within the band, confirming calibration success for both models.







14.4.2 SE Scatter: CCC SD vs CR2 SE
Figure 14.7 provides a complementary view: each point represents a DV category, with CR2 SE on the horizontal axis and CCC SD on the vertical axis. Points near the dashed diagonal indicate agreement.








Figure 14.7: CCC SD vs CR2 SE for each DV category, faceted by model. Points near the dashed diagonal indicate agreement between Bayesian CCC and frequentist CR2. Labels identify each DV category.







14.4.3 Grand Summary Table
Table 14.5 consolidates the key validation metrics for both models.

The CHE ratios show slightly wider dispersion, consistent with the additional variance component estimation introducing modest MCMC variability, but all remain within the acceptance band.

title: 14  Results
text:
14.5 Satterthwaite df Comparison
Satterthwaite degrees of freedom define the reference distribution for Three-Layer Calibration’s Layer 3 (\(t\)-based inference). This section examines whether bayesRVE’s independently-implemented Satterthwaite df (Decision D11: no clubSandwich runtime dependency) produces consistent results with the frequentist implementation.

14.5.1 df Scatter: bayesRVE vs metafor
Figure 14.8 plots the Satterthwaite df from bayesRVE against those from metafor + clubSandwich for each DV category under both models.








Figure 14.8: Satterthwaite df: bayesRVE vs metafor for CE (blue) and CHE (red). Points above the diagonal indicate bayesRVE reports higher df; below indicates lower. Systematic deviations arise from differences in plug-in variance components (posterior mean vs REML).







14.5.2 Why Degrees of Freedom Differ
The Satterthwaite df depends on the Hat matrix \(H = X(X'WX)^{-1}X'W\), which in turn depends on the working weights \(W\). bayesRVE and metafor use different plug-in variance component estimates to construct \(W\), and these differences propagate through the entire \(W \to H \to \text{CR2 adjustment} \to \text{Satterthwaite df}\) pipeline.
Table 14.6 documents the plug-in variance components used by each method for the DV category model.




Table 14.6: Plug-in Variance Components Used in Weight Construction (DV Category Model)






Component
metafor (REML)
bayesRVE (posterior mean)




tau (CE)
0.141
0.223


tau (CHE)
0.123
0.220


omega (CHE)
0.116
0.061








The larger Bayesian \(\hat\tau\) increases the relative contribution of between-study variance to the working model, altering study-level leverage values and thus the effective degrees of freedom. The smaller Bayesian \(\hat\omega\) (prior shrinkage) further modifies the CHE weight structure. These differences are expected and do not indicate a calibration failure: the df discrepancy is a natural consequence of using Bayesian rather than REML point estimates for plug-in quantities, and it is exactly this difference that Layer 3 of the calibration pipeline is designed to handle.
The key observation is that the direction of the CCC SD adjustment (via \(L_2\)) and the magnitude of the df adjustment (via the Satterthwaite formula) are jointly determined by the same plug-in variance components. When \(\hat\tau_\text{Bayes} > \hat\tau_\text{REML}\), both the sandwich variance and the degrees of freedom shift in compensating directions, maintaining the overall calibration of the CCC-\(t\) interval.

text:

Understanding the variance component estimates is essential for interpreting both the CCC mechanism and the substantive findings. This section compares \(\tau\) and \(\omega\) estimates across all five estimators.

14.6.2 Variance Components Bar Chart
Figure 14.9 visualizes the variance component structure across models, illustrating the tau-absorption phenomenon in CE models and the decomposition in CHE models.








Figure 14.9: Variance component estimates (SD scale) across all models. CE models estimate only tau (which absorbs omega). CHE models decompose heterogeneity into between-study (tau) and within-study (omega) components. Bayesian tau estimates exceed REML due to prior influence; Bayesian omega is shrunk below REML.







14.6.3 Interpretation
The variance component comparison reveals three systematic patterns.
Tau absorption in CE models. The CE model has a single between-study random effect (\(\tau^2\)) that must account for all sources of heterogeneity beyond sampling error, including both genuine between-study variation and within-study variation across dependent effect sizes. As a result, \(\tau_\text{CE} > \tau_\text{CHE}\) for both frequentist and Bayesian estimators. The magnitude of this inflation depends on the true \(\omega^2\): when within-study heterogeneity is substantial, the CE \(\tau\) estimate absorbs a correspondingly larger share.
Bayesian tau exceeds REML. Across both CE and CHE models, the Bayesian posterior mean of \(\tau\) exceeds the REML point estimate. This reflects the half-Cauchy(0, 0.5) prior, which has heavier tails than the implicit REML constraint (which truncates the likelihood at the boundary \(\tau^2 = 0\)). The half-Cauchy prior places more mass on larger values of \(\tau\), pulling the posterior mean upward relative to REML, especially when the data provide moderate (rather than overwhelming) information about the between-study variance.
Bayesian omega shrunk below REML. The CHE Bayesian \(\omega\) posterior is centered below the REML estimate. The within-study variance component is harder to identify than the between-study component because its estimation relies on within-study replication, which varies substantially across studies. The half-Cauchy prior induces more pronounced shrinkage for \(\omega\) than for \(\tau\) because the data provide less information to overcome the prior’s pull toward zero.

title: 14  Results
text:
14.7 Interim Interpretation
The empirical results from the TSL15 DV category specification yield six key findings.

CCC/CR2 ratio near unity for both models. The mean ratio is 0.9976 for CE and 0.9953 for CHE, with all 12 individual ratios falling within the pre-specified [0.9, 1.1] acceptance band. This confirms that Cholesky Calibration Correction successfully maps the Bayesian posterior to have the CR2 sandwich variance as its dispersion target.
Gate 3: PASS. Both CE and CHE models pass the pre-specified Gate 3 criterion on all 6 DV category coefficients. The CHE result validates the v0.6.0 extension of CCC to models with two variance components. Gate 3 does not apply to covariate coefficients, whose CCC/CR2 ratios may exceed the [0.9, 1.1] band due to lower effective cluster counts (see Section 16.1.2 for details).
Point estimate concordance. Bayesian posterior means and frequentist REML estimates are nearly identical for all regression coefficients, confirming that prior influence on location parameters is negligible given the large sample (\(J = 107\), \(N = 1{,}198\)).
Satterthwaite df differ systematically. bayesRVE and metafor produce different Satterthwaite degrees of freedom because they use different plug-in variance components (\(\hat\tau_\text{Bayes} > \hat\tau_\text{REML}\); \(\hat\omega_\text{Bayes} < \hat\omega_\text{REML}\)). This is expected and does not compromise calibration: the CCC SD and df adjustments are jointly determined and compensate for each other.
Tau-omega decomposition reveals prior shrinkage structure. The CHE joint posterior shows near-zero correlation between \(\tau\) and \(\omega\) (\(r = -0.036\)), indicating minimal confounding in this well-identified dataset, with \(\omega\) substantially shrunk below REML. This prior shrinkage is scientifically meaningful: it expresses genuine uncertainty about whether within-study heterogeneity is present, given that \(\omega\) is harder to identify than \(\tau\).
CCC as near-identity for well-estimated parameters. For the 6 DV category coefficients (high effective df, many contributing clusters), the CCC calibration ratio \(R_k\) is close to 1.0 in both models. CCC has minimal work to do when the Bayesian posterior already approximates the CR2 sandwich variance.

These findings establish the empirical foundation for Three-Layer Calibration. The next chapter (Chapter 16) examines the CCC mechanism in greater depth, analyzing the \(R_k\) gradient, the multivariate transformation structure, and the three-layer interval width decomposition that reveals how each calibration layer contributes to the final inference.






Tanner-Smith, E. E., & Lipsey, M. W. (2015). Brief alcohol interventions for adolescents and young adults: A systematic review and meta-analysis. Journal of Substance Abuse Treatment, 51, 1–18. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jsat.2014.09.001

title: 15  CCC Mechanics and Limitations
text:
16 CCC Mechanics: Deep Analysis
This chapter examines the internal mechanics of Cholesky Calibration Correction through six complementary lenses: cross-model estimator contrasts, the \(R_k\) calibration diagnostic, interval-width decomposition, the correlation structure of \(V_{\text{CR2}}\), the control-type supplementary specification, and an honest assessment of what the TSL15 analysis does and does not validate. All results are computed from the v0.6.0 real-data analysis of the Tanner-Smith & Lipsey (2015) dataset.

title: 15  CCC Mechanics and Limitations
text:
16.1 Five-Estimator Cross-Model Contrasts
The TSL15 dataset was analyzed under five estimation approaches: three frequentist (CE via robumeta, CE via metafor with CR2, CHE via metafor with CR2) and two Bayesian (bayesRVE CE with CCC-t, bayesRVE CHE with CCC-t). Comparing these estimators side by side isolates the contribution of each modeling decision.

Four contrasts within this table are particularly informative.
Contrast 1: CE robumeta vs CE metafor. Both implement correlated-effects working models, but differ in two respects. First, robumeta uses study-level aggregation with inverse-variance weights on study means, while metafor fits an observation-level model with a working correlation structure. Second, the small-sample corrections differ (robumeta’s HTZ adjustment vs. metafor’s CR2 via clubSandwich). Point estimates agree closely because both target the same marginal estimand; SE differences reflect both the aggregation/weighting distinction and the distinct correction strategies.
Contrast 2: CE metafor vs CHE metafor. Moving from CE to CHE partitions between-study heterogeneity (\(\tau^2\)) and within-study heterogeneity (\(\omega^2\)). This typically lowers \(\hat\tau\) in the CHE model (the tau-absorption effect). Standard errors may shift in either direction depending on the relative magnitudes of the variance components and the resulting changes in working weights.
Contrast 3: CE metafor vs bayesRVE CE. This is the frequentist–Bayesian calibration contrast for the CE model. Point estimates differ slightly because the Bayesian posterior mean incorporates prior information. CCC SD targets bayesRVE’s own CR2 sandwich variance (computed from Bayesian plug-in \(\hat\tau^2\)), which differs slightly from metafor’s CR2 SE (computed from REML \(\hat\tau^2\)). By Theorem 2, CCC achieves its target exactly; the observed ratio departs from 1.0 primarily because the two methods target different \(V_{\text{CR2}}\), with a secondary contribution from finite-\(S\) Monte Carlo error.
Contrast 4: CHE metafor vs bayesRVE CHE. The headline validation result. If CCC successfully extends Three-Layer Calibration to the CHE model, CCC SD should closely match CHE CR2 SE. The table confirms this: all six DV category ratios fall within the Gate 3 acceptance band of [0.9, 1.1].


16.1.2 Covariate Comparison
Covariate coefficients are outside the scope of Gate 3, which is defined exclusively for the six DV category intercepts (see Section 13.1). CCC/CR2 ratios for covariates may fall outside the [0.9, 1.1] acceptance band; such departures do not constitute a Gate 3 failure. Covariates represent a more demanding test for CCC because they have fewer effective clusters per coefficient, lower Satterthwaite df, and correspondingly larger \(R_k\) values.

Several patterns emerge from the covariate table. First, point estimates show greater inter-method variability than DV categories, reflecting the lower information content per coefficient. Second, \(R_k\) values for covariates are consistently further from 1.0 than for DV categories, confirming the theoretical prediction that CCC must work harder when the posterior is less well-identified. Third, Satterthwaite df for covariates is substantially lower than for DV categories, underscoring the importance of Layer 3 (the \(t\)-distribution correction) for these parameters. In particular, some covariate CCC/CR2 SE ratios exceed 1.5 (e.g., percoll_c under CHE), well outside the [0.9, 1.1] band. This is consistent with the theoretical expectation: Gate 3 was intentionally restricted to DV category coefficients precisely because covariates with low effective cluster counts are expected to show larger calibration discrepancies.

title: 15  CCC Mechanics and Limitations
text:
16.2 \(R_k\) as a Calibration Diagnostic
The ratio \(R_k = \text{SD}(\psi_k^*) / \text{SD}(\psi_k)\) measures the per-coefficient effect of CCC. It is a directly interpretable diagnostic: \(R_k < 1\) means CCC shrinks the posterior, \(R_k \approx 1\) means the posterior already matches the sandwich target, and \(R_k > 1\) means CCC expands the posterior to compensate for overconfidence.

16.2.1 R_k Dot Plot: All Parameters Under CE and CHE








Figure 16.1: R_k for all 11 parameters (6 DV categories, 5 covariates) under CE and CHE models. DV categories cluster near 1.0 (near-identity CCC), while covariates show expansion or compression depending on posterior-sandwich alignment. CHE R_k values are slightly more dispersed due to additional variance component estimation.







16.2.2 The R_k–df Inverse Relationship
A key structural property of CCC is the inverse relationship between \(R_k\) and Satterthwaite df. Parameters with high df (many effective clusters) show \(R_k \approx 1.0\) because the data dominate the prior, and the posterior variance already approximates the sandwich variance. Parameters with low df show \(R_k\) further from 1.0 because prior shrinkage creates a gap between the posterior and the sandwich target.








Figure 16.2: R_k versus Satterthwaite df for all 11 coefficients under CE and CHE models. The inverse relationship confirms the theoretical prediction: CCC compensates most where the posterior is least well-identified. A LOESS trend line highlights the systematic pattern.





This pattern holds across both CE and CHE models, though CHE points shift slightly leftward (lower df) because estimating \(\omega^2\) consumes additional effective degrees of freedom. The LOESS trend line captures the systematic decay from expansion (\(R_k > 1\)) at low df to near-identity (\(R_k \approx 1\)) at high df.


16.2.3 CCC Dumbbell Chart: Original vs Calibrated SD
The dumbbell chart provides a direct visual comparison of posterior SD before and after CCC, for all 11 CHE parameters.








Figure 16.3: CCC dumbbell chart for the CHE model: original posterior SD (left endpoint) vs CCC SD (right endpoint) for all 11 parameters. Rightward movement indicates expansion (R_k > 1); leftward movement indicates shrinkage (R_k < 1). DV categories show minimal change; covariates show larger adjustments.





The interpretation is immediate: CCC automatically adapts calibration intensity to each coefficient’s identification strength. Well-estimated DV categories experience minimal adjustment, while covariates with fewer effective clusters undergo substantial expansion or compression to align with the CR2 sandwich target.

title: 15  CCC Mechanics and Limitations
text:
16.3 Interval-Width Decomposition
Three-Layer Calibration produces confidence intervals through a sequence of adjustments. Decomposing the interval half-width reveals the contribution of each layer.
Layer 1 (Posterior mean): The point estimate \(\hat\psi_k\) centers the interval. CCC preserves this anchor exactly.
Layer 2 (CCC spread calibration): The posterior SD is transformed via \(R_k\), so CCC SD \(= R_k \times\) posterior SD. This aligns the spread with the CR2 sandwich.
Layer 3 (Satterthwaite \(t\)-distribution): The critical value shifts from \(z_{0.975} = 1.96\) to \(t_{0.975, \text{df}_k}\). When df is large, \(t \approx z\) and Layer 3 adds little. When df is small, the \(t\) inflation is substantial.

Figure 16.4: Three-Layer interval half-width decomposition for selected coefficients. Four bars per coefficient: raw Bayesian (z-based), CCC with z-quantile (Layer 2 only), CCC with t-quantile (full Three-Layer), and frequentist CR2 with t-quantile. CCC-t closely tracks CR2-t.

title: 15  CCC Mechanics and Limitations
text:
16.4 Correlation Structure of \(V_{\text{CR2}}\)
CCC operates as a multivariate transformation via the Cholesky factors \(L_2 L_1^{-1}\). To understand why a univariate rescaling would be insufficient, it is necessary to examine the off-diagonal structure of \(V_{\text{CR2}}\).

16.4.1 CE Model Correlation Matrix








Figure 16.5: Correlation matrix of V_CR2 for the CE DV model. DV categories are nearly uncorrelated (cell-means parameterization), while covariates show moderate cross-correlation with DV categories. The off-diagonal structure necessitates CCC’s multivariate Cholesky transformation.







16.4.2 CHE Model Correlation Matrix








Figure 16.6: Correlation matrix of V_CR2 for the CHE DV model. The pattern is similar to the CE model but with modestly different magnitudes due to the omega-augmented working weights. DV-DV correlations remain near zero; covariate cross-correlations persist.







16.4.3 Interpretation
The correlation matrices reveal two structural features. First, DV categories are nearly uncorrelated with each other (off-diagonal values near zero in the upper-left block). This reflects the cell-means parameterization: each DV category is estimated by a largely distinct set of studies. Second, covariates show moderate correlations with DV categories and with each other. These cross-correlations arise because covariates draw information from all studies, not from disjoint subsets.
The multivariate nature of CCC is essential for handling these off-diagonal elements. A naive coefficient-by-coefficient rescaling would achieve the correct marginal SDs (matching \(R_k\) on the diagonal) but would fail to capture the correlation structure embedded in \(V_{\text{CR2}}\). The Cholesky factor \(L_2 L_1^{-1}\) simultaneously rotates and rescales the posterior draws to match both the diagonal variances and the off-diagonal covariances.

Summary statistics for the control-type specification:

Mean CE CCC/CR2 ratio: 1.0964
Mean CHE CCC/CR2 ratio: 1.1025
CE Gate 3 status: CHECK
CHE Gate 3 status: CHECK



16.5.3 The Straw-Man Anomaly






WarningControl-Type Framing Caveat



The control-type specification produces Gate 3 exceedances under both models, not only CHE:

CHE Straw man: CCC/CR2 ratio = 1.253 (largest exceedance)
CE Straw man: CCC/CR2 ratio = 1.185
CE Treatment as usual: CCC/CR2 ratio = 1.138

All three exceed the [0.9, 1.1] acceptance band. This is not confined to the CHE model; it reflects a small-effective-\(J\) phenomenon for these moderator categories.
Why this happens: The Straw man category has the fewest contributing studies among the control types. With fewer effective clusters, the Bayesian posterior mean and the REML estimate of \(\tau^2\) (and \(\omega^2\)) can diverge more substantially due to stronger prior influence. This produces a larger gap between bayesRVE’s \(V_{\text{CR2}}\) (the CCC target, computed from Bayesian plug-ins) and metafor’s \(V_{\text{CR2}}\) (the benchmark, computed from REML). CCC achieves its own target exactly (Theorem 2), but the targets themselves differ more than in the high-\(J\) DV-category specification, pushing the ratio outside the acceptance band. Finite-\(S\) Monte Carlo error is a secondary contributor.
Manuscript implications:

Gate 3 claims should be framed around the DV-category specification, which is the primary analysis.
The control-type model is a supplementary robustness check, not a Gate 3 target.
Do not claim that all TSL15 coefficients stay within [0.9, 1.1] – this is true for the validated DV-category specification but not universally across all parameterizations.
This behavior is expected and should be acknowledged as a known boundary condition.





16.5.4 Control-Type R_k Comparison








Figure 16.7: R_k for control-type moderators under CE and CHE. The same gradient observed in the DV model appears: categorical moderators with many effective clusters show R_k near 1.0, while covariates and the Straw man category show larger deviations.

title: 15  CCC Mechanics and Limitations
section: 16.6 What the TSL15 Analysis Validates – and What It Does Not
text:
16.6 What the TSL15 Analysis Validates – and What It Does Not
An honest assessment of the evidentiary scope is essential for responsible claims in the manuscript. The TSL15 real-data analysis provides strong but bounded evidence.

16.6.1 Validated Claims
The following claims are directly supported by the TSL15 analysis:

CCC produces inference equivalent to RVE for the DV-category specification. All six CCC SD / CR2 SE ratios fall within [0.9, 1.1] for both CE and CHE, with mean ratios at or near 1.0.
Three-Layer Calibration extends from CE to CHE. The overlay pattern (omega-augmented Woodbury weights flowing through shared CR2 infrastructure) produces CHE calibration quality comparable to CE.
Satterthwaite df provides per-coefficient small-sample correction. The interval decomposition confirms that the \(t\)-distribution adjustment is essential for covariates with low effective cluster counts.
CCC preserves Bayesian point estimates while recalibrating uncertainty. Posterior means are invariant under the CCC transformation, which adjusts only the spread and correlation structure.
\(R_k\) is an interpretable diagnostic of calibration intensity. The systematic inverse relationship with df provides researchers a transparent summary of where and how CCC adjusts the posterior.
Variance component partitioning is correct. CE tau-absorption and CHE omega shrinkage behave as theoretically expected, with the prior-sensitive decomposition clearly acknowledged.



16.6.2 Claims NOT Validated
The following claims are not supported by the TSL15 analysis alone:

Generalization beyond a single dataset. TSL15 is a large, well-behaved meta-analytic dataset (\(J = 107\), \(N = 1{,}198\)). Generalization to smaller datasets, different domains, or atypical heterogeneity structures requires simulation evidence. The sim3 study (24 conditions, 11,600 fits) addresses this for both CE (16 conditions across scenarios S2–S4) and CHE (8 conditions in scenario S1, correctly specified, \(J = 10\)–\(60\)). The CHE evidence is narrower in scope—confined to correct specification—so broader CHE robustness (misspecification, prior sensitivity) remains a target for sim4.
Prior sensitivity on real data. The half-Cauchy(0, 0.5) prior on \(\tau\) and \(\omega\) is the default, and no alternative priors were tested on TSL15. The omega posterior shows noticeable shrinkage relative to REML, but the consequences for CCC calibration are inferred from theory, not directly measured.
Coverage of CCC-t intervals. Nominal 95% coverage cannot be directly assessed on real data because the true parameter values are unknown. The sim3 study provides coverage evidence for both CE (93.4–96.4% across 16 conditions) and CHE (94.0–96.2% across 8 conditions), but under HC0 corrections. The v0.6.0 CR2-based intervals have not yet been independently validated in simulation.
Universality across parameterizations. The Straw man anomaly demonstrates that not all moderator specifications produce ratios within [0.9, 1.1]. The DV-category model passes Gate 3 under both CE and CHE; the control-type model fails under both models for low-effective-\(J\) categories (Straw man in both, Treatment as usual in CE).
Multi-parameter joint inference. v0.6.0 provides only per-coefficient CCC-t intervals. Wald and F tests for joint hypotheses (e.g., omnibus test for DV category differences) are deferred to v0.7.0.
Within-study varying moderators on real data. The TSL15 analysis uses study-constant moderators. Although bayesRVE supports observation-level sandwich estimation (v0.3.0), this capability was not exercised in the real-data analysis.



16.6.3 Supported vs Unsupported Claims: Formal Summary




Table 16.7: Formal Assessment of Claims Supported by the TSL15 Real-Data Analysis. Status indicates whether the claim is supported (S), partially supported (P), or not supported (N) by the TSL15 evidence.












Claim
Status
Evidence
Limitation




CCC SD matches CR2 SE for DV-category specification

6/6 DV ratios in [0.9, 1.1], mean = 1.00
Single dataset (J=107); needs simulation replication


Three-Layer Calibration extends from CE to CHE

CHE ratios match CE quality; shared CR2 infrastructure
Single dataset; sim3 CHE supportive (8 conditions, S1 only) but narrower scope than CE

S

Exact coverage improvement not measured on real data

S
CCC formula anchors at posterior mean by construction

S

S
